notes: Motoi Copilot voice. Dry, tech-focused. Vocabulary + observation only.

messages.content: what's hallmark?
stamped mark indicating metal fineness, sometimes maker and assay office
what's touchmark?
maker's personal stamp, often initials or a small symbol
what's assay-office?
government body that tests and stamps metal purity (UK, Ireland, EU)
what's fineness-mark?
the numeric portion — 925, 750, 585 — indicating parts-per-thousand purity
what's date-letter?
British hallmark component indicating year of assay
what's sterling?
92.5% silver, 7.5% copper — the standard silver alloy
what's fine-silver?
99.9% silver, softer than sterling, less tarnish, stamped .999
what's argentium?
modern silver alloy with germanium, tarnishes less than sterling
what's vermeil?
sterling base with at least 10 microinches of gold plating
what's gold-filled?
mechanically bonded gold layer, at least 1/20 by weight, marked GF
what's gold-plated?
thin gold coating over base metal, marked GP or HGE
what's rolled-gold?
older term for gold-filled, common on antique pieces
what's karat?
what's carat?
gemstone weight — 1ct = 200mg (distinct from karat)
what's rose-gold?
gold alloyed with copper for a pink tone
what's white-gold?
gold alloyed with nickel or palladium, often rhodium-plated
what's green-gold?
gold alloyed with silver and cadmium for a pale-green tone
what's electrum?
natural or ancient alloy of gold and silver
what's shakudo?
Japanese copper-gold alloy patinated blue-black
what's shibuichi?
Japanese copper-silver alloy patinated silver-gray
what's mokume-gane?
Japanese laminated-metal wood-grain-pattern technique
what's niello?
black copper-silver-lead-sulfur inlay in engraved metal
what's repoussé?
reverse-side hammering to raise a pattern on the face
what's chasing?
front-side hammering to refine a repoussé pattern
what's granulation?
tiny fused metal balls (Etruscan revival signature)
what's filigree?
twisted wire soldered into openwork
what's anticlastic?
hammered so surface curves in two opposite directions (saddle shape)
what's synclastic?
hammered so both curvatures go the same way (bowl shape)
what's forged?
shaped by hammering rather than cast
what's lost-wax-cast?
cast from a wax model burned out of the mold
what's die-struck?
stamped between two hardened steel dies (mass-market end)
what's hand-fabricated?
every element built by hand from sheet or wire (high maker claim)
what's enamel?
glass fused to metal by kiln firing
what's plique-à-jour?
enamel with no backing, light passes through
what's cloisonné?
enamel bounded by soldered wire cells
what's champlevé?
enamel filling engraved recesses in the metal
what's guilloché?
engine-turned engraved pattern often under enamel
what's milgrain?
fine bead-textured edge (Edwardian/Art Deco signature)
what's prong-setting?
metal claws holding stone from above
what's bezel-setting?
metal collar wrapping the stone's girdle
what's channel-setting?
stones set in a groove between two parallel walls
what's pavé-setting?
small stones set close like a paved surface
what's tension-setting?
stone held only by metal tension across the girdle
what's halo-setting?
ring of smaller stones around a center stone
what's cabochon?
smooth-domed stone, no facets
what's faceted?
stone cut with flat surfaces for light return
what's brilliant-cut?
58-facet standard diamond cut
what's old-mine-cut?
square cushion pre-1900 hand-cut diamond, chunky facets
what's rose-cut?
flat-bottom faceted-dome stone, medieval-through-Victorian
what's step-cut?
rectangular parallel-facet cut (emerald, baguette)
what's matrix?
surrounding rock visible in turquoise or opal
what's doublet?
layered stone (opal slice on black backing)
what's triplet?
three-layer stone assembly
what's treated-stone?
heat, radiation, oil, or diffusion enhancement — ethical listings disclose
what's lab-grown?
chemically identical to natural, produced in lab (FTC disclosure required)
what's Mohs-hardness?
1-10 scratch scale — diamond 10, corundum 9, quartz 7 (daily-wear threshold)
what's refractive-index?
light-bending measure used to identify cut gems
what's specific-gravity?
density measure used to identify unmounted gems
what's spring-ring?
small round clasp with sliding-catch, low-end but reliable
what's lobster-clasp?
spring-loaded curved-jaw clasp, mid-tier standard
what's box-clasp?
tongue-in-slot clasp with safety catch, high-end bracelet standard
what's ring?
band worn on the finger — US sizes 3 to 13
what's band?
plain ring, usually a wedding-style piece
what's solitaire?
ring with one center stone, no side stones
what's cluster?
multiple stones grouped in one setting
what's eternity-band?
ring set with stones all the way around
what's half-eternity?
ring set with stones only across the top half
what's necklace?
worn around the neck
what's chain?
flexible linked portion of a necklace or bracelet
what's pendant?
object hanging from a chain
what's choker?
necklace 14-16 inches, sits at throat
what's princess-length?
necklace 18 inches, sits at collarbone
what's matinee-length?
necklace 22-24 inches, sits mid-chest
what's opera-length?
necklace 30-36 inches
what's rope-length?
necklace 36+ inches
what's cable-chain?
uniform oval links, all-purpose
what's curb-chain?
flattened interlocking links, lie-flat, everyday-wear
what's figaro-chain?
alternating short and long links, Italian-standard
what's rope-chain?
twisted-braided appearance
what's box-chain?
square link, minimal, contemporary
what's snake-chain?
smooth tube, no visible links, delicate
what's herringbone?
flat woven appearance, drapes but kinks easily
what's singapore-chain?
faceted twisted chain, sparkles under light
what's wheat-chain?
braided-appearance, four-strand construction
what's bracelet?
worn on wrist
what's cuff?
bracelet with open gap, slides over wrist
what's bangle?
rigid closed bracelet, slides over hand
what's tennis-bracelet?
flexible line of set stones, usually diamonds
what's charm-bracelet?
chain with decorative attached charms
what's earring?
worn on ear
what's stud?
single-stone or small earring on post
what's hoop?
circular earring
what's huggie?
small tight hoop that hugs the lobe
what's dangle?
earring that hangs below the earlobe
what's drop?
earring with single element hanging from stud
what's chandelier?
multi-tiered dangle earring
what's post?
the thin metal shaft that passes through the ear
what's butterfly-back?
friction earring back with two wings
what's screw-back?
threaded earring back, more secure
what's brooch?
pin worn on clothing, hinged pin-and-clasp
what's cameo?
carved relief portrait, usually in shell or stone
what's intaglio?
recessed carved image, opposite of cameo
what's signet-ring?
ring with flat top, historically for wax-sealing
what's cocktail-ring?
oversized statement ring, mid-20th-century onward
what's eternity?
shorthand for eternity-band
what's birthstone?
gemstone traditionally associated with a birth month
what's gemstone?
naturally-occurring or lab-grown mineral cut for ornament
what's diamond?
crystalline carbon, hardness 10, the reference stone
what's sapphire?
corundum, any color but red, hardness 9
what's ruby?
red corundum, hardness 9
what's emerald?
green beryl, hardness 7.5-8, typically included and oil-treated
what's pearl?
organic gem from mollusk, freshwater or saltwater
what's opal?
hydrated silica with play-of-color, hardness 5.5-6.5
what's turquoise?
hydrous copper-aluminum phosphate, hardness 5-6
what's amethyst?
purple quartz, hardness 7
what's aquamarine?
blue-green beryl, hardness 7.5-8
i see: shiny yellow mark 925
seller is claiming gold-plated silver (vermeil or gold-over-sterling)
i see: shiny yellow mark 14K with fading tips
solid gold with everyday wear pattern — not plated
i see: silver color no mark magnetic
steel or nickel alloy — silver is not magnetic
i see: silver color no mark non magnetic
could be sterling, could be aluminum, could be pot-metal — weigh in hand
i see: greenish wrist under band
copper leaching — piece is base metal or low-grade plated
i see: no mark heavy piece white metal
possible platinum (density) or possible pot-metal (cheap)
i see: 925 on a band with cabochon turquoise
Southwest US Native or Native-style piece — check for hallmark or signature
i see: cabochon with spiderweb matrix
high-grade turquoise — spiderweb matrix commands premium
what's opal-with-black-back?
possibly a doublet — opal slice glued to onyx, disclose
i see: emerald with visible inclusions
normal — jardin is expected; a flawless emerald is unusual
i see: diamond with black inclusion
included stone, SI or I grade — visible flaw reduces value
i see: stone that scratches with a nail
softer than 2.5 — likely glass or paste, not a durable gem
i see: stone that fogs instantly under breath
the fog test — real diamond disperses fog immediately, moissanite and CZ hold it
i see: cold heavy clear stone
either diamond or good CZ — cannot tell by feel alone, tester needed
i see: milky white pearl uniform perfect
likely imitation — real pearls have subtle surface variation
i see: pearl with rough feel on tooth
real (nacre grit) — imitation is smooth
i see: cast piece with visible mold line
production-cast — lower maker-tier than hand-fabricated
i see: solder joint visible inside ring
sized ring — check if sizing was done by a jeweler or roughly
i see: band with worn through plating
gold-plated, base metal now visible — value is low, disclose
i see: yellow metal with copper tone underneath
gold-filled worn through, or gold-plated over copper
i see: matte finish on white metal ring
brushed, sandblasted, or lightly acid-etched — style choice, not defect
i see: extreme shine on white metal ring
possibly freshly rhodium-plated white gold, or possibly polished silver
i see: tarnish in recesses of repousse piece
silver — normal patina, adds character, don't polish before appraisal
i see: no tarnish on silver color piece
possibly argentium, possibly stainless, possibly recently polished
i see: signet with engraved initials and crest
personal seal-ring — reduces value for resale unless historically notable
i see: signature inside band that you cannot place
unknown maker — photograph the mark, research before pricing
i see: signature inside band a jeweller recognizes immediately
known maker — multiplies value, verify not counterfeit
i see: cast in mark not stamped
mark is part of the mold, not applied after — often a fake or a repro
i see: sharp crisp stamp with clean edges
applied hallmark — usually genuine, still verify with loupe
i see: worn shallow stamp inside old band
consistent with genuine age — fake marks often too crisp for their apparent age
i see: chain that kinks when hung
herringbone — normal characteristic, disclose to buyer
i see: chain with tarnished links and shiny links alternating
mixed metals or partial re-plating — investigate
i see: clasp with safety latch
high-end construction — box clasp with figure-eight safety, or lobster with safety loop
i see: ring shank worn thin on underside
worn ring — will need re-shanking if the metal is worth saving
i see: prong tip worn flat on diamond ring
check that stone is not loose — flat prongs are re-tipping candidates
i see: bezel with a lifted edge
bezel-set stone at risk — needs a jeweler to re-crimp before wear
i see: large yellow stone in a yellow metal cluster
possibly citrine or possibly synthetic yellow sapphire — testing needed
i see: faceted stone with perfect symmetry
likely machine-cut or lab-grown — hand-cut has small asymmetries
i see: faceted stone with slight asymmetry
likely hand-cut, older piece — check for old-mine or old-European cut
i see: yellow metal ring marked HGE
heavy-gold-electroplate — plated, not solid, disclose
i see: yellow metal ring marked 1 20 14K GF
gold-filled — mechanically bonded, wears well, priced above plated
i see: silver color ring marked 950
platinum — very heavy, look for PT or PLAT nearby
i see: silver color ring marked 585
European mark for 14K gold that has been white-gold-alloyed or rhodium-plated
i see: yellow metal piece with no mark but heavy
possibly unmarked solid gold from a jurisdiction without mandatory hallmarks — test
i see: shell with carved female profile
cameo — check whether shell (translucent when held to light) or plastic (uniform)
A ring, yellow-toned, stamped '14K' on the inner shank.

Solid 14-karat gold (58.3% gold, alloy remainder). The mark '14K' is the US standard for 14-karat gold. Solid, not plated. Confirm by weight and by wear pattern — solid gold wears uniformly; plated wears through.
A ring, yellow-toned, stamped '14K GP' on the inner shank.
Gold-plated ring — thin gold coating over base metal. GP = gold-plated. The metal underneath is usually brass or nickel. Wear will show through in months to years of daily wear.
A ring, yellow-toned, stamped '1/20 14K GF'.
Gold-filled — mechanically bonded gold layer at 5% of the ring's weight. Gold-filled is thicker than plated and wears far longer. Marked as fraction (1/20 = 5%) plus karat plus GF.
A ring, silver-toned, stamped '925'.

Sterling silver — 92.5% silver, 7.5% copper. 925 is the international sterling standard, stamped as a numeric fineness mark. If the ring is also yellow-toned externally, it is likely vermeil or gold-plated sterling.
A ring, silver-toned, stamped '.999'.
Fine silver — 99.9% silver. Fine silver is softer than sterling and tarnishes less. Often seen in coin-silver reworks or high-purity casting.
A ring, silver-toned, no mark visible on inside or outside.

Unknown — could be silver, stainless, nickel alloy, or pot-metal. No mark is not evidence of no value — many older US pieces and modern craft pieces are unmarked. Weigh, test with magnet (silver is non-magnetic), and if still unknown use a silver-testing solution or XRF.
A ring, silver-toned, stamped 'PLAT'.

Platinum — one of the densest jewelry metals, unusually valuable. Platinum marks: PLAT, PT950, PT900, 950, 900. Very heavy for size. Cool to the touch. Does not tarnish. Ten times rarer in jewelry than gold.
A ring, yellow-toned, stamped '18K 750'.

Solid 18-karat gold (75% gold), marked both US and European conventions. 18K = 750 (per thousand). Dual-marking is common on pieces made for export or European-market resale. Softer than 14K, warmer yellow.
A ring, rose-toned, stamped '14K'.

Rose gold — 14K gold alloyed with a higher proportion of copper. Rose gold's pink comes from copper. The mark stays 14K because it is still 58.3% gold; only the alloy composition of the remainder changes.
A ring, silver-toned, stamped '585' with a small crown mark next to it.

14-karat white gold, European hallmark (crown indicates gold assay). 585 = 14K (58.5% per thousand — Europe rounds; US calls it 14K = 58.3%). White gold is nickel- or palladium-alloyed and often rhodium-plated for the bright white finish.
A necklace clasp with '925 ITALY' and a small oval maker mark.

Sterling silver, Italian-made, with a registered maker mark. Italian sterling is a major supply category. The maker mark is usually a registered stamp — worth photographing and looking up if you resell.
A bracelet stamped '10K' inside the clasp.

Solid 10-karat gold — the lowest karat legally called 'gold' in the US. 10K is 41.7% gold. Harder and more durable than higher karats. Common for daily-wear pieces and men's rings. Value calculation uses the 0.417 factor against gold spot price.
A pendant stamped '22K'.

Solid 22-karat gold — 91.7% gold, common in South Asian and Middle Eastern jewelry. 22K is very soft — hallmarks may look shallow because the metal deforms easily. Preferred in cultures where jewelry doubles as savings; often unmarked in modern Indian gold but stamped '22K' for export.
A ring stamped '24K' on the outside band.

Suspicious — 24K is very soft and rarely used for durable jewelry. Real 24K exists but is unusual as a ring. External stamping (rather than inside the band) is a fake-marker red flag. Test before believing.
A ring, yellow-toned, stamped 'HGE'.

Heavy Gold Electroplate — thick plating, but plating nonetheless. HGE indicates plating heavier than standard GP but still not solid or gold-filled. Older fashion-jewelry mark. Value is largely the design, not the metal.
A ring stamped 'STER' on the inner band.

Sterling silver — 'STER' is the abbreviated older US mark for sterling. US pieces from the mid-20th century often used 'STER' or 'STERLING' spelled out rather than '925'. Both refer to the same alloy.
A cuff stamped 'MEXICO 925' with a stylized artist mark.

Mexican sterling silver, artist-signed. Mexican silver has a substantial studio tradition — Taxco pieces from mid-century onward are collector-grade. Look up the artist mark; some marks multiply value.
A ring, yellow-toned, with no mark and a magnet sticks to it.

Not gold — likely gold-plated steel or gold-tone base metal. Gold is not magnetic. Neither is silver, copper, brass, or platinum. If a magnet sticks, the core is a magnetic metal — usually steel or nickel.
A ring, silver-toned, with no mark and a magnet does not stick.

Ambiguous — could be silver, aluminum, pot-metal, or nickel-based alloy. Further testing required. Non-magnetic rules out steel and iron but leaves many options. Weight, sound (silver rings musically when tapped), and chemical test resolve.
A pendant stamped '925' with the letter 'S' inside a shield next to the mark.

Sterling silver with a maker's shield touchmark — worth researching the shield symbol. Shield-enclosed maker marks are common European and British tradition. The exact shield shape and the letter identify the maker; databases and hallmark books exist for lookup.
A ring, silver-toned, with '925' and 'CZ' both stamped inside the band.

Sterling silver setting with cubic-zirconia stone. CZ stamped near the setting is the ethical disclosure that the clear stone is cubic zirconia, not diamond. Common on affordable engagement-style pieces.
A chain, yellow-toned, with '14K' stamped on the clasp.
Solid 14-karat gold chain. Chain marks are stamped on the clasp or on a small metal tag near the clasp. On very fine chains the mark may be tiny and require magnification.
A chain, yellow-toned, with '14K GF' stamped on the clasp.

Gold-filled chain — a durable middle tier between plated and solid. Gold-filled chains hold up to daily wear for years, unlike plated. Preferred by makers who want a solid-gold look at a lower price point.
A pearl necklace, uniform white pearls, tap on your teeth feels slippery.

Imitation — likely glass or plastic. Real pearls feel gritty on the tooth. The 'tooth test' — genuine pearl nacre has microscopic texture; a real pearl feels gritty. Imitation pearls feel smooth. Combine with visual: real pearls have tiny surface variations.
A pearl necklace, pearls slightly irregular, gritty on the tooth.

Real pearls — likely freshwater or cultured. Tooth-test grit plus surface irregularity is the classic real-pearl combination. Freshwater pearls are common, affordable, and often irregular. Saltwater pearls (akoya, south sea, tahitian) are usually rounder and more uniform but still gritty.
A bracelet with what look like diamonds all around, no stamp on the metal.

Unknown — could be genuine tennis bracelet, could be CZ or crystal on plated base. Test the metal first. A missing stamp is the loudest signal here. A genuine diamond tennis bracelet would ordinarily carry a metal mark (14K or 18K or PLAT). No mark means either an antique from before mandatory marking or a costume piece.
A pair of earrings stamped '925' on the posts.

Sterling silver posts — often the whole earring is sterling but sometimes only the posts. Sterling posts are hypoallergenic-adjacent (nickel-free unless argentium is not used) and reduce ear irritation. When only the post is sterling and the front is plated brass, some sellers still stamp only the post — read the listing carefully.
A pair of earrings with a small stamp reading '585' and a triangle.

14K gold, European hallmark (triangle indicates gold assay in some countries). European hallmarks combine numeric fineness with a symbol indicating metal type — triangle, crown, or head for gold; various for silver. Not every country uses the same symbol.
A ring band with '925' and a small stamp of a fish.

Sterling silver with a Danish or Scandinavian maker mark (fish symbols are used by several Nordic maker traditions). Symbol marks after the fineness often identify country or maker. Scandinavian silver has a strong studio tradition; some fish-marks are highly desirable.
A signet ring, gold-toned, engraved with initials, no fineness mark.

Personal signet — the engraved initials reduce resale unless the ring is historically or maker-notable. Test the metal. Signet rings without marks may be pre-1900 pieces (predating standard hallmarking laws in many jurisdictions), family heirlooms, or costume. Testing solves the metal question; provenance research determines whether initials add or subtract value.
A bracelet, yellow-toned, marked '1/10 12K GF'.

Gold-filled — 10% of weight is 12K gold, mechanically bonded. Higher-fraction gold-filled (1/10 vs 1/20) means proportionally more gold and better wear. 12K is a slightly lower karat than 14K but still substantial.
A ring stamped 'RGP'.

Rolled Gold Plate — older term for gold-filled, common on Victorian and Edwardian pieces. RGP predates the modern 'GF' marking convention. Frequently seen on antique watch chains and lockets. Wears well due to mechanical bond.
A necklace stamped 'VERMEIL' on the clasp.

Vermeil — sterling silver base with at least 10 microinches of gold plating. Vermeil is the US legal term for 'gold-over-sterling' with a minimum plating thickness. Wears better than gold-plated brass. Priced above plated, below gold-filled.
A necklace stamped 'RHODIUM PLATED' on the clasp.

The finish is rhodium plating — usually over sterling or over white gold. The underlying metal is not disclosed by this mark alone. Rhodium is a thin, bright, protective plating. Common over white gold to give the brilliant-white appearance. When the rhodium wears, the underlying metal (which may be slightly yellow-tinged white gold) shows.
A ring stamped '18K' outside on the band, in raised letters.

Suspicious — raised (cast-in) marks are less trustworthy than stamped (post-cast) marks. Genuine hallmarks are almost always struck AFTER casting or fabrication, leaving a slightly recessed impression. Raised marks that are part of the mold are common on costume pieces and counterfeits.
A silver-toned ring, no mark, band shows brass-yellow when scratched inside.

Silver-plated brass — the base metal is exposed by the scratch. The scratch test on the least-visible part of a piece is a common trade test. Silver over brass is the most common costume construction. Value is design-only.
A yellow-toned ring, no mark, band shows silver-white when scratched inside.

Gold-plated silver, or gold-plated white base metal — possibly vermeil if the silver is sterling. Silver-white under yellow plating narrows to two possibilities: gold-plated sterling (vermeil) or gold-plated nickel/steel. A magnet test resolves — magnet stick means nickel/steel base.
A ring stamped '925 THAI'.

Sterling silver, Thai-made. Thai silver is a major supply source — Karen hill-tribe silver and Bangkok manufacturing both stamp origin. Karen hill-tribe silver is often higher-fineness (95-99%) than sterling.
A ring, yellow-toned, with '10K' and a small maker's initial in an oval.

Solid 10K gold with a US maker's mark. US makers self-stamp — there is no legal US hallmark system. Common practice is a small oval or lozenge with initials or a logo, next to the karat mark. Some marks are worth researching.
A cuff, silver-toned, stamped 'ALPACA'.

German silver / nickel silver — not silver at all. A copper-nickel-zinc alloy. Alpaca (also 'alpacca') is the trade name in some regions for nickel silver. Contains no silver. Common in Mexican and South American craft jewelry. Costume-tier.
A ring, silver-toned, stamped 'SILVER' but no fineness number.

Suspicious — genuine silver almost always carries a fineness mark. 'SILVER' alone may be a costume-tier or generic stamp. The word 'SILVER' without a fineness number is not a legally-defined claim. Could be silver, could be silver-tone. Test to verify.
A ring, silver-toned, stamped 'COIN'.

Coin silver — usually 90% silver, 10% copper. Older US standard, pre-sterling. Coin silver was the US standard before sterling; pieces marked COIN are often mid-1800s. Slightly less pure than sterling. Collector-grade in some contexts.
A ring, yellow-toned, stamped '9K'.

Solid 9-karat gold — the British minimum-legal-gold standard (37.5% gold). 9K is legal to call 'gold' in the UK and Commonwealth but not in the US (US minimum is 10K). Common on British and Australian jewelry. Slightly less warm-yellow than higher karats due to more copper.
A brooch, yellow-toned, stamped '15K'.

Older gold standard — 15K was legal in Britain from 1854 to 1932. Antique piece. 15K marks predate 1932 in Britain; often paired with a Chester, Birmingham, or London assay mark and a date letter. Excellent age-marker.
A cuff, silver-toned, stamped 'FINE SILVER'.

Fine silver — 99.9% silver, softer than sterling, less tarnish. Word-form marks for high-purity silver are common on studio pieces. Fine silver deforms more easily than sterling — think about durability for daily wear.
A ring, yellow-toned, stamped '18K' and '750' with a small 'i' inside a hexagon.

18K gold, Italian hallmark. The hexagonal mark indicates Italy. Italy uses a hexagonal maker's mark containing a number (identifying the province and manufacturer) — a full lookup requires the province code. Adds provenance.
A pendant, silver-toned, stamped '830'.

Continental European silver — 83% pure. Common in Scandinavian, German, and Baltic pieces. 830 silver was the German standard until 1888, and continued in Scandinavia. Slightly softer than sterling, distinct provenance. Marker for older continental European work.
A ring, silver-toned, stamped '800'.

Silver at 80% purity — common in older German, Italian, and Middle Eastern pieces. 800 silver was widely used across continental Europe pre-1900. Not sterling by modern standards but legally silver in many jurisdictions. Look at style for approximate date.
A ring, yellow-toned, stamped '14K PLUMB' or '14KP'.

Plumb 14K — exactly 14K (58.3%), no legal tolerance below. In the US, standard 14K allows a small tolerance below 14K. 'Plumb' (P) certifies the metal is exactly 14K or higher. Signals precision maker.
A cuff, yellow-toned, stamped '14KT' (with T).

14-karat gold — the T stands for 'karat' in older US convention. Solid gold. Older US pieces often stamp 14KT or 18KT. Same meaning as modern 14K or 18K. Age-marker for pieces from mid-20th century and earlier.
A ring stamped 'PLAT 900 IRID 100'.

Platinum-iridium alloy — 90% platinum, 10% iridium. Traditional US platinum-jewelry alloy. Iridium hardens platinum for setting durability. This alloy was standard for US platinum jewelry from early 1900s through mid-century. Very durable.
A ring stamped '925 STERLING BALI'.

Sterling silver, Bali-style construction — Indonesian handmade tradition, granulation and wirework common. Bali silver is a distinct visual style — heavy granulation, twisted wire, tribal motifs. Handmade. Some makers signed, most unsigned. Look at the workmanship for value.
A necklace stamped '925 CHINA'.

Sterling silver, Chinese-made — often mass-produced, sometimes studio. Chinese sterling is a huge supply category with quality that varies from mass-market to high studio. The mark tells you sterling and Chinese origin; the style and workmanship tell you tier.
A ring, silver-toned, stamped 'TITANIUM'.

Titanium — not silver, not gold, but a modern hypoallergenic metal. Titanium jewelry is lightweight, hypoallergenic, and cannot be sized (does not solder like precious metals). Modern-only. Priced by design, not by metal weight.
A ring stamped 'TUNGSTEN' or 'TUNGSTEN CARBIDE'.

Tungsten carbide — extremely hard modern metal, cannot be sized or resoldered. Tungsten rings are popular for men's wedding bands due to durability. Cannot be cut off by ordinary means (requires specialized crackers). Priced by design, not by metal weight.
A ring stamped 'STAINLESS' or '316L'.

Stainless steel — usually surgical-grade (316L) for hypoallergenic wear. 316L is the medical-grade stainless standard. Common on body jewelry, sports jewelry, and inexpensive fashion pieces. Non-magnetic in typical alloys but some cheaper stainless is weakly magnetic.
A pendant stamped '.925' with a hyphen — '.925-'.

Sterling silver — the punctuation is common on some maker stamps and doesn't change the meaning. Some stamps include punctuation (.925 or .925-) as part of the die design. Meaning is unchanged: sterling silver. Focus on the number.
A ring, no mark, tap-tested rings musically for a long time.

Consistent with silver — silver rings musically when tapped. Not conclusive alone. The tap test: hold the ring loosely, tap gently against a hard surface. Silver rings clearly and holds the note. Base metals thud or ring briefly. Combine with weight, color, and chemical test.
A ring, yellow-toned, gives a slight green streak on a ceramic streak-plate.

Not gold — gold does not streak on ceramic. Likely brass or gold-plated brass. The streak test: gold is too dense and non-friable to leave a streak on unglazed ceramic. Brass leaves a slight yellow-green streak. Rough test — modern acid-testing is more definitive.
A ring, yellow-toned, marked '14K,' with a rich buttery shine and no reddish tone.

Solid 14K gold, most likely yellow gold (as opposed to rose or green). Color of solid gold shifts with alloy: rose (copper-rich), green (silver-rich), yellow (balanced). Rich buttery yellow is the typical US 14K appearance.
A ring, yellow-toned, marked '14K,' with a faint reddish tone.

14K rose gold or a copper-heavy alloy — still 58.3% gold, but alloy composition tilts pink. Rose tone from a 14K piece indicates the alloy metals are copper-heavier than balanced yellow. Same fineness, different visual class.
A ring, yellow-toned, marked '14K,' looking pale and almost greenish.

14K green gold — alloy is silver-heavy. Less common. Green-gold alloy uses silver and cadmium (or silver alone) instead of copper. Same 58.3% gold. Visually distinct — the pale green tone is subtle and easy to miss.
A ring, white-metal, stamped '14K,' with a slight yellow underhue.

White gold with rhodium plating that has worn — the underlying alloy is white-ish but not bright white. New white gold is often rhodium-plated for brilliant whiteness. As rhodium wears, the underlying alloy shows through, typically slightly warmer. Re-plating restores the bright finish.
A ring, white-metal, marked '14K,' brilliant bright white.

White gold, likely freshly rhodium-plated. Could also be palladium white gold (naturally whiter, less need for rhodium). Palladium white gold is a modern alternative to nickel white gold — brighter naturally, less rhodium dependence, hypoallergenic. Mark alone does not distinguish.
A ring stamped '14K' and 'C.T.'.

Solid 14K gold; the C.T. is likely a maker's initials or an old jewelry retailer signature. Initial-marks after karat are typically maker or retailer signatures. Some retailer marks (Tiffany, Cartier — do NOT invoke by name in operator listings) can multiply value. Research any unfamiliar initials.
A ring with a large clear stone that fogs briefly when breathed on.

Suspicious for diamond — genuine diamonds disperse fog almost instantly due to high thermal conductivity. The fog test: exhale on a stone. Diamond clears in under a second. Moissanite clears in 1-2 seconds. Cubic zirconia holds the fog for several seconds. Not definitive alone — a thermal tester is more reliable.
A ring with a large clear stone that shows rainbow flashes at every angle.

Likely moissanite or high-quality CZ — the color dispersion (fire) is higher than diamond's. Moissanite has 2.4× the dispersion of diamond, giving more visible rainbow-flash. Trained buyers can spot moissanite by the excess-of-fire signature. CZ also over-disperses.
A ring with a large clear stone with visible internal 'garden' inclusions.

Likely emerald (jardin is normal) or a natural-inclusion diamond. Rule out based on color. Emerald almost always shows jardin (natural inclusions) — a flawless emerald is very unusual. Included diamonds are lower-clarity grades (SI, I). The visible pattern helps identify.
A ring with a large clear stone that scratches under a steel-nail test.

Not diamond, corundum, or moissanite — likely glass, quartz, or paste. Anything above Mohs 5.5 will not scratch under a steel nail (steel is ~5.5). Diamond (10), corundum (9), topaz (8), quartz (7), and moissanite (9.25) are safe from a nail. Glass and softer stones scratch.
A ring with a green stone that shows subtle color-zoning under a loupe.

Consistent with natural emerald or natural tourmaline — synthetic stones usually lack the color-zoning. Natural stones grow slowly in inhomogeneous conditions; color-zoning (bands or patches of different intensity) is a natural signature. Lab-grown stones often lack it or have unnaturally uniform zoning.
A ring with a green stone marked 'EMERALD' on the certificate — center stone shows unnatural saturated uniformity.

Possibly lab-grown emerald (hydrothermal or flux). Certificate should disclose. Lab-grown emeralds are chemically identical to natural but often have distinctively uniform color and different inclusion patterns (spike-like, curved striae). FTC requires disclosure — check the paperwork.
A ring with a blue stone that shows silky sheen from within.

Consistent with natural sapphire — silk inclusions (fine rutile needles) are a natural signature. Silk in sapphire comes from tiny needle-like rutile inclusions. In heavily heat-treated sapphires the silk melts. Natural, un-heated, silked sapphires are rare and command a premium — always mention if you spot silk.
A ring with a red stone showing strong glow under UV / longwave black-light.

Consistent with natural ruby — most natural rubies fluoresce red under UV due to chromium content. The UV fluorescence test: natural rubies (especially Burmese) glow red under longwave UV. Some synthetic rubies also fluoresce; garnet does not. Combined with other tests, useful.
A ring with a black stone that has iridescent play-of-color.

Black opal — high-value variety of opal with dark body-color and rainbow play. Black opal is one of the most valuable opal types; the dark body enhances the visibility of the play-of-color. Check for doublet or triplet construction (opal slice on backing) which is common and should be disclosed.
A ring with a stone showing straight lines of play-of-color across the top only.

Likely opal doublet or triplet — the play is a thin slice on dark backing. Doublets have a thin opal top on an onyx or glass base. Triplets add a clear cap. Side-view under magnification shows the layer boundary. Priced significantly below solid opals; disclose.
A ring with a large purple stone showing color-change between daylight and incandescent light.

Likely alexandrite (chrysoberyl variety) or color-change garnet. Alexandrite is famously bluish-green in daylight, purplish-red in incandescent. Natural alexandrite is extremely valuable. Lab-grown and color-change garnet are more common. Certificate needed to distinguish.
A ring with a large brown stone that shows a cat's-eye of light across it.

Cat's-eye chrysoberyl or similar chatoyant stone (tigereye, cat's-eye tourmaline). Chatoyancy is a single band of reflected light that moves with the stone. Chrysoberyl cat's-eye is the most prized. Cabochon cut is required to display the effect.
A ring with a stone showing a six-rayed star of light when rotated.

Star sapphire, star ruby, or star rose-quartz — asterism from oriented rutile inclusions. Star stones require cabochon cut and specific inclusion orientation. Star sapphires and rubies are premium. Star rose-quartz is common and inexpensive. All show a six-rayed asterism.
A ring with a large opaque green stone with black veining.

Likely malachite or similar copper-mineral stone (chrysocolla, azurmalachite). Malachite is a copper-carbonate with characteristic banded green pattern. Soft (Mohs 3.5-4) so it wears; use in pendants and pins more than daily-wear rings. Very distinctive appearance.
A ring with a large opaque blue stone with dark matrix veins.

Likely turquoise, potentially high-grade if the matrix pattern is spiderweb. Turquoise ranges from cheap dyed magnesite to premium Sleeping Beauty (no matrix) or Number Eight (spiderweb matrix). Matrix pattern, color saturation, and country of origin drive value.
A necklace where the chain is smooth-tubed with no visible links.

Snake chain — smooth, articulated, delicate. Snake chain construction — many small linked segments giving the appearance of a continuous tube. Elegant, but prone to kinking if bent sharply. Common at multiple metal tiers.
A necklace where the chain is flat, woven-looking, and drapes like ribbon.
Herringbone chain. Herringbone has a flat woven appearance, drapes beautifully, and kinks easily if handled roughly. Common in gold and sterling. Disclose the kink tendency to buyers.
A necklace where the chain has alternating small oval and long oval links.
Figaro chain. Figaro chain has a repeating pattern of two or three short flat links followed by one long flat link. Italian tradition, very common at all price tiers, unisex.
A necklace where the chain looks like twisted rope.
Rope chain. Rope chain has a twisted appearance, sometimes hollow (lighter, cheaper) sometimes solid (heavier, better wear). Popular for pendants due to good sturdiness.
A necklace with square links, minimalist appearance.
Box chain. Box chain has square cross-section links, giving a clean modern appearance. Popular for men's pendants and minimal contemporary jewelry.
A necklace with round links all uniform size.
Cable chain — the all-purpose default construction. Cable chain is the most common construction. Uniform round oval links. Works at any price tier. Sold by weight and length.
A necklace with slightly flattened interlocking links.
Curb chain. Curb chain has interlocking links flattened for lie-flat wear. Common on men's chains, ID bracelets, and utility jewelry.
A necklace that looks like a chain of tiny woven baskets.
Wheat chain — braided-appearance four-strand construction. Wheat chain has four strands braided together into a rope-like but flatter appearance. Sturdy, holds pendants well.
A necklace with faceted, sparkling twisted links.
Singapore chain — faceted for maximum light-play. Singapore chain is a twisted chain with faceted links to catch light. Delicate, feminine, common in delicate gold and sterling.
A necklace with round links joined by a small bar between each two links.
Bar-and-link chain — a stylistic variant used in modern minimal design. Contemporary chain style. The bar-link element creates rhythm and visual interest. Priced by weight and design work.
A ring with four small metal claws holding the center stone.

Prong setting — the classic diamond-solitaire mount. Four-prong is the classic solitaire setting; six-prong (Tiffany-style — do not name-brand in listings) is another common variant. Prongs need periodic re-tipping as they wear.
A ring with a metal collar completely wrapping the girdle of the stone.

Bezel setting — most protective mount. Bezel setting wraps the stone's girdle in metal, protecting it. Best for active-wear pieces. Reduces light entry to the stone; may make the stone look darker than in a prong setting.
A ring with stones set in a groove between two rails of metal.
Channel setting. Channel setting has stones secured in a groove with metal walls on both sides. Popular for eternity and wedding bands. Very durable — stones cannot easily be knocked loose.
A ring surface covered with small stones set close together like a paved road.
Pavé setting. Pavé setting uses tiny prongs to hold small stones very close together. Rich appearance, high sparkle. Small stones can be lost if not periodically checked.
A ring where the center stone appears suspended between two walls of metal with no visible prongs.
Tension setting. Tension setting relies on metal spring pressure across the girdle. Extremely modern-looking. Requires precise workmanship and hard-metal alloys; not for soft metals.
A ring with a center stone encircled by a ring of small accent stones.

Halo setting. Halo setting places small accent stones around the center stone, making it appear larger and adding sparkle. Very popular for engagement rings 2010-onward. Center stones can range from small to substantial.
A ring with three stones — a large center and two smaller flanking stones.

Three-stone setting — often symbolic (past, present, future) in engagement contexts. Three-stone rings can be classic (three diamonds), sentimental (birthstones of family members), or engagement (past-present-future symbolism). Setting details determine value.
A ring with a large center stone and a shank set with small stones.
Solitaire-with-pavé-band or accent-shank ring. The center is a solitaire; the shank carries additional small stones. Increases sparkle and perceived weight of the piece. Common at all price tiers.
A ring with a diamond that shows a chunky, high-crown facet pattern with a small table.

Old-mine-cut diamond — hand-cut, pre-1900, cushion-shaped. Old-mine-cut diamonds are pre-1900 hand-cut stones with high crowns, small tables, and chunky facets. Prized by collectors for their warm, romantic light-return. Distinct from modern brilliant.
A ring with a diamond that shows a flat bottom and a faceted dome.

Rose-cut diamond — historic cut, popular Victorian-era and revived contemporary. Rose-cut diamonds have a flat bottom (no pavilion) and a faceted dome. Softer sparkle than brilliants. Historical (medieval-through-Victorian) and revived in contemporary artisan jewelry.
A ring with a rectangular diamond, parallel step-cut facets, chamfered corners.

Emerald-cut diamond — step-cut rectangular with cut corners. Emerald-cut diamonds emphasize clarity over sparkle (parallel facets act as clarity windows). Because inclusions show, only higher-clarity stones are commercially cut this way.
A ring with a square diamond, four-sided step-cut.

Asscher cut — square emerald-cut variant. Asscher cut is a square version of the emerald cut with slightly steeper steps. Vintage-modern look. Requires high-clarity stones for the same reasons emerald cut does.
A ring with a diamond having 58 facets in the classic pattern.

Round brilliant cut — the modern standard. The 58-facet round brilliant is the most common diamond cut, engineered for maximum light return. Standardized by Marcel Tolkowsky in 1919 and refined since.
A ring with a diamond in an elongated oval brilliant pattern.

Oval brilliant cut. Oval brilliant is a modified round brilliant stretched into an oval. Popular for solitaires as it appears larger than a round of equal carat. Watch for the 'bowtie' effect — a dark central band that indicates poor cut.
A ring with a diamond in a teardrop shape.
Pear-shaped brilliant cut. Pear shape (teardrop) is a modified round with one pointed end. Requires careful setting to protect the tip. Popular for pendants and drop earrings as well as rings.
A ring with a diamond in a marquise shape — pointed at both ends.
Marquise brilliant cut. Marquise cut is elongated with points at both ends. Named for the Marchioness of Pompadour. Points are the most vulnerable feature — often V-prong-protected.
A ring with a diamond in a heart shape.
Heart brilliant cut. Heart-cut is a modified pear or marquise cut with a cleft at one end. Requires excellent symmetry to be well-executed. Common in sentiment-jewelry.
A ring with a diamond in a rectangular cushion shape with brilliant faceting.

Cushion brilliant — modern version, sometimes called modified cushion. Modern cushion cut is a squared-off rectangle with rounded corners and brilliant-style faceting. Distinct from the older cushion (which was step-cut or old-mine). Very popular 2010-onward.
A ring with a diamond that looks like a rectangular emerald-cut but only 5-10 facets total, no crown, low profile.

Baguette cut — small rectangular step-cut, usually as accent stones. Baguettes are small rectangular step-cuts, almost never used as center stones. Common in Art Deco and modern eternity bands as accents.
A ring with a diamond that looks like a baguette with tapered ends.
Tapered baguette. Tapered baguettes narrow toward the ends. Often used flanking a solitaire in traditional three-stone rings.
A necklace pendant is a smooth-domed cabochon on a yellow-gold bezel.

Cabochon-set pendant — the stone is un-faceted, held by a metal collar. Cabochon cut is used for opaque stones, chatoyant stones, star stones, and for style choices with translucent stones. Bezel setting protects the cabochon.
A pendant with a stone showing a play of iridescent color across its dome.

Cabochon opal — likely white or crystal opal if the body is light, black opal if dark. Cabochon-cut opal shows play-of-color best. Body color, brightness of play, and pattern (pinfire, harlequin, flame) drive value. Check for doublet construction on the side.
A brooch is a carved shell portrait in relief, oval frame.

Shell cameo — most common cameo type. Shell cameos are carved from mollusk shells, typically bull-mouth or queen conch. Hold to light — real shell is translucent, plastic is uniform. Age and quality of carving drive value.
A brooch is a carved semi-precious stone portrait in relief, agate layered.

Hardstone cameo — often sardonyx or agate. Hardstone cameos use layered agate or sardonyx where the carver exploits the color layers for the portrait. More valuable than shell. Depth of relief and quality of layering matter.
A ring with an intaglio carving — the design recessed into the stone.

Intaglio ring — historical seal-ring convention or contemporary revival. Intaglio is the reverse of cameo: the design is cut INTO the stone rather than in relief. Historically used for wax-seals. Contemporary artisan revival popular in men's jewelry.
A brooch with a flat pin-back, hinged clasp, and safety catch — no rollover pin.

Modern brooch construction — post-1900. Modern brooches have short pins with rotating C-clasps and safety catches. Older brooches (pre-1900) have long pins extending well past the frame, with simpler C-clasps.
A brooch with a pin extending well past the frame edge, and a simple C-clasp with no safety catch.

Antique brooch — pre-1900 construction, pin extended to secure through heavy Victorian fabric. Long-pin brooches are pre-1900 because Victorian fabrics were heavy. The C-clasp without safety catch is period-correct. Handle with care — the pin can bend.
A brooch with a trombone-style clasp (sliding tube).
European brooch, common 1890-1930. Trombone clasps slide open like a trombone slide. European convention, common on Art Nouveau and early Art Deco brooches. Dates the piece to that window.
A brooch with a roll-over safety catch (a small metal ring that flips to secure the pin).
Modern brooch, post-1930 convention. Roll-over safety catches largely replaced simple C-clasps in the 20th century. Standard modern brooch construction.
A brooch with a stem-and-lever safety catch.

Mid-century brooch, roughly 1940-1970 convention. Stem-and-lever safety catches were popular mid-century. Less secure than modern flip catches but more secure than plain C-clasps. Dating hint but not definitive.
A pair of earrings with wires that pass through the ear and bend back.

French wire earrings — most common wire style, elegant, easy to lose. French wires (also called shepherd hooks) are U-shaped wires with a bent-back end. Elegant but not the most secure. Adding a rubber stopper reduces loss.
A pair of earrings with hinged wires that snap closed against a post.
Lever-back earrings — more secure than French wires. Lever-backs have a hinged wire that snaps closed. Much more secure than French wires. Preferred for heavier or more valuable earrings.
A pair of earrings with a very small round hoop and a hinged closure.
Huggie earrings. Huggies are tight small hoops with hinged closures. Sit close to the earlobe. Popular for everyday wear and for second/third piercings.
A pair of earrings with a screw-thread post and a threaded back nut.

Screw-back earrings — high security. Screw-back earrings have a threaded post and a matching nut that spins onto the thread. Very secure — used for valuable stones. Slower to put on than friction backs.
A ring in an art-nouveau style — flowing organic lines, plique-à-jour enamel.

Art Nouveau period (1890-1910) or contemporary revival. Art Nouveau jewelry features flowing organic lines, natural motifs (dragonflies, wisteria), enamel, opal, moonstone. Peak period 1890-1910. Modern revivals exist — hallmarks and construction help date.
A ring in Art Deco style — geometric, symmetric, contrasting stones.

Art Deco period (1920-1935) or contemporary revival. Art Deco jewelry is geometric, often features platinum or white gold, contrasting stones (onyx-and-diamond, sapphire-and-diamond), calibré-cut stones, milgrain edges. Peak 1920-1935.
A ring in Victorian style — dark stones (jet, onyx), mourning motifs, hair-work.

Victorian period (1837-1901) or contemporary mourning-revival. Victorian jewelry spans a huge range but includes mourning jewelry with jet, onyx, hair-work, snake motifs (Victoria wore one), and heavy gold pieces. Sub-eras (Early, Grand, Late) differ.
A ring in Edwardian style — filigree, milgrain, platinum-over-gold, pearls.

Edwardian period (1901-1915). Edwardian jewelry features fine filigree, milgrain edges, platinum-topped-gold construction (Edwardian pieces often have gold under-carriage with platinum crown), and pearls. Delicate and lace-like.
A ring in Retro style — bold, oversized, rose gold, big citrine or aquamarine.

Retro period (1935-1950). Retro jewelry is bold and sculptural, often rose or yellow gold in large forms, big citrine, aquamarine, or synthetic stones (materials were scarce during WWII). Machine-age feel.
A ring in Mid-Century Modern style — cluster settings, textured metal, colored stones.
Mid-Century Modern (1950-1970). Mid-century jewelry features cluster settings, textured brushed gold, colored stones, atomic and floral motifs. American and Scandinavian traditions both prominent.
A ring with heavy, minimalist geometric design in brushed silver.

Brutalist or Modernist studio jewelry — 1960s-1970s. Brutalist and modernist studio jewelry is heavy, minimalist, often brushed rather than polished, uses cast forms and simple geometric shapes. Studio makers signed. Look for maker mark.
A cuff bracelet with hammered surface and a single large cabochon turquoise.

Southwest US Native or Native-style — often Navajo or Zuni tradition. Southwest US Native jewelry commonly features hammered sterling and turquoise. Navajo tradition emphasizes silver work; Zuni emphasizes stonework and inlay. Look for artist signatures — Native makers often sign.
A ring with small stones set in a raised silverwork pattern with fine granulation.

Bali silver tradition or similar Indonesian studio work. Bali silver features granulation, twisted wire, and detailed stone-setting on sterling bases. Handmade, sometimes signed. Common motifs: floral, geometric, mystical.
A ring stamped 'K18' (K before number, not after).
18-karat gold, Japanese convention. Japanese jewelry marks put K before the karat number. Same meaning as 18K in US convention. Signals Japanese origin — Japanese craftsmanship often high-quality.
A ring stamped '333' and 'GERMANY'.

8-karat gold, German convention. Very low karat — sometimes not considered 'gold' in US jurisdiction. 333 = 8K (33.3% gold). Legal to sell as gold in Germany and much of Europe. Not US-legal as 'gold'. Older European standard, still common on antique pieces.
A ring stamped '375'.

9-karat gold — British minimum-legal (37.5%). 375 = 9K British standard. Common on UK, Australian, and NZ jewelry. Slightly less warm-yellow due to higher copper content. Legal as 'gold' in Commonwealth, not in US.
A ring stamped '417'.
10-karat gold — US minimum (41.7%). 417 = 10K US convention expressed per-thousand. Common on modern US pieces, especially those marketed internationally where numeric marks are preferred.
A ring stamped '833' — silver piece.
Silver at 83.3% purity — Dutch or European convention. 833 silver was a Dutch and European standard, common on 19th-century pieces. Below sterling but legally silver in Netherlands historically.
A ring stamped 'RUSSIA' and '875'.

Russian silver at 87.5% purity — Soviet-era convention. 875 was the Soviet-era Russian silver standard. Common on 20th-century Russian pieces. Slightly below sterling but legally silver in USSR/Russia.
A ring, silver-toned, stamped 'MEXICO' and 'TS-101' inside the band.

Mexican sterling with a Taxco maker code — TS marks identify Taxco makers by numeric code. Taxco silver marks include TA, TC, TE, TF, TG, TH, TI, TJ, TK, TL, TM, TN, TO, TP, TQ, TR, TS, TT, TU, TV, TW, TX, TY, TZ followed by numbers, uniquely identifying the maker. Some are collectible.
A ring with intricately-detailed silver work and a small stamp of a Fu-dog or dragon.

Chinese studio silver or export silver — the mythological creature marks the tradition. Chinese export silver marks vary widely — pseudo-hallmarks were common. Fu-dog, dragon, and phoenix marks appear on both genuine studio pieces and export-market pieces. Style, weight, and workmanship help distinguish.
A ring with heavy filigree and colored enamel work, marked '925' with a bee symbol.

Portuguese silver — bee is a common Portuguese assay-house mark. Portuguese silver hallmarks include the bee, boar's head, and other symbols. Portugal has strong filigree and enamel traditions. Sometimes very high-quality.
A ring with a black stone that has slight red glints under angled light.

Possibly garnet (dark red almandine can appear black in low light) or possibly black spinel. Dark almandine garnet often appears near-black but shows deep red under angled light. Black spinel is truly black and doesn't show red. Cut and setting also help — antique pieces often used almandine cabochons.
A ring with an orange-red stone showing exceptional brilliance and no visible inclusions.

Possibly spessartine garnet (orange), padparadscha sapphire, or lab-grown alternative. Rich orange-red gems include spessartine and mandarin garnet, padparadscha sapphire (extremely rare, valuable), imperial topaz, and modern lab-grown alternatives. Certification recommended for higher-value stones.
A ring with a stone that shows blue-and-red color-shift at different angles.

Possibly tanzanite (blue-purple pleochroism) or possibly iolite. Tanzanite is famously pleochroic — showing blue and purple from different crystal angles. Iolite (dichroite) does similarly. Cutter chooses which color faces up. Distinct signature of pleochroic stones.
A ring with a pink stone that shows lighter pink at edges and deeper pink in center.

Consistent with morganite (pink beryl) or pink sapphire — color zoning typical. Morganite is pink beryl, cut in cushion or oval, popular in rose-gold settings. Pink sapphires are harder (Mohs 9 vs 7.5-8 for morganite) and pricier. Color zoning is normal in both.
A ring with a stone showing pale yellow body and rainbow-fire flash from within.

Possibly heliodor (yellow beryl), yellow sapphire, or citrine — all in the pale-yellow range. Yellow gemstones include heliodor, yellow sapphire, citrine (yellow quartz), yellow diamond, and imperial topaz. Testing (specific gravity, refractive index) separates. Citrine is common, cheap; yellow diamond and yellow sapphire are premium.
A ring with a very pale purple stone that fades in strong sunlight over hours.

Possibly amethyst (light-sensitive) or synthetic sapphire — natural amethyst fades under prolonged sunlight. Amethyst can fade if exposed to direct sunlight for extended periods. Some heat-treated amethyst is particularly light-sensitive. Store in dark to preserve color. Synthetic sapphires generally do not fade.
A ring with a stone that shows a milky sheen — silver-blue over white.

Moonstone (adularescence) — the blue sheen floats over the stone body. Adularescence is moonstone's signature optical effect — a blue or white sheen that floats and moves with the light. Best moonstones show blue sheen; whites are common and cheaper. Cabochon cut always.
A ring with a stone that shows a metallic-golden sheen against dark body.

Labradorite (labradorescence) — golden or blue metallic sheen against gray body. Labradorite's labradorescence is a metallic sheen — usually blue, green, or gold — that flashes at specific angles. High-flash pieces are called spectrolite. Cabochon cut required.
A ring with a stone showing red-and-green flashes in a dark-body cabochon.

Fire opal or precious black opal — play-of-color with warm hues. Fire opal has a warm body color (orange or red) with or without play-of-color. When it also has play, called precious fire opal. Very desirable. Body color and play both drive value.
A ring with a stone showing green-body and needle-like red inclusions.

Possibly demantoid garnet — the 'horsetail' inclusions are diagnostic. Demantoid garnet is a rare green garnet variety. The 'horsetail' inclusions (branching fibrous crystals of chrysotile) are diagnostic and INCREASE value — a rare case where inclusions are prized. Russian demantoid is the collector's grade.
A ring with two silver-toned rings soldered together at a fixed angle.

Toi et moi ring — two-stone or two-band construction, French sentimental tradition. 'Toi et moi' (you and me) rings feature two stones or two bands representing a pair. French sentimental tradition, revived in modern engagement designs. Distinct from three-stone rings.
A ring with three interlocking bands that can be pushed together to form one ring.

Puzzle ring or Turkish wedding ring — traditional four-band interlocking construction (or three-band variant). Puzzle rings are 3-band, 4-band, or more, interlocking such that removing one and reassembling requires knowing the pattern. Historical Middle-Eastern tradition (used to test bride's cleverness). Contemporary revival popular.
A ring with a stone that shifts from green to red under different lighting.

Alexandrite (or lab-grown alexandrite / color-change garnet). Natural alexandrite shifts from green (daylight) to red (incandescent). Lab-grown alexandrite exhibits the same shift and is a fraction of the price. Certificate required to confirm natural.
A ring with a stone that shows two colors depending on crystal orientation.

Pleochroic stone — tanzanite, iolite, tourmaline, or andalusite candidates. Pleochroism is a natural property of anisotropic crystals — different colors from different crystal directions. Cutters choose orientation for the most desirable face-up color. Testing distinguishes species.
A ring with a pearl that shifts iridescent colors across its surface.

Peacock pearl or high-quality Tahitian pearl. Peacock and Tahitian pearls show iridescent overtones — green, blue, purple — over their body color. Round Tahitian pearls with high overtone are premium; drop and baroque shapes are also collector-grade.
A necklace of large uniform white pearls, each slightly asymmetric, warm luster.

Likely South Sea pearls — largest cultured pearls, warm creamy luster. South Sea pearls (from the Pinctada maxima oyster) are the largest cultured pearls, typically 10-15mm. Warm creamy white to golden. High value. Slight asymmetry is normal even in high grades.
A necklace of tiny very-round white pearls, high mirror-luster.

Akoya pearls — the classic small round cultured white pearl. Akoya pearls (from Pinctada fucata) are the classic pearl-strand pearl. Small (6-9mm typical), very round, mirror-luster. Japanese and Chinese production dominates.
A ring with a pearl set on a metal cup with a peg glued into the drilled hole.

Pearl-on-post construction — standard for pearl rings, watch for glue integrity. Pearl rings typically use a metal cup with a peg glued through a drilled pearl. Glue can weaken over time — check for looseness. Some pearls are only half-drilled (glued) or fully drilled (threaded through).
An estate-sale box contains a jumble of forty rings. You have six minutes before the next buyer arrives.

Sort in two passes: first, magnet-test everything to eliminate steel and nickel plated; second, group the survivors by mark — 925/STER together, K-marks together, unmarked heavy pieces together, unmarked light pieces to the side. Take the K-marks and any heavy unmarked pieces for further evaluation. Estate-sale triage is exactly what a Kellman glance trains you for. Two-pass sorting compresses 40 items to a manageable subset in the time available. Magnet-plus-mark is the fastest coarse sort.
A ring stamped '18K' has a green tinge showing under the yellow-gold surface, visible only in a magnified inspection of the underside.

Solid 18K green-gold alloy, not plating. The green shows through because the alloy IS greenish — silver-and-cadmium alloyed with 75% gold. Green gold is genuine solid gold with a silver-rich alloy composition. Novices often mistake the greenish undertone for plating wear. The mark is the source of truth: 18K stamped means solid.
A necklace clasp with '925' is attached to a chain that shows the base metal at every wear point.

Sterling-silver clasp on a silver-plated base-metal chain — a common bait-and-switch construction. Sellers sometimes attach a genuine sterling clasp (which carries the required legal mark) to a plated chain to avoid marking-law issues. The mark applies only to the clasp; the chain itself is not silver. Buyer should be told.
A ring with a large center diamond has small holes drilled through the pavilion facets.

Laser-drilled diamond — likely inclusions were laser-drilled to remove or reduce dark inclusions. Laser drilling is a common diamond enhancement — a fine channel is drilled to an inclusion to allow acid to bleach it. Enhancement must be disclosed. The drill channels are visible under a loupe as very fine straight lines. Value is reduced vs an equivalent-clarity untreated stone.
A ring with an emerald has visible oiling residue at surface-reaching inclusions.

Oil-treated emerald — nearly all emeralds are oil-treated to fill surface-reaching fractures. Disclosure required. Oiling is the most common emerald treatment — cedar oil, canada balsam, or resin fills surface fractures to improve apparent clarity. Cleaning ruins the treatment. Untreated emeralds command a premium. Type of oil (traditional, modern resin) affects durability.
A brooch stamped '750' has a small mark showing a woman's head in profile.

French 18K gold. The Marianne's head is the French assay-office mark for imported gold; the eagle's head is the French mark for domestic 18K. French hallmarks are complex — the head symbols distinguish domestic/imported and karat. Eagle head = French 18K domestic; Marianne head = imported 18K; horse head = imported 14K. Detailed reference books cover full systems.
A ring with an aquamarine has slightly greenish tone. Under filtered light the color deepens to blue.

Untreated aquamarine — natural color often has green tone; heat treatment removes the green to give pure blue. Most commercial aquamarine is heat-treated to remove greenish tones and produce pure blue. Untreated stones with visible green tone are increasingly valued by collectors who prefer natural over treated.
A ring with a tanzanite has intense blue with no violet showing face-up.

Heat-treated tanzanite — natural tanzanite is more brown-purple; heat treatment (about 500°C) shifts it to the desired violet-blue. Nearly all commercial tanzanite is heat-treated — the color shift is dramatic and permanent. Untreated tanzanite is rare and collector-graded. Standard practice, disclosure required but often minimal.
A ring with a large golden sapphire, no visible inclusions, glowing color.

Possibly beryllium-diffusion-treated sapphire — a modern treatment that alters color to yellow/orange/pink. Beryllium diffusion is a modern treatment that dramatically alters sapphire color from pale or off-color to intense yellow, orange, or pink (padparadscha imitation). Legally must be disclosed. Value reduced vs untreated. Certification distinguishes.
A ring with a large diamond that shows blue fluorescence under UV lamp.

Fluorescent diamond — 25-35% of diamonds show blue fluorescence under longwave UV. Slight discount in market pricing but not a defect. Blue fluorescence can slightly mask yellow tint in a diamond (masking a Z-M range down to look H-J) but strong fluorescence in colorless stones causes 'milky' appearance in sunlight. Market discount is 5-15% for strong fluorescence in top color-grade stones.
A ring with an old-mine-cut diamond has soft warm sparkle vs the crisp bright sparkle of a modern brilliant.

Old-mine-cut sparkle character — hand-cut for candlelight, not electric light. The 'romantic' sparkle. Old-cuts were designed before electric lighting; they scatter and warm the light rather than throwing bright pinpoints. Collectors value the romantic character. Vintage market prices old-cuts by carat plus 'antique premium.'
A ring with an emerald mounted in yellow gold, and the piece has 'MADE IN COLOMBIA' engraved inside the band.

Colombian emerald origin claim — Muzo, Chivor, or Coscuez mines historically produce the finest emeralds. Origin markers on emerald pieces add value if genuine — Colombian emeralds command premium over Zambian or Brazilian. Origin cannot be conclusively determined without laboratory analysis, so 'MADE IN COLOMBIA' engravings are seller claims, not verifications.
A ring with a pearl that shows tiny concentric circular patterns on the surface under magnification.

Consistent with natural saltwater pearl (Akoya, South Sea) — the concentric patterns are nacre growth rings. Nacre grows in concentric layers; the growth pattern is visible under magnification. Imitation pearls have painted or coated surfaces with no such pattern. Combined with tooth-test grit, definitively real.
A ring in Georgian style with a foil-backed rose-cut diamond in a closed-back setting.

Georgian jewelry (1714-1837) — foil-backing was standard to enhance stone color; closed-back settings are period-correct. Georgian jewelry rarely comes to market. Foil-backing (metal foil behind the stone to reflect light and add color) is period-correct but limits modern cleaning. Closed backs (no light entry from below) are period-standard. Verify age with construction and hallmark research.
A ring in Art Deco style with calibré-cut sapphires — small custom-shaped stones fitted to the setting.

Art Deco period (1920-1935) — calibré-cutting was a signature of the period. Calibré-cut stones are cut to fit a specific setting — trapezoidal, half-moon, custom shapes. Labor-intensive. Signature of Art Deco period. Modern jewelry rarely uses calibré. High-quality Deco pieces command premium.
A ring stamped 'PLAT' with a broken prong on a solitaire — the metal is torn, not bent.

Genuine platinum — platinum tears rather than bends when severely stressed, because of its work-hardening behavior. Platinum's mechanical behavior — it work-hardens and tears rather than bending easily — is a subtle indicator of authenticity. White-gold prongs bend; platinum prongs tear or break cleanly. Not a primary test but a corroborating observation.
A ring with an emerald has 'no oil' certificate from GIA.

Untreated emerald with laboratory certification — premium value, rare in commercial market. GIA and other major labs certify treatment status. 'No oil' or 'no treatment' certifications on emerald are rare and command significant premium. Verify certificate number against issuing lab's database.
A ring in Retro style with a large aquamarine set in rose gold, alongside small ruby accents.

Retro period (1935-1950) — bold aquamarine plus rose-gold-plus-ruby was a signature combination during WWII material scarcity. Retro-period jewelry often paired big colored stones (aquamarine, citrine) with rose gold and small red accents (ruby, garnet, or synthetic). Materials were scarce; makers designed for visual impact with less precious content.
A ring with three fine gold bands soldered together and stamped '15K' with a British hallmark.

Victorian trinity ring — the three-band construction symbolizes Father-Son-Holy Ghost or past-present-future, popular Victorian sentimental jewelry. Trinity or Russian wedding rings are three interlocking bands. Some Victorian pieces are soldered together (fixed); some are loose (interlocking puzzle). Historical value depends on maker, hallmarks, and condition.
A ring with a large stone that a magnet moves visibly when brought close.

Suspicious — most gemstones are not magnetic. Suggests iron-rich material (hematite, magnetic garnet variant, or synthetic ferrite). A few gemstones have measurable magnetism — hematite is famously magnetic, and some garnet varieties (almandine) have detectable magnetism. Strong magnetism suggests hematite, magnetic synthetic, or ferrous inclusion. Not diamond, corundum, beryl, or quartz.
A ring shank shows two solder joints of different colors — one yellow, one silver-white.

Ring has been sized more than once, and the sizing was done with mismatched solder alloys or with different metals bridging the shank. Sizing solder should ideally match the ring metal, but sometimes silver solder is used on gold (mismatch shows). Multiple sizings visible = ring has been resized multiple times, which weakens the shank over time. Consider replacing the shank.
A brooch with a Trombino clasp shows a small nick in the plating exposing white base metal.

Silver-plated or nickel-silver brooch, not sterling. Age likely European 1890-1930 based on clasp style. Clasp style dates the piece; wear pattern reveals the metal. Silver-plated or nickel-silver European brooches are common in this window. Value is design and age, not metal weight.
A pair of earrings shaped as diagonally-set stones with unusual multi-color enamel and rose-cut center stones.

Art Nouveau earrings — Iberian or French tradition, likely 1895-1905 window. Art Nouveau earrings are relatively rare compared to brooches and pendants of the period. Enamel plus rose-cut center stones plus diagonal setting are period signatures. Iberian and French makers are most common; check for maker mark.
A ring with a plain gold band and a very light-weight feel for its size.

Hollow gold band — a construction that reduces gold content while maintaining external appearance. Hollow-gold construction is common for larger bands to keep weight (and cost) down. Weight in hand is the tell. Some hollow-gold pieces are labeled 'hollow'; many are not. Value calculated by actual gold content, not apparent size.
A necklace with a natural pearl (not cultured) — surface shows highly-detailed nacre patterning under magnification.

Natural pearl — extremely rare in modern market. Value can be very high. Natural pearls (formed without human intervention) are extremely rare. The overwhelming majority of pearls in the market are cultured. Distinguishing natural from cultured requires X-ray analysis (natural pearls have concentric growth from a small center; cultured pearls have a large central bead nucleus). Send to lab for confirmation.
A ring with a large yellow stone certified as 'natural yellow sapphire, no heat treatment.'

Untreated natural yellow sapphire — premium classification for corundum. Untreated corundum (sapphire or ruby) commands significant premium over heat-treated equivalents. Yellow sapphire is a beauty for daily wear (Mohs 9). Reputable lab certifications include GIA, AGL, Gübelin, SSEF.
A ring with a large red stone certified as 'natural Burmese ruby, no heat treatment.'

Untreated Burmese ruby — the highest tier of ruby classification. Burmese (Myanmar) rubies are the historical standard for premium rubies — the pigeon-blood-red color is the reference. Untreated Burmese rubies with lab certification command extraordinary prices. Verify certificate against lab database.
A ring with a large blue stone certified as 'natural Kashmir sapphire, no heat treatment.'

Untreated Kashmir sapphire — historical premier origin, cornflower-blue with velvety texture. Kashmir sapphires (mined roughly 1880-1930) are considered the finest sapphires ever produced. Very rare in modern market. Untreated Kashmir with certification is museum-tier — six-figure per carat is normal. Verify aggressively.
A ring with a stone certified as 'natural padparadscha sapphire.'

Padparadscha — the rare pink-orange corundum variety, priced above almost all other sapphires. Padparadscha is the pink-orange to orange-pink color range of sapphire. Genuine natural stones are among the most valuable corundum. Beryllium-diffusion-treated stones can mimic. Reputable lab certification distinguishes.
A ring with a stone certified as 'natural Paraiba tourmaline.'

Paraiba tourmaline — the copper-bearing tourmaline from Brazil (or Mozambique/Nigeria analog), electric neon-blue-green, extremely valuable. Paraiba tourmaline was discovered in Brazil in the 1980s; similar copper-bearing tourmalines have since been found in Mozambique and Nigeria (marketed as Paraiba-type or by origin). Brazilian Paraiba is the premium. Electric neon color is the signature.
A ring with a natural pearl comes with a lab report describing 'button-shape, saltwater, 8.5mm, no treatment.'

Detailed pearl lab report — supports value claim; verify authenticity of report and lab reputation. Reputable pearl labs (GIA, SSEF, GIT) issue detailed reports including shape, environment (saltwater/freshwater), size, and treatment status. Report should be verified against issuing lab. Natural saltwater pearls are extraordinarily rare and valuable.
A ring with a diamond has a laser-inscription on the girdle matching a GIA report number.

GIA-inscribed diamond — the girdle-inscribed number can be verified against GIA's database to confirm authenticity of the report and match to stone. GIA inscribes report numbers on the girdle of graded diamonds using microscopic laser lettering. Cross-verify by looking up the number on GIA's public database — the report should show the same 4Cs the paper report claims. Fraud risk is inscription mismatch (paper and stone don't match).
A ring with a diamond has an inscription on the girdle that doesn't match any reputable lab format.

Suspicious — could be a fantasy inscription, could be a fictitious lab. Do not accept without verification. Non-standard girdle inscriptions may be personal (initials, dates) or may be from unreliable 'certification' companies (some grade-inflated, some fictional). Always cross-check against issuing lab. If lab doesn't have a database, be very skeptical of the report.
A ring with a large clear stone and CGL or IGI report.

Non-GIA/AGL/Gübelin/SSEF report — read the report skeptically. IGI is more lenient historically; CGL varies. GIA is the market benchmark. Not all labs are equally strict. GIA is the market benchmark for diamonds; AGL and Gübelin for colored stones. IGI is widely used commercially but historically more lenient on grading. Adjust price expectations accordingly.
A ring with a large brown-tinted diamond graded 'K color, VS1 clarity, ideal cut' by GIA.

K-color is faint yellow-tint (still in the 'near colorless' commercial range). Ideal cut with high clarity — solid mid-tier diamond. Priced significantly below D-J color grades. GIA color scale: D-F (colorless, premium), G-J (near colorless, standard), K-M (faint yellow, discounted), N-Z (light yellow to yellow, budget or fancy-color range). K-color at VS1 clarity in ideal cut is a value-oriented purchase.
A ring with a diamond graded 'D color, IF clarity, ideal cut' by GIA.

Top-tier commercial diamond — colorless, internally flawless, ideal cut. Premium price. D-IF-ideal is the peak of commercial diamond grading — the top tier a market-available stone reaches without going into 'investment-diamond' or 'museum' territory. Prices are set aggressively. Verify report against GIA database.
A ring with a diamond that shows a small chip on the girdle.

Chipped diamond — usually reduces value 20-40% for a girdle chip, depending on size. Some chips can be re-polished if the setting allows. Girdle chips are the most common diamond damage. Small chips can sometimes be polished out (re-cutting reduces carat weight). Setting sometimes hides small chips. Full disclosure required if selling.
A ring with a diamond has a feather-shaped inclusion reaching from center to girdle.

Reaching feather — potential durability concern. If it reaches the girdle, an impact could propagate the feather. Feathers are internal fractures. Reaching-feather-to-girdle is a durability concern — stone can potentially split under impact. GIA lab reports note reaching feathers explicitly. Insurance should cover the stone.
A ring with a lab-grown diamond graded 'F color, VS2 clarity' with a small IGI inscription.

Lab-grown diamond — chemically identical to natural, priced at about 20-40% of equivalent natural. FTC requires 'lab-grown' or equivalent term in commercial listing. Lab-grown diamonds are increasingly common. IGI grades most; GIA also grades. Priced at 20-40% of natural equivalent, and pricing has been decreasing as production scales. Consumer preference varies. FTC-compliant disclosure required.
A ring with a moissanite stone marked 'FOREVER ONE.'

Charles & Colvard's high-end moissanite — colorless equivalent to natural diamond. Not diamond, distinct thermal signature. Moissanite (silicon carbide) is a diamond simulant with distinct properties: doubly refractive (facet doubling visible under magnification), high dispersion (extra fire), and specific thermal signature. Moissanite-testers exist; general diamond-testers can be fooled.
A ring with a large pale stone marked 'GOSHENITE.'

Goshenite — colorless beryl (same species as emerald and aquamarine, but colorless). Goshenite is the colorless variety of beryl. Not a common commercial stone. Hardness 7.5-8, decent for jewelry. Named for Goshen, Massachusetts where notable specimens were found.
A ring with a peach-orange stone in a rose-gold setting.

Likely morganite (pink beryl) or peach sapphire — modern wedding-market favorites. Morganite in rose gold has been a popular wedding-ring trend since roughly 2015. Peach sapphire is a harder alternative (Mohs 9 vs 7.5-8). Both work well in rose gold aesthetically. Testing distinguishes.
A ring with a large clear-ish stone and no report. When tested with a diamond-tester, it reads 'diamond.'

Consistent with diamond OR moissanite (both trigger positive on standard thermal diamond-testers). Use a moissanite-tester to distinguish. Standard diamond-testers measure thermal conductivity; both diamond and moissanite are high-conductivity. Moissanite-testers use electrical conductivity to distinguish. Modern combo-testers do both. Faceted doubling under magnification is a moissanite indicator.
A ring with a large clear stone that shows visible doubling of the facet lines under a loupe.

Moissanite — doubly refractive, so back facets appear doubled when viewed through the crown. Doubling of facet lines under loupe is a fast moissanite indicator (diamonds are singly refractive; no doubling). Look through crown angled toward pavilion facets. Fast and definitive.
A ring with a large clear stone that shows a rainbow at the girdle.
