Consistent with CZ (cubic zirconia) — CZ shows strong dispersion at the girdle. Diamond does too but with different character. Girdle-rainbow is not definitive alone — diamonds show dispersion. But strong obvious girdle-fire on a large stone is a CZ indicator. Combine with weight (CZ is heavier for size than diamond) and thermal test.
A ring with a large blue stone that shows a slight color-change from blue (daylight) to purple (incandescent).

Color-change sapphire — the corundum equivalent of alexandrite. Rare and valuable. Color-change sapphires show alexandrite-like shifts (usually blue-to-purple or green-to-red). Rare, and command premium over standard sapphire. Certification recommended to authenticate.
A pair of earrings with old-mine-cut diamonds, closed-back settings, hand-fabricated silver-topped-gold construction.

Georgian or early Victorian (1780-1850) — silver-topped-gold (silver on top to hold the stone, gold underneath the ear-back) plus closed-back plus old-mine-cut = period cluster. Silver-topped-gold construction (silver crown for the diamond, gold shank for the ear-back to prevent tarnish rubbing off on skin) is a period signature. Old-mine-cut plus closed-back plus this construction is a strong Georgian/early-Victorian dating.
A brooch with pave-set diamonds around a central natural pearl, edwardian construction.

Edwardian brooch (1901-1915) — pave diamonds around natural pearl was a signature period design. Edwardian jewelry commonly paired natural pearls with tiny pave diamonds in delicate platinum-topped-gold construction. Natural pearl content adds significantly to value if verifiable.
A ring with a large centre stone flanked by two small emerald-cut stones in a Deco geometric mounting.

Art Deco three-stone ring — centre plus flanking stones in geometric setting is a period-signature engagement design. Deco three-stone rings often featured a central round or emerald-cut diamond flanked by baguette or trapezoid-cut sapphires or emeralds. Platinum construction was standard. Signed pieces (major houses) command premiums.
A necklace with layered graduated pearls, the biggest in the centre, tapering to smaller at the clasp.

Graduated pearl strand — classical mid-century pearl-necklace construction. Graduated strands (pearls decreasing in size toward the clasp) were the mid-century standard. Uniform strands became dominant later. Grading, matching, and drilling quality all affect value.
A ring shows evidence of extensive polishing — the milgrain edges are softened, some engraving is nearly worn away.

Over-polished antique piece — value reduced. Milgrain is now damaged; original detail lost. Polishing an antique piece removes metal — milgrain edges and engraving detail cannot be restored without replating and re-engraving (which would further alter authenticity). Value is diminished. Advise buyers to avoid polishing antique pieces.
A ring, sold as antique, shows machine-perfect stone cutting and no wear on high points.

Suspicious — antique claim conflicts with machine-cut stones and no-wear condition. Likely modern reproduction or vintage-style piece. Machine-perfect cutting is post-1900. No-wear on high points on a claimed antique is a red flag. Combined, likely a modern reproduction or a vintage-style piece being misrepresented. Test hallmarks against period conventions.
A brooch with signed 'JAR' in tiny script, small pave-set colored stones in unusual color combinations.

Possibly signed Joel Arthur Rosenthal (JAR) — extremely valuable if authentic. Verify with expertise. Signed contemporary designer jewelry (JAR, other major names — do not name-brand in operator listings without verification) can command extraordinary premiums. Signatures are counterfeited on both antique and contemporary pieces. Provenance documentation matters.
A ring with a diamond-shaped like a marquise, in a Cartier-style pattern (do not name-brand in listing) — signed 'CARTIER' inside the shank.

Claimed Cartier — very valuable if authentic. Signature is straightforward to counterfeit, so verification is essential. Also check for the reference number typically inscribed near the signature. Major luxury house signatures multiply value. All are frequently counterfeited. Authentic pieces typically carry a reference number in addition to the signature. Send to auction-house specialist or luxury-authentication service before final pricing or sale.
A ring with a natural intense-yellow diamond graded 'Fancy Vivid Yellow' by GIA.

Fancy-vivid yellow diamond — a distinct classification. Priced by color-strength and lab report against 'fancy color diamond' scale (not the D-Z scale). Fancy-color diamonds are graded on a separate scale (Faint, Very Light, Light, Fancy Light, Fancy, Fancy Intense, Fancy Vivid, Fancy Deep, Fancy Dark). Vivid is one of the top tiers. Yellow is common; other colors (pink, blue, green) are rarer and pricier.
A ring with a pink diamond graded 'Fancy Pink' by GIA — small stone.

Fancy pink diamond — even at small size, extremely valuable. Pink diamonds are among the rarest fancy colors. Pink diamonds are rare (major supply from Argyle mine, now closed). Any GIA-certified Fancy-Pink is significant. Verify certification aggressively. Auction-house consultation recommended for pricing.
A ring with a natural blue diamond graded 'Fancy Intense Blue' by GIA.

Fancy Intense Blue diamond — extraordinarily rare and valuable. Boron-doped color, largely from Cullinan mine historically. Blue diamonds are rare (Cullinan mine, South Africa, is the primary source). Fancy Intense Blue is a very high grade. Six- to eight-figure per carat for larger stones. Museum-tier verification required.
A ring with a chameleon diamond that changes color when heated or kept in dark.

Chameleon diamond — natural color-change diamond, usually green-to-yellow. Rare classification. Chameleon diamonds change color reversibly with heat or prolonged darkness. Rare classification, certified separately by major labs. Distinct from color-change sapphire and alexandrite (which change with lighting).
A ring in a modernist Scandinavian style, silver with a large baroque pearl centred, signed 'Georg Jensen.'

Danish modernist studio jewelry — Georg Jensen tradition (do not brand-name-drop in operator listings unless verified). Look up the design reference number typically on the piece. Danish studio jewelry from major maisons (do not name-drop in operator-facing listings) has consistent signatures and design-reference numbers stamped on the piece. Verify against reference books. Values vary widely by design and era.
A ring stamped 'SANDU' inside the shank.

SANDU (Sandhurst Design Unit) or another studio maker mark — research the mark for identity and provenance. Unknown maker marks require research — hallmark reference books, online databases, auction-house records. Some studio marks have significant value premiums attached; others are unknown or worthless.
A ring with a fully-cut natural blue sapphire, unheated cert, with a slight golden 'gooseberry' inclusion visible under magnification.

Consistent with Sri Lankan (Ceylon) origin — gooseberry inclusions are a Ceylon signature. Certain inclusion types are diagnostic of origin. Gooseberry inclusions (small rounded golden-appearing zircon inclusions surrounded by tension halos) are characteristic of Ceylon sapphires. Silk (fine rutile needles) also common in unheated Ceylons. Reputable labs assess origin.
A ring with a natural Padparadscha-color sapphire, unheated cert. Includes silk and rutile needles.

Consistent with Sri Lankan or Madagascan Padparadscha — silk is a natural-untreated indicator. Silk in an unheated stone confirms natural origin (heat destroys silk). Padparadscha from Sri Lanka historically dominates the market; Madagascar has become a significant source since 2000s. Origin affects pricing.
A necklace with pearls that show clear surface blemishes (small pits, tiny bumps) but nice orient.

Real pearls with normal surface variation — grades below AAA. Orient (surface iridescence) can still be excellent even with surface blemishes. Pearl grading is multi-factor: shape, size, luster, surface, matching, color. AAA/AA/A grades are marketing terms; each vendor may define differently. Nice orient with visible surface blemishes = mid-grade attractive real pearls.
A pearl necklace with pearls showing metallic silver, gold, and pistachio-green colors — largest 12mm.

Golden or multicolor South Sea pearls — natural color, not dyed. Premium tier. Golden and multicolor South Sea pearls (from the golden-lipped variant of Pinctada maxima) are highly valued. Verify color is natural (not dyed) by checking drill holes — dyed pearls show color concentration at drill points. Reputable dealer or lab report resolves.
A necklace with pearls sold as 'natural' but graded 'cultured' by SSEF X-ray analysis.

Cultured pearl misrepresented as natural — significant value discrepancy, seller must correct. X-ray radiography distinguishes natural from cultured pearls by the presence of a central bead nucleus (cultured) vs concentric organic growth (natural). SSEF and other pearl labs perform this test. Misrepresentation is a legal issue in commercial sales.
A ring with pearl showing signs of gluing but no visible metallic peg — post has come out at some point.

Pearl-ring where the pearl-post has separated; the pearl has been re-glued back to the mount. Structural weakness likely. Pearls glued to metal posts eventually fail. A re-glued pearl is a repair candidate — either replace the post-and-glue with fresh materials, or replace the pearl with a new drilled specimen. Not a permanent state.
A ring with a green Paraiba-type tourmaline that shows different tone at different angles.

Pleochroism in tourmaline — natural property, often exploited by cutter for best face-up color. Tourmaline is strongly pleochroic. Cutter orients the stone for the most desirable face-up color. Paraiba tourmaline's electric blue-green is at its best when oriented to maximize color; angled views may show green shifts.
A ring with a natural-color pearl of unusual peacock hue certified as 'natural color, saltwater, Tahitian.'

Natural-color Tahitian — significant premium over dyed. Peacock overtone with body-color natural is top tier. Tahitian pearls in natural body colors (dark grey through black with overtones of green, blue, purple) are premium. Some Tahitians are dyed to enhance color; certification confirms natural color. Peacock overtone is especially valued.
A cuff sold as antique Georg Jensen (do not brand-name in operator listing without verification) with a design number matching a documented 1930s reference.

If verified, valuable Georg Jensen studio piece. Verify signature and design number against reference books; consult auction records. Studio-signed pieces with matching design references are premium. Fakes exist but are usually caught by comparison against reference books. Auction-house consultation recommended for high-value verification.
A ring with a diamond that shows different fluorescence in different parts under UV — spotty rather than uniform.

Zoned fluorescence — natural feature. Common in some source-region diamonds. Note in listing if strong. Spotty or zoned fluorescence is a natural feature indicating growth-zone variation. Not a defect, but note if it affects the stone's visual appearance in sunlight (strong fluorescence can cause 'milky' appearance).
A ring with a colored stone graded 'natural, no treatment' by a lesser-known lab you don't recognize.

Skeptical — 'no treatment' is a valuable claim that requires reputable lab certification. Verify the lab's reputation before accepting. Certain 'labs' exist to inflate grades and treatment status for a fee. Reputable colored-stone labs: GIA, AGL, Gübelin, SSEF, GRS (for corundum). Others: verify before accepting the certificate at face value. Adjust price to lab strictness.
A ring with a large brown-tinted diamond graded 'brown, VS2, ideal' by GIA.

Fancy brown diamond ('champagne,' 'cognac,' or 'chocolate' in marketing terms). Priced by color tone plus clarity and cut. Brown diamonds are the most common fancy color. Marketing terms (champagne, cognac, chocolate) correspond to lightness/darkness of brown. Priced significantly below colorless equivalents, but distinctive.
A brooch with pave diamonds and central pearl, signed 'Cartier' with Paris hallmark, in a Cartier fitted box.

Original Cartier piece with fitted box — presentation adds provenance value. Verify signature, hallmark, box, and any accompanying documentation. Original presentation (box, papers, receipts) adds significant value for luxury-house pieces. Faked boxes exist but are usually detectable to specialists. Auction-house consultation recommended for major-house pieces.
A ring with a stone that shows a 'silk' appearance internally — very fine rutile needles distributed uniformly.

Un-heated corundum (sapphire or ruby) — silk is destroyed by heat, so its presence indicates natural, un-treated stone. Silk (fine rutile needle inclusions in corundum) is destroyed by the standard heat-treatment process. Its presence in a corundum stone is strong evidence of no heat treatment. Combined with reputable lab cert, confirms premium classification.
A necklace with 'natural' pearls sold at $500. X-ray analysis at a lab confirms they are natural saltwater pearls.

Massively underpriced — natural saltwater pearls even in small strand can be worth thousands to tens of thousands, depending on size and matching. Consider re-pricing before sale. Natural saltwater pearls (formed without human intervention) are extraordinarily rare in modern market. A verified strand of natural pearls can command dramatic prices. The lab confirmation dramatically changes the pricing model — consider consulting a pearl specialist before selling.
A ring with a large clear stone marked 'diamond' on tag, no report, in an estate lot.

Test before pricing. In estate lots, unmarked or informally-marked 'diamonds' can be anything from real to CZ to glass. Do not price as diamond without at least a diamond-tester positive plus visual inspection. Estate lots frequently mix genuine diamonds, moissanites, CZs, and glass simulants. Tag descriptions are often inherited and inaccurate. Test everything. Even a positive diamond-tester requires cut/clarity assessment for accurate pricing.
A cuff with heavy hammered silver and a large cabochon of malachite showing extensive banding.

Handmade artisan sterling cuff with malachite. Priced by workmanship and stone quality. Southwest US or Bali-style depending on details. Handmade cuffs with soft stones (malachite Mohs 3.5-4) are decorative rather than daily-wear. Value driven by workmanship quality, stone size and banding, and maker signature if any. Photograph and research any hallmark.
A ring with a large stone that has slight bubble-like inclusions clustered in one area.

Suspicious for glass or paste — natural stones rarely have gas-bubble inclusions. Genuine stone inclusions are usually mineral (crystals, needles, feathers, clouds). Gas-bubble inclusions are a strong indicator of glass, paste, or synthetic imitation. Natural gemstone inclusions are almost always mineral (other crystals, fluid inclusions, growth structures). Under high magnification, bubbles are diagnostic.
A ring with a small stone showing curved striae (growth lines) rather than straight, in a claimed 'natural sapphire.'

Curved striae are diagnostic of flame-fusion synthetic corundum (Verneuil process). NOT natural sapphire. Verneuil-process (flame-fusion) synthetics have curved growth striae because they crystallize on a rotating boule. Natural corundum has straight or hexagonal growth structures. Curved striae under magnification is definitive for synthetic.
A ring with silver-work showing evidence of skilled granulation, filigree, and enamel — sold as 'antique Etruscan revival.'

Consistent with 19th-century Etruscan revival tradition (Castellani, Giuliano, contemporaries). Verify with hallmark research; genuine pieces are significant collector items. Etruscan revival jewelry (1860-1900) drew on ancient Etruscan techniques — granulation, filigree, and archaeological motifs. Major makers signed. Fakes exist. Genuine pieces from top makers are museum-grade. Consultation with a specialist recommended.
what's MDM?
mobile device management — enterprise lock preventing re-provisioning
what's activation-lock?
account-locked device that cannot be re-activated without owner credentials
what's iCloud-lock?
the specific term for account activation lock on Apple devices
what's FMI?
'Find My iPhone' — enables activation lock, must be off before resale
what's IMEI?
international mobile equipment identity — 15-digit phone identifier
what's MEID?
mobile equipment identity — CDMA-specific device identifier
what's ESN?
electronic serial number — older CDMA identifier
what's carrier-lock?
phone constrained to one mobile network — unlock command reverses
what's network-unlock?
device firmware modification allowing any carrier's SIM
what's jailbroken?
iOS device with security restrictions removed — usually reduces resale
what's rooted?
Android device with elevated system privileges — usually reduces resale
what's bootloader?
small program that starts the operating system — unlocking affects warranty
what's OEM?
original equipment manufacturer — matched-source parts
what's aftermarket?
third-party parts, not from original manufacturer
what's refurbished?
used device restored to working order, often with warranty
what's certified-preowned?
manufacturer- or platform-certified refurbished, usually with warranty
what's as-is?
sold with no warranty and no return acceptance for defects
what's for-parts?
sold as non-working, buyer expects to salvage components
what's battery-health?
percentage of original battery capacity remaining — Apple exposes this natively
what's cycle-count?
number of complete charge/discharge cycles — proxy for battery age
what's wear-level?
SSD wear indicator — percentage of write endurance consumed
what's SMART-data?
hard-drive self-monitoring and reporting — indicates drive health
what's warranty-status?
remaining manufacturer warranty — verifiable by serial-number lookup
what's AppleCare?
Apple extended warranty program — verifiable by serial
what's GSX-report?
Apple internal service report — sometimes shared by refurbishers
what's GSX-lock?
internal service-flag marking device as ineligible for out-of-warranty repair
what's burn-in?
OLED/LCD persistent-image defect from long-term static display
what's dead-pixel?
single non-functioning pixel on a display
what's stuck-pixel?
single pixel stuck on one color — sometimes recoverable, sometimes permanent
what's backlight-bleed?
uneven backlight showing at edges of LCD panel
what's ghost-touch?
touchscreen registering phantom inputs
what's vertical-lines?
screen defect — usually connector or panel failure
what's T2-chip?
Apple security co-processor 2018-2020, affects repairability
what's M-series-chip?
Apple Silicon (M1/M2/M3/M4), integrated CPU+GPU+neural engine
what's iGPU?
integrated graphics — shares system RAM
what's dGPU?
discrete graphics — separate video memory (VRAM)
what's VRAM?
video memory — dedicated GPU memory (GDDR5/6/6X, HBM)
what's TB4?
Thunderbolt 4 — 40 Gbps USB-C variant, PCIe and DisplayPort tunneling
what's USB-C-3.2?
USB-C at USB 3.2 speeds — not necessarily Thunderbolt
what's USB-PD?
USB Power Delivery — negotiated charging up to 240W
what's MagSafe?
Apple magnetic charging connector — MacBook (2006-2016 original + 2021-onward revival)
what's Lightning?
Apple pre-USB-C proprietary connector (iPhone/iPad up to 2023)
what's IP-rating?
ingress-protection — IP67/68 = dust and water resistance ratings
what's eSIM?
embedded SIM — digital-only cellular provisioning
what's dual-SIM?
device supporting two SIMs (physical or physical+eSIM)
what's SoC?
system-on-chip — CPU, GPU, memory controller, etc. on one die
what's NPU?
neural processing unit — dedicated AI-acceleration hardware
what's TPM?
trusted platform module — hardware crypto for Windows 11 compatibility
what's secure-enclave?
Apple hardware crypto — biometric and key storage isolation
what's UEFI?
modern PC firmware replacing BIOS — Secure Boot capable
what's eSATA?
external SATA — legacy external-drive interface, mostly deprecated
what's NVMe?
modern PCIe-attached SSD protocol — much faster than SATA SSD
what's PCIe-generation?
PCIe 3/4/5 — successive doublings of per-lane bandwidth
what's RAM-generation?
DDR4/DDR5 — not backward compatible physically
what's monitor-Hz?
refresh rate — 60/120/144/240Hz + variable-refresh
what's laptop?
portable computer with integrated keyboard and screen
what's notebook?
synonym for laptop, often smaller-formfactor
what's desktop?
non-portable computer, usually with separate monitor
what's all-in-one?
desktop with monitor integrated (iMac, some Windows)
what's workstation?
high-end desktop for professional workloads
what's tablet?
flat touchscreen computer, often detachable-keyboard variants
what's smartphone?
pocket-sized computer with cellular capability
what's smartwatch?
wrist-worn computer with health/notification features
what's e-reader?
e-ink display device optimized for reading
what's wireless-earbuds?
in-ear Bluetooth audio, usually with charging case
what's headphones?
over-ear or on-ear audio, wired or wireless
what's monitor?
standalone display for computer output
what's keyboard?
input device — mechanical, membrane, chiclet, low-profile
what's mouse?
pointing device — optical, laser, trackball
what's trackpad?
integrated or standalone touch-input surface
what's webcam?
camera for video calls — standalone or built-in
what's microphone?
audio input — USB, XLR, condenser, dynamic
what's printer?
hardcopy output — inkjet, laser, thermal
what's router?
network device connecting devices to Internet — WiFi 6/6E/7 tiers
what's modem?
device translating provider signal to Ethernet
what's NAS?
network-attached storage — file server for home/office
what's external-drive?
USB or Thunderbolt storage — HDD or SSD inside
what's SSD?
solid-state drive — no moving parts, faster than HDD
what's HDD?
hard disk drive — spinning platter, higher capacity per dollar
what's RAM?
working memory — measured in GB, tiered by generation
what's CPU?
central processing unit — the main processor
what's GPU?
graphics processing unit — dedicated video/AI processor
what's power-adapter?
wall brick converting AC to device DC — wattage-rated
what's charger?
casual synonym for power adapter
what's cable?
connecting wire — USB, HDMI, Thunderbolt, DisplayPort
what's dongle?
small adapter converting one connector type to another
what's dock?
expansion device adding ports to a laptop
what's case?
protective enclosure for phone/tablet/laptop
what's screen-protector?
thin film or glass over device screen
what's stylus?
pen input device — Apple Pencil, S-Pen, active or passive
what's console?
gaming device — home or handheld, with proprietary OS
what's controller?
gaming input device
what's VR-headset?
head-mounted display for virtual reality
what's AR-glasses?
wearable augmented-reality display device
what's camera-body?
digital camera without lens — DSLR or mirrorless
what's lens?
detachable camera optics — prime or zoom, mount-specific
what's camera-flash?
external flash unit — TTL or manual, mount-specific
what's gimbal?
stabilization device for camera or phone
what's drone?
unmanned aerial vehicle — recreational or professional
what's TV?
consumer television — LCD, OLED, QLED, mini-LED variants
what's soundbar?
compact multi-speaker home-audio bar
what's speaker?
wired or wireless audio speaker
what's receiver?
AV receiver — home-theater audio hub
what's vintage-audio?
1970-1990 stereo separates — turntable, receiver, cassette, CD
what's turntable?
vinyl-record player
i see: listing title says macbook photos show 2015 hinge design
pre-USB-C MacBook, likely retina or Air — verify by exact port photo
i see: listing title says macbook photos show magsafe 3 and notch
2021+ Apple Silicon MacBook Pro with notch, high value
i see: phone listing with no serial and no imei
seller-omitted identifiers — cannot verify status, high risk
i see: phone listing with imei and blur in photo
seller hid IMEI — ask for a clear photo before buying
i see: phone listing with visible imei in photo
you can look up carrier status, activation lock, MDM before buying
i see: iphone back glass with hairline crack
repair candidate — Apple charges hundreds for glass; whole-back replacement
i see: iphone lightning port with visible lint and corrosion
compressed-air first; if charging fails after, port replacement needed
i see: usb c port that doesn t hold cable tightly
port wear or debris — clean first, replacement if worn spring-contacts
i see: macbook with butterfly keyboard year 2016 2019
keyboard mechanism is fragile — check every key before buying
i see: macbook with scissor magic keyboard year 2020 onward
reliable keyboard mechanism, better wear over time
i see: screen photo shows vertical line across panel
connector or panel failure — screen replacement needed
i see: screen photo shows yellow band along edge
backlight-bleed — usually cosmetic, sometimes indicates panel damage
i see: screen photo shows persistent image of app icons
OLED burn-in — permanent, reduces device value
i see: keyboard photo shows shine on EASDF and space
developer-use pattern — heavy code entry, low overall hours
i see: keyboard photo shows shine on WASD and space
gaming-use pattern — many hours, check thermal history
i see: keyboard photo shows shine on vowels and common consonants
office-use pattern — writing-heavy, low gaming stress
i see: trackpad shows a single worn shiny spot
single-hand-use pattern — right-handed shine at bottom-right
i see: hinge photo shows visible gap when laptop closed
hinge fatigue — internal cable strain, avoid or price down
i see: corners of laptop show dents
dropped device — check for internal damage; internal cables often loosen
i see: case shows scuffs and scratches consistent with use
normal wear pattern — cosmetic-only, price accordingly
i see: power adapter frayed at cable boot
replacement adapter needed — safety hazard as-is
i see: usb c cable with visible plastic strain relief crack
cable near failure — replace before it disconnects
i see: device turns on goes to setup then locks with warning
activation lock or MDM lock — cannot use, low salvage value
i see: device boots to a corporate logo
enterprise-provisioned device — usually still MDM-locked
i see: iphone shows cannot verify parts warning in settings
device has been repaired with non-genuine parts — reduces value
i see: iphone battery health below 80
Apple's replacement threshold — battery replacement needed for full function
i see: phone shows water damage indicator red
device has experienced water exposure — voided warranty, hidden damage possible
i see: laptop runs hot immediately on simple task
thermal issue — dried thermal paste, dust in fans, or failing fan
i see: laptop fan noise changes tone during use
failing fan bearing — replace before failure
i see: device photo shows stickers covering back and sides
cosmetic hiding — remove and re-photo before buying; check for damage under stickers
i see: device photo shows freshly cleaned shiny case
prep-for-sale detailing — legitimate; sometimes hides recent repair
i see: listing photos are stock images not actual device
cannot assess condition — ask for actual-device photos before buying
i see: listing description copies manufacturer spec sheet
lazy seller or reseller — ask specific condition questions
i see: listing title says original box but photo shows generic box
listing inconsistency — ask before assuming, box adds resale value
i see: listing says tested working but photos show off device
ask for boot photo or video before buying
i see: listing says factory reset shows setup screen in photo
verified reset state — good sign, next-user-friendly
i see: listing says factory reset but photo shows home screen
not reset — either seller has not done it, or does not know how
i see: listing price far below market for model
either seller does not know model value, has hidden defect, or listing is scam
i see: listing price far above market for model
seller expects rare condition or original packaging — verify claims
i see: device boot shows checkerboard artifact
GPU failure — common on older Mac laptops with dGPU; expensive repair
i see: device shows restart loop at boot
OS corruption or hardware failure — attempt recovery reinstall
i see: device camera lens shows dust under glass
sensor-level dust — cleaning requires disassembly
i see: device battery swelling visible through case
immediate replacement required — safety and expansion damages internals
i see: laptop power button requires multiple presses
button wear or power-management issue — investigate
i see: device shows region restriction warning on language change
region-locked device — check what functions are restricted
A phone listed as 'like new' shows a chipped corner in the third photo.

Condition mismatch — chipped means 'good' or 'fair,' not 'like new.' Condition grades are meaningful — 'like new' means no visible cosmetic damage. Any chip drops to 'good.' Photos always beat description; trust what you see.
A laptop's power adapter in the photo is frayed at the cable boot.

Adapter needs replacement — safety hazard. Factor cost into offer. Frayed adapters are a fire hazard and imminent-failure risk. Genuine replacement adapters cost $50-100. Third-party adapters are cheaper but riskier.
A phone listing says '256GB' but the settings-screen photo shows '128GB.'

Listing wrong — storage is 128GB per the device. Verify before buying. The device is authoritative. Sellers can mislabel — check the About screen photo for actual storage. Sometimes it is a mistake, sometimes it is deception.
A tablet is listed with a photo showing 'Setup Assistant' on screen.

Factory-reset — ready for next user. Good sign. Seeing the initial setup screen (Language / Region choice) means device has been erased. Next user gets a fresh start. Preferred state for buying used devices.
A phone photo shows a home screen with apps installed.

Not reset — seller has not erased. Verify erase before purchase. Home screen with apps means device is still logged into an account. Buyer must have seller sign out and erase, or receive credentials to do so. Never accept a not-erased device.
A laptop listing shows 'Model A2141' in a photo of the bottom case.

16-inch MacBook Pro 2019-2020 (Intel). Model number identifies exactly. Apple laptops have a model number on the bottom case (small print). Cross-reference to an Apple support lookup to identify exactly. Model A2141 = 16-inch MBP Intel 2019-2020.
A phone listing photo shows 'Battery Health: 92%' in Settings.

Solid battery — above Apple's 80% replacement threshold. Reasonable life left. iPhone battery health above 80% is functional. 100% is factory-new; 80% is where Apple recommends replacement. 92% is a good used-device number.
A phone listing photo shows 'Battery Health: 76%.'

Battery below threshold — replacement recommended. Factor ~$100 cost into offer. Below 80% battery health triggers reduced peak performance on newer iPhones (feature can be disabled). Apple battery service is ~$99. Third-party is cheaper but voids warranty.
A laptop listing photo shows a USB-C port with visible black discoloration inside.

Port corrosion or heavy debris — clean with compressed air first; if charging fails after cleaning, port replacement needed. USB-C ports collect pocket lint and debris. Compressed air often solves. If port is corroded (usually from moisture exposure), replacement is needed. Cleaning first is always the low-cost move.
A phone listing photo shows a Lightning port with visible metal wear at the contacts.

Heavy charging use — cable connection may become intermittent. Port replacement possible. Metal wear at port contacts indicates thousands of connections. Common on 3-5 year old daily-used phones. Still functional but sensitive to cable seating. Port replacement is possible but requires opening the phone.
A laptop listing shows 'M2' in the About This Mac screenshot.

Apple Silicon M2 chip — 2022-2023 era MacBook. Approximate age two to three years. M-series chips (M1 2020, M2 2022, M3 2023, M4 2024) date the machine and identify performance tier. About This Mac shows exact chip and configuration.
A tablet listing shows 'A15 Bionic' in the specs.

iPad or older iPhone chip from 2021-2022. Aligns with iPad Air 5 or iPhone 13 generation. Apple chip names map to release years. A15 Bionic = iPhone 13 (2021) and iPad Air 5 (2022). Cross-reference to device model to confirm.
A phone listing photo shows a red or pink line inside the SIM tray slot.

Water-damage indicator triggered. Device has experienced moisture exposure. Voided warranty, potential hidden damage. The SIM tray sticker changes color from white to red/pink on moisture contact. Once triggered, voided warranty. Device may still work but internal corrosion may be developing.
A laptop listing shows the screen with a vertical purple line running the full height.

Screen or display-cable failure. Screen replacement needed. Cost varies by model — hundreds to over $1000 for premium models. Vertical or horizontal lines are usually either the screen panel or the display cable that connects panel to logic-board. Diagnosis distinguishes; both are expensive on modern laptops.
A laptop keyboard photo shows shine on E, A, S, D, F, and space bar; other keys look unused.

Coder-use pattern — developer touch-typing hits home row heavily. Low overall hours despite visible key shine. Home-row shine pattern indicates touch-typing use. Common on developer machines. Total hours may be moderate; the shine looks worse than it is because it is concentrated on a few keys.
A laptop keyboard photo shows shine on W, A, S, D, spacebar, and shift; other keys used moderately.

Gamer-use pattern — WASD movement plus space and shift. Likely many hours of gaming; check thermal history. WASD shine indicates gaming use. Gaming laptops run hot; sustained thermal stress can degrade GPU, battery, and thermal paste. Ask about thermal maintenance history.
A power adapter photo shows the connector shape as MagSafe 2.

MacBook (2012-2018 era). MagSafe 2 is not compatible with MagSafe 1 or MagSafe 3 devices. MagSafe generations: 1 (2006-2012), 2 (2012-2018), 3 (2021-onward). Not cross-compatible without adapters. MagSafe 3 accepts higher wattage. Wattage matters — always match to device requirements.
A phone listing photo shows the back with 'iPhone' in one font and 'FCC ID' below in another.

Consistent with genuine Apple back-glass. Font mismatch or wrong FCC ID position = counterfeit indicator. Genuine iPhones have precise typography and consistent regulatory labeling. Counterfeit iPhones often show typographical inconsistencies — font weight, kerning, spacing. Compare to genuine reference photo online.
A phone listing photo shows the box with a barcode. The barcode's IMEI does not match the phone's About screen.

Box and device do not match — possible swap. Confirm which is correct or reject the listing. Box IMEI should always match device IMEI. Mismatched box is a common tactic to inflate perceived value (box from higher-tier model with lower-tier device). Compare all three: box, device, receipt.
A tablet listing photo shows the screen showing 'Activation Required.'

iCloud-locked device. Cannot activate without the previous owner's Apple ID and password. Effectively non-functional as-is. Activation Lock is Apple's anti-theft protection. Locked device is functionally a paperweight for normal use. Value drops to parts-only. Seller must un-lock before sale (requires original owner's Apple ID).
A phone listing photo shows 'This iPhone is supervised and managed by [Company].'

MDM-managed device — enterprise-locked. Cannot use as personal phone until MDM enrollment removed. Value is limited. MDM (Mobile Device Management) is an enterprise lock. Only the managing organization can remove it. Personal buyers get a device that constantly re-enrolls on wipe. Effectively unusable.
A laptop listing photo shows the About This Mac screen with 'macOS Sonoma 14.5.'

Current-generation macOS. Recent update, running latest software. Positive signal. Current OS version indicates recent maintenance. Older macOS on modern hardware might indicate seller doesn't maintain or the device can't run current OS (older Macs).
A laptop listing photo shows 'macOS Big Sur' on hardware from 2023.

Outdated OS on new-ish hardware. Suggests neglected updates. Ask why. 2023 hardware should easily run 2024 macOS. Older OS suggests the seller either doesn't maintain the machine or has a specific reason (compatibility with specific software). Ask before buying.
A phone listing shows 'unlocked' in the title and 'AT&T' in the carrier field.

Contradiction. 'Unlocked' means no carrier lock; 'AT&T' as carrier could mean 'sold through AT&T but now unlocked' OR 'still locked to AT&T.' Clarify with seller. Ambiguous listings need clarification. 'Unlocked' should mean any-carrier compatible. 'Carrier: AT&T' next to it usually means 'originally sold by AT&T.' Ask 'confirm carrier-unlocked' before buying.
A power supply photo shows '87W' in the label, adjacent to a laptop marked as 'MacBook Pro 16-inch.'

Under-powered adapter. 16-inch MacBook Pro requires 96W or higher. 87W adapter will charge slowly or maintain-not-charge under load. Wattage matching matters. Laptops draw more power under load than adapter provides -> battery drains during use. 87W is 15-inch generation. 16-inch needs 96W+ (2019-2020 Intel) or 140W (2021+ M-series Pro/Max).
A laptop bottom-case photo shows two Phillips-head screws replaced with hex-head screws.

Device has been opened for internal service. Screws non-original. Ask for repair history. Mismatched screws = machine has been opened. Legitimate repairs (battery replacement, SSD upgrade) leave this trace. Also indicates DIY work. Ask what was done and by whom.
A phone screen photo shows blue and pink burn-in artifacts of the status bar.

OLED burn-in — permanent. Reduces device value. Common on older OLED phones with heavy use. OLED pixels degrade at different rates depending on use. Long-static elements (status bar, keyboard, app icons) burn in over years. Not repairable; screen replacement solves. Value discount 30-50%.
A laptop listing photo shows chunky rectangular ports on both sides.

Pre-USB-C era laptop (roughly 2015 or earlier for Apple, 2018 or earlier for many PCs). USB-A, HDMI, Ethernet all common. Port layout dates the device roughly. USB-A dominated pre-2018 laptops; USB-C dominates post-2018. Mixed port layouts indicate transition-era (2016-2018).
A laptop listing photo shows four identical oval ports (two per side) and nothing else.

USB-C-only laptop — 2016+ MacBook Pro, some Dell XPS, others. All-USB-C requires dongles for most legacy accessories. USB-C-only means USB-A devices, HDMI displays, and Ethernet all require adapters. Some users prefer this simplicity; others find it frustrating. Adapters cost $10-50 each; a dock consolidates.
A phone listing photo shows the back with a large square camera module.

iPhone 11+ era (2019 onward) or Pixel 6+ era (2021 onward). Square/rectangle module distinguishes from earlier vertical or horizontal strip designs. Camera module shape dates the phone roughly. Square modules = late 2019+. Small circular = pre-2018. Big-rectangle = pro-tier flagship 2019+.
A laptop listing photo shows a keyboard where the letters have partly worn off some keys.

Heavy-use device. Depending on the pattern (touch-typist, coder, gamer) suggests use profile. Function still fine; cosmetic wear. Keycap wear is cosmetic — key still works. Common on 3+ year daily-use laptops. Consider replacement keyboard for aesthetic ($100-300 depending on model).
A phone listing photo shows a hairline crack running horizontally across the back glass.

Back glass crack. Apple replacement cost is high ($400-500 out of warranty). Third-party $100-200. Cosmetic effect on price. Back glass cracks on iPhones changed from mid-priced repair (glass-only) to expensive (whole rear assembly) starting around iPhone 12. Newer iPhones are much more expensive to repair back-glass on.
A phone listing photo shows the front screen with a large starburst crack originating from one corner.

Screen crack — replacement is standard. Cost varies: $200-400 for Apple, $100-200 for third-party. Screen replacement is common. Third-party parts often work fine but iPhone shows 'unable to verify parts' warning on newer models. Verify parts warranty from the shop.
A laptop listing photo shows the trackpad with a shiny spot at the bottom-right corner.

Right-handed user pattern — thumb rides bottom-right. Indicates handedness plus significant use. Trackpad function fine; cosmetic wear. Trackpad wear patterns reveal handedness and use frequency. Shine spot doesn't affect function. Some users add matte trackpad protectors to prevent.
A laptop hinge photo shows a visible small gap between screen and body when the laptop is closed.

Hinge is loose. Internal display cables may be under strain. Screen may open unevenly. Repair needed for long-term use. Hinge issues on modern thin laptops are hard to repair — hinges are integrated into unibody designs. Consider whether the laptop is worth the repair cost before buying.
A laptop listing photo shows a corner of the case with a visible dent.

Dropped device. Cosmetic dent visible; internal damage possible (cables loosened, screen alignment). Boot test essential. Drops cause both cosmetic dents and internal cable disconnections. Test all functions after purchase or before completing purchase. Storage integrity check recommended.
A phone listing has no photo of the back — just front.

Ask for a back photo before buying. Back may show cracks, dents, or camera-lens damage the seller is hiding. Sellers who omit certain angles are usually hiding something. Ask for missing angles specifically. If they refuse or delay, walk away.
A phone box photo shows the box has been opened neatly along one edge.

Standard opened-box state. Original packaging present, valuable for resale. Box condition itself affects value. Original boxes add resale value. Neatly opened is normal; ripped or damaged boxes reduce value. Complete accessories in box (cable, unused papers) further increase value.
A laptop listing photo shows the About Mac screen with 'This Mac (aging)' warning.

Older Mac no longer receiving major OS updates. May still work but security patches limited. Reduced resale value. 'Vintage' and 'obsolete' are Apple's terms for older hardware. Vintage means limited service; obsolete means none. Not necessarily broken but end-of-life. Software support ends too — check macOS compatibility list.
A phone listing photo shows 'iPhone 12 Pro' text on screen but the design in photo has three cameras arranged differently than the actual 12 Pro.

Suspicious — device does not match model claimed. Verify by comparing to genuine iPhone 12 Pro reference photos. Camera module layouts are model-specific. Any mismatch with the claimed model is a serious red flag — could be counterfeit device or wrong model listing. Always cross-check.
A laptop listing shows the Windows 'System Information' page with 'Windows 11 Pro.'

Modern Windows install. Confirms Windows 11 compatibility (TPM 2.0, UEFI Secure Boot, supported CPU). Windows 11 has hardware requirements. If installed and running Windows 11, the machine meets them. Older laptops (pre-2018 typically) do not qualify.
A laptop listing photo shows 'Windows 10 Home' on hardware from 2024.

New hardware running old Windows. Suggests seller has not upgraded, or has specific reason. Windows 10 goes EOL October 2025 — upgrade path matters. Windows 10 support ends October 2025. Modern hardware should run Windows 11. If seller keeps Windows 10 there is usually a reason (specific software compatibility, personal preference).
A phone listing photo shows 'Storage Almost Full' warning.

Not factory reset. Device is loaded with the seller's data. Insist on reset before purchase, or credentials to reset. Storage Almost Full means user data is present. Must be erased before ownership transfer. Never accept a device with owner's data; you can't verify erase completeness without doing it yourself.
A tablet screen photo shows a horizontal purple band along the top 20% of the screen.

Screen defect — likely display panel or ribbon-cable issue. Replacement needed. Horizontal or vertical bands = panel or cable failure. Replacement cost varies — tablet screens can be $150-500 to replace. Weigh against device residual value.
A power adapter listing shows 'compatible with MacBook Pro 2019-2023' but the connector is USB-C, not MagSafe 3.

Compatible via USB-C charging for 2016-2020 MBP (which are USB-C only). 2021+ MBP Pro/Max also accept USB-C but MagSafe 3 is native. Wattage matters. Modern MacBooks accept USB-C charging as an alternative to MagSafe. Wattage must match — 96W minimum for 14-inch Pro/Max, 140W for 16-inch. Third-party USB-C adapters work if wattage matches.
A phone listing photo shows the box with a plastic wrap seal still intact.

Sealed box — unopened, presumed new. Verify seal is genuine (should be tightly-adhered, tamper-evident with tabs). Genuine sealed boxes have tamper-evident seals — tabs that break when removed. Re-sealed boxes may look sealed but are detectable. Buy sealed boxes only from reputable sources.
A laptop listing photo shows Activity Monitor with 'Memory Pressure: Red' during idle.

Memory shortage — likely runaway process or insufficient RAM for workload. Investigate before assessing. Red memory pressure during idle is abnormal — usually means a specific app is misbehaving or the machine has too little RAM for a running workload. Reboot resolves most; if it recurs, RAM upgrade or app diagnosis needed.
A phone listing photo shows 'This iPhone Has Been Refurbished' notice.

Officially Apple-refurbished device. Comes with 1-year Apple warranty. Higher value than generic refurb. Apple Certified Refurbished carries the Apple 1-year warranty and full support. Third-party refurb varies wildly. Apple refurb pricing is usually 10-15% below new.
A laptop listing photo shows the About screen with '8GB RAM' on a 2023 MacBook Pro.

Base configuration. Adequate for light use; insufficient for professional workflows. Priced-tier accordingly. Base 8GB is the entry-level for MacBook Pro. Modern workflows (video editing, ML, virtualization) need 16-32GB+. Higher RAM configurations cost significantly more at purchase and hold value better.
A camera-body listing photo shows a shutter-count display of '65,000.'

Moderate use for a DSLR. Most consumer cameras rated 100,000-150,000 shutter actuations; pro bodies 300,000+. 65k = mid-life. Shutter count is the primary wear indicator on DSLRs. Consumer (Canon Rebel, Nikon D3xxx/D5xxx) rated 100k. Enthusiast (D7xxx, Canon xxD) rated 150k. Pro bodies rated 300k+. Mirrorless cameras don't have mechanical shutters (or have electronic shutters), changing the analysis.
A lens listing photo shows the front element with a small scratch visible in reflected light.

Front-element scratch usually has minimal effect on image quality (unless very deep/large). Some effect on flare handling. Priced accordingly — $50-200 discount depending on lens value. Front-element damage is much less impactful than rear-element damage. Coating scratches slightly affect flare handling but typically don't show in normal image capture. Test-shoot before buying if possible.
A lens listing photo shows visible fungus growth inside the lens elements.

Fungus inside lens. Spreads to other lenses if stored together. Cleaning is possible but expensive; often not worth for consumer lenses. Fungus etches lens coatings over time. Small early-stage fungus can be cleaned professionally; established fungus is permanent damage. Store lenses with silica gel to prevent.
A wireless-earbud listing photo shows heavy ear-wax buildup on the mesh tips.

Hygiene issue. Not a defect but off-putting. Deep-clean or replace ear tips. Sound may be muffled until cleaned. Ear-wax buildup is common on used earbuds. Replaceable ear tips are $10-30. Mesh screens sometimes clean with careful brushing; sometimes not. Consider hygiene when buying used personal-audio.
A wireless-earbud listing photo shows the charging case with a green light on.

Case has battery charge. Verify case charging via cable if possible. Case-battery health separately from earbud-battery health. Wireless earbud cases have separate batteries from the earbuds. Case battery charges earbuds; earbuds run from own batteries during use. Both age. Case usually lasts longer but replacement cases cost $50-100.
A monitor listing photo shows the panel-info sticker on the back reading '144Hz, 1440p, IPS.'

Gaming-tier monitor. 1440p resolution, 144Hz refresh rate, IPS panel type. Good all-purpose gaming/productivity monitor. Panel specs directly tell you the use tier. IPS = good colors + wide viewing angles (vs TN = fast + narrow, VA = deep blacks). Refresh rate and resolution combine to price tier.
A monitor listing photo shows the screen powered on with visible red pixels stuck across a diagonal band.

Dead-pixel band or panel failure. Multiple dead pixels in a band suggest connector or panel damage, not isolated pixel failures. Isolated dead pixels are common on new panels (some warranties won't cover unless 5+ pixels). Banded pixel failure = ribbon cable or panel damage, more serious. Replacement panels are usually not economical vs a new monitor.
A gaming console listing photo shows the console with heavy dust visible in cooling vents.

Dust buildup. Consoles need periodic cleaning for thermal performance. Reduces cooling efficiency; contributes to thermal throttling and fan noise. Dust buildup in consoles is normal but reduces cooling. Compressed-air cleaning helps. If console has been running hot, thermal paste replacement may be needed. Ask about maintenance history.
A controller listing photo shows visible wear on the thumbstick friction rings.

Heavy use. Thumbsticks are the first wear point on controllers. Replacement modules are $10-30 DIY; full replacement controller $60-70. Controller thumbstick wear is the classic use indicator. Drift is the next symptom (stick reports movement when centered). Replaceable modules exist for some controllers; not for all.
A camera listing has a description mentioning 'stuck aperture.'

Aperture blades stuck open or closed. Repair possible but often expensive relative to lens value. Especially common on older lenses. Stuck aperture is a common older-lens problem. Some lenses can be serviced ($100-300); others (cheap kit lenses) are not worth repair. Test aperture operation before buying used lenses.
A drone listing photo shows one propeller has a nicked tip.

Propeller damage. Propellers are cheap ($5-20 for a set). Replace immediately — damaged props cause flight instability and can fail mid-flight. Never fly with damaged propellers. Prop failure in flight = crash risk. Propellers are user-replaceable and cheap. Small nicks are non-negotiable — replace.
A TV listing photo shows the screen with dark cloudy patches in the corners.

Backlight bleed or clouding. Usually cosmetic; visible in dark scenes. Not repairable. Discount accordingly. Backlight clouding is a manufacturing variation. Some tolerance is normal on LCD TVs. Severe clouding is visible during dark scenes (film watching). OLED TVs don't have this issue but have burn-in risk instead.
A soundbar listing photo shows the back panel with 'HDMI eARC' port.

Modern soundbar with eARC (Enhanced Audio Return Channel). Supports lossless audio from TV via one HDMI cable. Post-2018 generation. eARC = enhanced audio return, supports high-bitrate lossless audio. HDMI ARC (older) supports compressed only. eARC on soundbar and TV both required for lossless. Look for the label specifically.
A receiver listing photo shows 'Atmos' and 'DTS:X' listed in the specs.

Modern AV receiver with object-based surround sound support. 7.1.4 or higher speaker configuration capable. Dolby Atmos and DTS:X are object-based surround formats (adding height channels). Require compatible speakers (ceiling or upfiring). Modern receivers 2015+ typically support both.
A turntable listing photo shows visible dust on the platter and no cartridge visible on the tonearm.

Missing cartridge — either removed for shipping or missing/lost. Ask before assuming. Replacement cartridges $50-500+. Turntable cartridges (the assembly holding the stylus that contacts the record) are removable and separately-priced components. Missing cartridge = must add cost of new one to purchase price.
A turntable listing photo shows the stylus visible with a slight bend.

Bent stylus. Damages records if used. Replace stylus (or entire cartridge) before playing anything. Bent stylus is a critical defect — will damage records. Some cartridges have replaceable styli ($30-150); others require full cartridge replacement.
A speaker listing photo shows one driver with a torn cone.

Driver damage. Repair possible for some speakers (re-cone service) but usually not economical for consumer speakers. Sound quality compromised. Driver damage = compromised sound quality. Re-cone services exist for enthusiast speakers ($100-300 per driver). Consumer speakers often not worth repair; buy replacement.
A vintage-audio receiver listing photo shows the front panel with meters and knobs.

1970-1980s stereo separate. Collectible if brand and condition support. Look for maker and model number for market value. Vintage stereo receivers have collector market. Certain brands (do not name-drop) command premium; others are commodities. Condition (working, cosmetically intact) drives value dramatically.
A camera listing photo shows the sensor visible with dust spots when zoomed in.

Sensor dust. Common on interchangeable-lens cameras. Sensor cleaning is a routine maintenance task ($30-80 professional; DIY possible with care). Sensor dust shows in photos as dark spots (visible against light uniform backgrounds like sky). Standard maintenance; not a defect. Wet-clean or dry-clean methods exist.
A camera listing photo shows the LCD screen with visible scratches.

Screen scratches. Cosmetic; doesn't affect image capture. Screen protector helps prevent further. Discount modestly. LCD screen on cameras is used for playback and menu navigation. Scratches are cosmetic. Screen replacement possible ($100-200) but rarely worthwhile.
A microphone listing photo shows the XLR connector with slight bend at the pin.

Damaged connector. Sometimes flexes back into place; sometimes requires repair. Test before buying. XLR connector pins can flex. Gentle straightening sometimes works. Persistent bent pin = repair or replace connector. Sound quality unaffected if connector still makes reliable contact.
A router listing shows 'WiFi 6' in the title and '2.4/5GHz dual-band' in specs.

Modern router. WiFi 6 = 802.11ax standard. Dual-band 2.4/5GHz is standard configuration. WiFi 6E and WiFi 7 add 6GHz band. WiFi generations: WiFi 5 (802.11ac, 2013), WiFi 6 (802.11ax, 2019), WiFi 6E (adds 6GHz, 2021), WiFi 7 (2024). Router WiFi generation should match client devices for best performance.
A NAS listing shows '2-bay' and '4TB HDDs' in configuration.

Small NAS with 4TB drives, likely 4TB usable in RAID 1 configuration, 8TB in JBOD. Personal/small-office scale. NAS bay count and drive size determine total capacity. RAID configuration affects usable. RAID 1 (mirror) halves capacity; RAID 5+ (parity) more efficient with drive redundancy. 2-bay = personal scale.
An external drive listing shows 'USB 3.0' and '2TB' in specs.

USB 3.0 (5 Gbps) 2TB external drive. Common consumer configuration. Internal drive type (HDD or SSD) is a critical question. Consumer external drives at 2TB are usually HDDs (mechanical); SSDs at 2TB cost significantly more. Ask if seller doesn't specify. HDD is fine for backup and bulk storage; SSD needed for performance.
A printer listing photo shows a page with a horizontal band of missing ink.

Clogged printhead or empty ink cartridge. Usually cleanable — printer maintenance cycle solves. Persistent bands = printhead replacement (expensive). Inkjet printers commonly show print defects from clogged nozzles. Cleaning cycles fix most. Some clogs are permanent — printhead replacement is typically not economical vs buying a new printer for consumer printers.
A printer listing shows 'toner cartridge needs replacement' warning.

Laser printer with depleted toner. Toner cartridges are consumables ($50-200). Verify cartridge fits model exactly before purchase. Toner cartridges are model-specific. Buy the exact part number listed for the printer. Third-party alternatives (compatible cartridges) are cheaper but sometimes have printhead-recognition issues.
A smartwatch listing photo shows the crown-side edge with a chip in the metal.

Cosmetic chip. Doesn't affect function. Might affect water resistance if IP-rated seal is compromised. Smartwatch case chips are cosmetic unless they damage the water-resistance seal. Apple Watch water-resistance can be compromised by damage; test with the water-resistance verification (or accept as compromised).
A smartwatch listing photo shows the battery-info screen reading 'Cycle count: 800.'

High cycle count. Battery near or past service threshold. Battery replacement $80-100 depending on model. Smartwatch batteries wear like phone batteries. Cycle counts over 500 usually indicate reduced capacity. Battery replacement service available for most smartwatches from OEM or third-party.
A tablet listing photo shows one corner of the screen with a small crack pattern radiating from an impact point.

Impact damage. Cracked screen. Replacement cost varies significantly — Apple iPad screens $150-500+ depending on model. Impact damage to tablet screens is common. Full screen replacement is usually needed (not just glass). Cost varies by model; larger tablets more expensive.
A laptop listing shows 'A1502' as the model in a bottom-case photo.
13-inch MacBook Pro Retina (late 2013 to early 2015). A1502 is the 13-inch Retina MacBook Pro from 2013-2015 era. Intel processor, non-Touch Bar, MagSafe 2 charging. Approximate age 10 years now.
A laptop bottom-case photo shows 'A2338.'
13-inch MacBook Pro M1 or M2 (2020-2022). A2338 = 13-inch MacBook Pro M1 (2020) or M2 (2022). Apple Silicon, function-key row (older Touch Bar). Verify M1 vs M2 from About screen.
A laptop bottom-case photo shows 'A2442.'
14-inch MacBook Pro M1 Pro/Max (2021-2022). A2442 = 14-inch MBP with M1 Pro or M1 Max, notch, MagSafe 3, three USB-C, HDMI, SD, and headphone jack. Marks the return of ports to MacBook Pro line.
A phone bottom-edge photo shows a Lightning port with squared corners.

iPhone 5 through iPhone 14 (Lightning was replaced by USB-C on iPhone 15 in 2023). Lightning connector was on iPhones from iPhone 5 (2012) through iPhone 14 (2022). iPhone 15 (2023) switched to USB-C. Bottom-port shape = generation indicator.
A phone bottom-edge photo shows a USB-C port.

iPhone 15 or newer (2023+). Also all recent iPads and Android phones. USB-C on iPhone starts with iPhone 15 (2023). All iPads use USB-C since 2018. Most Android phones USB-C since 2017. Some entry-level Androids still micro-USB.
A phone photo shows 'iPhone' text on the back with no color or shape.

iPhone X or later. iPhone SE (2016 and later) and iPhone 6-8 still had 'iPhone' text plus color logo. Pure text-only + Apple logo = X and forward. Apple back-label evolution: colored logo + text (iPhone 1 through 5), silver logo + text (iPhone 5s through 8), silver logo only (iPhone X and later). Text presence dates the device roughly.
A laptop listing photo shows the About This Mac screen with 'Chip Apple M3 Pro.'

M3 Pro MacBook — 2023-2024. Higher-tier chip than base M3; supports up to 36GB unified memory. M-series tiers: base (M1/M2/M3/M4), Pro, Max, Ultra. Pro is mid-tier; suitable for professional workloads. Max is high-end. Ultra is workstation-tier (only Mac Studio and Mac Pro). Configuration matters for value.
A phone listing photo shows two rear cameras stacked vertically.

iPhone X, XS, 11 (standard), or similar dual-camera generation. Not the pro-tier tri-camera arrangement. Camera count and arrangement date the phone. Vertical dual-cam = iPhone X-XS-11 era (2017-2019). Triple camera in square = 11 Pro forward. Camera evolution is fast — use as anchor for era.
A phone listing photo shows three rear cameras in a square arrangement.

iPhone 11 Pro through 15 Pro or Pro Max — the square-triple-camera design was iPhone 11 Pro (2019) onward for Pro-tier models. Square-triple-camera = Pro-tier iPhone 11+ (2019 onward). Actual model requires cross-referencing to size, notch/Dynamic Island, and other features.
A phone screen photo shows a small round dark cutout for the front camera at the top-center.

Punch-hole camera. Common on many Android flagships and iPhone 14/15 Pro (Dynamic Island evolved this). Front-camera arrangements evolved: home-button-below (pre-2017), notch (iPhone X-13, many Androids), punch-hole (Android 2019+), Dynamic Island (iPhone 14 Pro+). Cutout shape and location date the phone.
A tablet listing shows 'iPad Pro (M4)' as the model.

iPad Pro M4 (2024). Latest Pro-tier iPad. Tandem OLED, 11 or 13 inch, thinnest iPad ever. M-series chips in iPad Pro: M1 (2021), M2 (2022), M4 (2024, skipped M3). Screen: LCD then Mini-LED (2021-2022) then Tandem OLED (2024). Latest is technological showcase.
A watch listing photo shows the case marked 'Series 9.'

Apple Watch Series 9 (2023). Aluminum or stainless steel, GPS or GPS+Cellular. Apple Watch series: 1-9 plus SE (multiple generations) and Ultra (1 and 2). Series 9 launched 2023, upgraded chip vs Series 8, added double-tap gesture. Aluminum vs steel affects price.
A watch listing photo shows the case marked 'Apple Watch Ultra 2.'

Apple Watch Ultra 2 (2023). Rugged titanium case, action button, 100m water resistance, brightest screen. Apple Watch Ultra tier — rugged design for extreme sports. Ultra 1 (2022), Ultra 2 (2023). Higher price tier. Larger 49mm case. Titanium construction.
A laptop listing shows the About screen with 'MacBook Pro (16-inch, 2019).'

Intel-era 16-inch MacBook Pro. Introduced improved thermal design over 2016-2019 15-inch. Last generation before Apple Silicon. 2019 16-inch MBP was significant — retired the butterfly keyboard, added 8-core Intel, improved thermals. Direct predecessor to M1 Pro/Max 16-inch (2021).
A phone listing shows the About screen with 'iPhone 15 Pro Max, 512GB.'

Current-generation flagship (2023). Titanium case, USB-C, A17 Pro chip, tetraprism telephoto lens. iPhone 15 Pro Max is the premium tier of the 2023 generation. Titanium (lighter than steel used in earlier Pros), USB-C, tetraprism 5x telephoto (Max only, not standard Pro). Highest MSRP tier.
A phone listing shows 'iPhone 16 Pro' in the About screen.

2024 flagship-tier iPhone. A18 Pro chip. New camera button (capacitive). Apple Intelligence-ready hardware. iPhone 16 Pro (2024) adds new camera button, wider Dynamic Island, A18 Pro chip, and required hardware for Apple Intelligence. Priced as flagship tier.
A laptop listing photo shows a Windows 10 desktop with 'Intel Core i7-8550U' in system info.

Kaby Lake Refresh (8th gen Intel U-series, 2017). Ultrabook processor. Getting old for 2026; probably still functional but slow. Intel Core i7-8550U was a 2017-2018 mainstream ultrabook processor. 15W thermal design. Getting long in the tooth for modern workloads. Windows 11 supported (barely, requires specific TPM configuration).
A laptop system info shows 'Intel Core i9-13900H.'

Intel 13th generation, high-end mobile H-series. 2023 gaming/workstation laptop tier. Powerful; runs hot. Intel Core i9-13900H = 13th gen mobile (2023) high-performance chip. Common in gaming laptops and mobile workstations. Hybrid P-cores + E-cores architecture. Thermal management is critical.
A laptop system info shows 'AMD Ryzen 9 7940HS.'

AMD Ryzen 7000 series (Phoenix), mobile high-performance. 2023 laptop tier. Competitive with Intel i7/i9 mobile. AMD Ryzen 7000 mobile = 2023 laptop tier. Phoenix architecture. Good for productivity and gaming. RDNA3 integrated graphics or paired with discrete Radeon/GeForce dGPUs.
A laptop system info shows 'NVIDIA GeForce RTX 4070 Laptop GPU.'

NVIDIA Ada Lovelace mobile. 2023 gaming laptop mid-high tier. Runs modern games at high settings. Ray tracing capable. NVIDIA RTX 4000 mobile series = 2023 laptop generation. RTX 4050/4060/4070/4080/4090 tiers. 4070 is upper-midrange. Ada Lovelace architecture. DLSS 3 with frame generation.
A monitor listing photo shows 'OLED' in specs and '3440x1440' resolution.

Ultrawide OLED monitor. Premium tier. 21:9 aspect ratio. Excellent color, deep blacks. Watch for burn-in risk with static content. OLED monitors emerged in gaming space around 2022-2023. Excellent image quality, wider color gamut, per-pixel blacks. Burn-in risk exists but modern panels have pixel-shift and other mitigations.
A monitor photo shows a stand with a curved back and a large brand medallion.

Premium tier monitor. Likely gaming or professional-oriented. Ergonomic stand indicates $500+ retail tier. Premium monitors have ergonomic stands (height, tilt, swivel, pivot adjustable). Basic monitors have simple tilt-only stands. Stand quality correlates with price tier.
A camera listing photo shows the mode dial marked with 'PASM+Auto+Custom.'

Enthusiast to prosumer camera. PASM (Program, Aperture, Shutter, Manual) plus custom modes indicates prosumer feature set. Mode dial layout indicates camera tier. Basic cameras: Auto + Scene modes. Prosumer: Auto + PASM. Pro: PASM + Custom slots (C1, C2, C3, etc.), no Scene. Sensor size and body construction also matter.
A camera listing shows 'APS-C' as the sensor size.

APS-C sensor — smaller than full-frame, larger than Micro Four Thirds. Common in enthusiast and mid-tier bodies. Sensor sizes: Micro Four Thirds < APS-C < Full-frame < Medium Format. APS-C is the largest common sensor size — good image quality, more compact bodies than full-frame. 1.5-1.6x crop factor.
A camera listing shows 'Full Frame' as the sensor size.

Full-frame (36x24mm) sensor. Professional tier. Larger sensor = more light, better low-light, shallower depth of field. Higher cost. Full-frame sensors match traditional 35mm film size. Advantages: better low-light, wider dynamic range, shallower DoF at same aperture. Higher cost, larger lenses.
A lens listing photo shows '50mm f/1.4.'

Standard prime, wide-aperture. Classic portrait/low-light lens. Every mount has a version. 50mm f/1.4 is the classic 'nifty fifty upgraded' — one stop faster than f/1.8, better bokeh, sharper wide open. Available in every mount. Price varies from ~$300 (Canon/Nikon budget) to $1500+ (premium).
A lens listing photo shows '70-200mm f/2.8.'

Professional telephoto zoom. Wedding, event, sports, portrait workhorse. Premium tier lens in every system. 70-200mm f/2.8 is the professional standard telephoto zoom — every serious brand has one. Sports, weddings, portraits, events. Weather-sealed, image-stabilized on modern versions. $1500-3000 retail.
A lens listing photo shows '16-35mm f/2.8.'

Professional wide-angle zoom. Landscape, architecture, wedding-context establishing shots. Premium tier. 16-35mm f/2.8 is the pro wide zoom — landscape, architecture, real estate, event establishing shots. Constant f/2.8 aperture, weather-sealed. $1500-2500 retail.
A lens listing photo shows '24-70mm f/2.8.'

Professional standard zoom — the 'do-everything' lens. Wide-to-short-telephoto range, constant fast aperture. 24-70mm f/2.8 is the workhorse — covers most subjects. Every pro system has one. Common single-lens choice for events. $1500-3000 retail.
A drone listing photo shows the drone body with a red band on the propeller mount.

DJI drone (specific model requires identification). Red-band coloring is DJI's forward-indicator convention. DJI drones (do not brand-name in operator listings) use color-coded arms — one color forward, another backward. Model varies by size and generation. Verify model from bottom-body sticker or app.
A drone listing shows 'return-to-home' as a feature in the specs.

GPS-equipped drone. Basic safety feature — drone returns to takeoff point on signal loss or low battery. Standard on all serious drones. Return-to-home is a critical safety feature. All modern serious drones have it. Prosumer and up. Verify GPS lock before flight for reliable RTH.
A phone battery replacement listing shows 'OEM' in the title.

Original Equipment Manufacturer part. Compatible with iPhone's parts-verification system on newer models. Higher tier than 'aftermarket.' OEM parts pass iPhone's parts-recognition system on newer models. Aftermarket may work functionally but trigger warnings. OEM sourced from Apple or Apple-authorized suppliers.
A phone battery replacement listing shows 'compatible' but no OEM claim.

Third-party battery. Functionally may work but iPhone will show 'Genuine part' verification warning. Slight capacity/quality variance. Third-party batteries can be reliable but iPhone displays a warning that the battery cannot be verified as genuine. Some third-party batteries have programmed capacity to match; others have real capacity but no chip programming.
A phone screen replacement listing shows 'True Tone required calibration' as a note.

Replacement screen requires True Tone calibration to work with iPhone's ambient-light color adjustment. Requires programming tool or calibration transfer service. True Tone is Apple's ambient-color-adjustment feature. Replacement screens lose it unless the original screen's chip is transferred, or a programming tool is used. Screen still works without True Tone.
A camera listing shows 'GPS embedded' as a feature.

Modern camera with built-in GPS for geotagging photos. Common on 2015+ enthusiast/pro bodies and phone cameras. Built-in GPS geotagging is common on modern cameras (2015+). Older cameras used external GPS accessories. Some phones and cameras support Bluetooth-tethered GPS from phones.
A camera listing photo shows a hot shoe with three contacts visible.

Standard 3-contact hot shoe. Compatible with most brand-agnostic flashes and accessories. Not TTL flash without matching brand and TTL contacts. Hot shoe contacts vary. Center + one contact = basic manual flash. Center + multiple contacts = TTL (through-the-lens metering) and possibly other functions. Each brand has proprietary layouts.
A camera photo shows an EF mount marking.

Canon EF (electronic focus) mount — Canon DSLR lens mount from 1987 until Canon transitioned to RF mount for mirrorless in 2018. Canon EF mount was the standard for Canon DSLRs. RF mount is the mirrorless successor. Adapters allow EF lenses on RF bodies. EF-S is APS-C-specific EF. Compatibility matters.
A camera photo shows an RF mount marking.

Canon RF mount — Canon's mirrorless mount, launched 2018. Newer lenses than EF, generally larger due to short flange distance. Canon RF mount is the modern Canon mirrorless system. RF lenses on RF bodies natively. EF lenses via adapter. Some RF lenses have no EF equivalent (e.g., RF 28-70mm f/2).
A camera photo shows an FE mount marking (Sony).

Sony FE mount — full-frame mirrorless mount for Sony's Alpha 7/9 series. Introduced 2013. Sony FE = full-frame E-mount. E-mount also exists in APS-C form (a6xxx series). FE lenses cover full-frame sensors; regular E-mount lenses may vignette on full-frame.
A camera photo shows an X mount marking.

Fujifilm X mount — APS-C mirrorless system. Distinct from GFX (medium-format). Fujifilm X mount is their APS-C mirrorless lineup — X-T, X-Pro, X-H, X-S series bodies. Excellent APS-C sensor and lens ecosystem. Retro-styled body design.
A camera photo shows a Z mount marking.

Nikon Z mount — Nikon's mirrorless mount, launched 2018. Full-frame (Z 6/7/8/9) and APS-C (Z 30/50/fc). Nikon Z mount is the modern Nikon mirrorless system. Large mount diameter allows for wide-aperture lenses (Z 58mm f/0.95 Noct). Z-mount adapter accepts F-mount DSLR lenses.
A camera photo shows an F mount marking.

Nikon F mount — DSLR mount, 1959-2020 primary Nikon mount. Longest-lived camera mount in history. Nikon F mount has been in continuous use since 1959. F-mount DSLR bodies discontinued 2020 (Nikon shifted to Z-mount). F-mount lenses still widely available used, adapt to Z-mount.
A camera photo shows an L mount marking.

L-mount alliance — Leica, Panasonic, Sigma share this full-frame mirrorless mount. L-mount is the Leica-Panasonic-Sigma alliance mount. Full-frame mirrorless bodies from all three brands share lenses. Distinct from Leica M-mount (rangefinder) and Leica S/SL earlier.
A watch listing shows 'sapphire crystal' as a feature.

Sapphire crystal watch crystal — hard, scratch-resistant. Standard on premium watches, some mid-tier. Watch crystals: acrylic (soft, cheap, easily polished), mineral glass (mid-tier, hard-coated), sapphire (Mohs 9, very scratch-resistant). Sapphire is standard on premium; some mid-tier ($200+) have it.
A watch listing shows '20 ATM' water resistance rating.

Diving-grade water resistance. 20 atmospheres = 200 meters. Suitable for actual diving (with proper watch design). Water-resistance ratings: 3 ATM (rain-splash only), 5 ATM (light swimming), 10 ATM (swimming), 20 ATM+ (diving, snorkeling), ISO 6425 dive-certified (professional diving). Rating alone doesn't guarantee dive-suitability.
A wireless-earbud listing shows 'ANC' and 'transparency mode' as features.

Active Noise Cancellation earbuds with transparency mode. Premium tier feature set. ANC = Active Noise Cancellation, uses microphones and processing to cancel ambient noise. Transparency mode uses same microphones to pass ambient sound through. Standard on premium wireless earbuds since ~2019.
A router listing shows 'mesh capable' in specs.

Mesh WiFi system. Multiple router nodes work together for whole-house coverage. Alternative to single high-power router. Mesh WiFi = multiple nodes forming a network. Better whole-house coverage than single router. Nodes communicate via dedicated backhaul or via WiFi. Common brands (do not name-drop) all offer mesh systems.
A NAS listing shows 'ZFS filesystem' in specs.

Enthusiast/pro NAS. ZFS is a modern filesystem with data-integrity features (checksumming, snapshots, self-healing). More powerful than typical consumer-NAS filesystems. ZFS filesystem is used in higher-tier NAS systems and self-built NAS (TrueNAS, FreeNAS). Features: data checksumming, snapshots, compression, deduplication, RAID-like RAID-Z. Consumer NAS may use ext4 or btrfs instead.
A vintage-audio receiver photo shows 'Silver-front' panel with black-anodized meter panel.

1970s-early-80s vintage audio aesthetic. Silver-face receivers are the classic vintage look. Vintage audio aesthetic: silver-face (1970s-early-80s), black-face (early 80s onward). Silver-face is more collectible. Look for maker, model, condition. Working vintage receivers command significant prices.
A turntable listing shows 'MC cartridge' as a spec.

Moving-coil cartridge — higher-tier than moving-magnet (MM). Requires phono preamp with MC compatibility. Turntable cartridge types: Moving-Magnet (MM, more common, higher output, cheaper) vs Moving-Coil (MC, higher-tier, lower output, requires more sensitive preamp). MC preamp support is not universal.
A turntable listing shows '33 1/3 and 45 RPM' as speeds.

Standard turntable — 33 1/3 (LPs) and 45 (singles). Some also support 78 (older shellac records). No 78 = cannot play pre-1948 records. Turntable speeds: 33 1/3 (LPs), 45 (singles), 78 (older shellac records). 78 support requires appropriate stylus profile and often a separate cartridge. Not universal.
A monitor listing shows 'HDR400' in specs.

Entry-level HDR (High Dynamic Range) certification. Real HDR needs HDR600+ or HDR1000+ for meaningful impact. HDR certification tiers: HDR400 (entry, mostly marketing), HDR600 (mid), HDR1000 (real HDR), HDR1400 (high-end). HDR400 requires only 400 nits peak brightness which is standard for many non-HDR monitors.
A monitor listing shows 'DisplayHDR 1000' in specs.

High-end HDR-certified monitor. 1000+ nits peak brightness, local dimming zones. Meaningful HDR performance. DisplayHDR 1000 = 1000 nits peak, local dimming (LCD) or per-pixel (OLED). Real HDR experience — noticeable in HDR content. Premium monitor tier.
A gaming console listing shows 'digital edition' in the title.

No optical disc drive. Cannot play physical game discs or Blu-rays. Digital purchases only through the platform's store. Digital-edition consoles (PS5 Digital, Xbox Series S) omit the disc drive. Digital-only ownership model. Cheaper hardware; games same price digitally. Cannot play disc-based backwards-compatible games either.
A drone listing shows 'obstacle avoidance' as a feature.

Camera- or sensor-based obstacle avoidance. Higher-tier drone. Standard on prosumer 2019+ models. Obstacle avoidance uses forward, downward, and sometimes surrounding sensors to detect and avoid obstacles. Prevents crashes into trees, walls, wires. Standard on prosumer drones from certain brands (do not name-drop) 2019+.
A VR headset listing shows 'inside-out tracking' as a feature.

Modern VR headset. Cameras on the headset itself track its position — no external base stations needed. Standalone-capable. Inside-out tracking (headset cameras track own position) vs outside-in (external cameras/base stations track headset). Inside-out is simpler for consumer use, adequate for most content. Outside-in is more precise (higher-end professional VR).
A phone listing shows 'Wi-Fi 7' as a feature.

Latest WiFi standard (2024). Requires WiFi 7 router for full benefit. Backward compatible with older WiFi standards. WiFi 7 (802.11be) launched 2024. Adds 320 MHz channels, MLO (multi-link operation), 4K QAM. Real benefit requires WiFi 7 router. Backward compatible so still connects to older APs.
A laptop listing shows 'Thunderbolt 5' in specs.

Latest Thunderbolt generation (2024). 80 Gbps bidirectional, 120 Gbps to display. Requires TB5 devices to benefit. Thunderbolt 5 doubles TB4 speeds (40 Gbps -> 80 Gbps bidirectional). Supports up to 240W USB-PD charging. Backward compatible with TB4/USB-C. Emerged on 2024+ high-end laptops.
A camera lens photo shows a wide barrel with two ring controls and an aperture ring visible.

Enthusiast/pro-tier lens design. Aperture ring on the lens (vs body-controlled aperture) is a manual-control feature — common on Fujifilm, some mirrorless designs, and vintage lenses. Aperture rings on lens body are a tactile feature — common on manual/vintage lenses, standard on Fujifilm lens designs, and recently reintroduced by Nikon Z-mount lenses. Preferred by manual-control-oriented photographers.
A camera listing shows 'IBIS' as a feature.

In-Body Image Stabilization. Sensor-shift stabilization system. Works with any lens (including non-stabilized lenses). Common on mirrorless bodies. IBIS (In-Body Image Stabilization) moves the sensor to counteract camera shake. Works independently of lens stabilization; can also work with lens-IS for combined stabilization. Standard on mid-range and up mirrorless bodies.
A microphone listing shows 'condenser' vs 'dynamic' in specs.
