Condenser microphone. Requires phantom power (48V typically). Sensitive; good for studio/vocal recording. Dynamic mics don't need power and are less sensitive. Microphone types: dynamic (rugged, less sensitive, common for stage vocals and instruments), condenser (sensitive, requires power, common for studio recording). Different use cases and price ranges.
A microphone listing shows 'USB' as connection type.

USB microphone. Plug-and-play, no interface required. Common for podcasting and casual voice recording. Not typically pro-tier. USB microphones are plug-and-play. Convenient for beginners and podcasters. XLR microphones require an audio interface but offer more flexibility. Pro-tier usually XLR.
A microphone listing shows 'XLR' as connection type.

XLR-connector professional microphone. Requires audio interface with XLR input. Standard pro-tier connection. XLR (three-pin balanced) is the professional audio connector standard. Requires an audio interface between mic and computer. More flexible and higher-quality signal path than USB. Standard in studio and live sound.
A laptop listing shows 'OLED display' as a feature.

OLED laptop display. Excellent color, per-pixel blacks. Watch for burn-in with heavy static content. OLED laptop displays are premium. Excellent contrast, wide color gamut, per-pixel emission. Burn-in risk with static content over years. Some laptops rotate pixels or offer burn-in reduction features.
A camera listing shows '4K 60p' video capability.

4K video at 60 fps. Modern camera capability. Common on mirrorless and prosumer DSLR from 2018+ high-end and 2020+ mid-range. Video specs: 1080p 60p (basic), 4K 30p (common 2016+), 4K 60p (common 2018+ high-end, 2020+ mid), 8K 30p (2020+ flagship). Higher frame rates and resolutions require more processing and heat dissipation.
A camera listing shows '10-bit color' video recording.

10-bit color depth video (1024 shades per channel vs 8-bit's 256). Better for color grading. Enthusiast/pro feature. 10-bit color depth means more color information per pixel. Advantages: better color grading headroom, smoother gradients, less banding in low-light. Requires HDMI 2.0+ for external recording; some cameras record internally.
A lens listing shows 'weather sealed' or 'moisture resistant' in specs.

Sealed against dust and light moisture. Not fully waterproof but survives light rain, dusty environments. Common on higher-tier lenses. Weather-sealing is common on higher-tier lenses. Includes gaskets at mount, focus/zoom ring seals, and switch seals. Not waterproof — light rain OK, submersion not. Body must also be sealed for full system protection.
A DSLR body photo shows 'PENTAX' branding.

Pentax DSLR. Niche brand today. K-mount lenses. Some models have unique features (weather-sealing, in-body stabilization) at affordable prices. Pentax is a niche but active DSLR brand. K-mount has 40+ years of backward compatibility. Weather-sealed and IBIS features at lower prices than Canon/Nikon equivalents. Small user base means less accessory ecosystem.
A camera listing shows 'medium format' as sensor size.

Medium-format sensor. Larger than full-frame. Professional/high-end tier. Fujifilm GFX and Hasselblad X-series most common in this space. Medium-format sensors (roughly 44x33mm to 60x45mm) are larger than full-frame. Higher resolution, better tonal range, more shallow DOF. Professional tier — most bodies $5k+, lenses expensive. Fujifilm GFX and Hasselblad X-series are the most affordable entries.
A watch listing shows 'automatic movement' in specs.

Mechanical self-winding watch. Wound by wrist motion. Requires periodic service. Higher-tier than quartz for enthusiast market. Watch movements: quartz (battery-powered, accurate, cheap), automatic mechanical (self-winding via wrist motion, requires service every 5-10 years, enthusiast tier), manual mechanical (hand-wound, similar service needs). Automatic is the classic enthusiast/luxury tier.
A watch listing shows 'quartz' movement.

Battery-powered watch. Accurate, low maintenance. Battery replacement every 2-5 years. Consumer to mid-tier. Quartz movements are battery-powered, extremely accurate (a few seconds/month deviation), low-maintenance. Consumer to mid-tier watches. Some pro/tactical watches also use quartz for reliability.
A watch listing shows 'sapphire crystal, sunburst dial, exhibition case-back.'

Enthusiast/luxury tier watch. Sapphire crystal + decorative dial + see-through case-back showing the movement. Priced accordingly. Watch details indicating enthusiast/luxury: sapphire crystal, decorative dial finishes (sunburst, guilloché), exhibition case-back showing decorated movement. Combined = premium tier.
A camera lens photo shows 'STM' or 'USM' in the model name.

Canon lens autofocus type. STM = Stepping Motor (smooth for video AF); USM = Ultrasonic Motor (fast for stills). Both current-generation. Canon lens AF motors: USM (Ultrasonic, fast, quiet-ish, older-generation on some lenses), STM (Stepping Motor, smooth for video, quiet), Nano USM (newest generation, fastest). Type affects video AF smoothness and stills speed.
A camera lens photo shows 'IS' in the model name.

Canon Image Stabilization lens. Stabilization helps handheld shots at slower shutter speeds. Standard on modern tele and long lenses. Canon IS = Image Stabilization. Nikon calls it VR (Vibration Reduction). Sony calls it OSS (Optical SteadyShot). All do the same thing — stabilize the image via lens element movement.
A wireless-earbud listing shows 'multipoint pairing' as a feature.

Can connect to two devices simultaneously. Switch between phone and laptop without repairing. Enthusiast tier feature. Multipoint pairing lets one earbud pair connect to two source devices at once. Common on business-oriented and premium earbuds. Not universal — many consumer earbuds are single-device.
A wireless-earbud listing shows 'IP54' rating.

Dust-protected and water-splash-resistant. Suitable for workouts and rain; not for full submersion. IP ratings: first digit dust (0-6), second digit water (0-9). IP54 = limited dust ingress, splash-proof. IPX7 = submersion up to 1m for 30 minutes. IP67/68 = high dust + submersion. Higher-rated earbuds handle sweat and rain better.
A router listing shows 'DFS channels supported' in specs.

5GHz DFS (Dynamic Frequency Selection) channel support. More available channels, less interference. Regulatory-mandated pause if radar detected. DFS channels are 5GHz channels shared with weather radar. Router pauses if radar detected. Provides more available spectrum but occasional micro-outages. Higher-tier routers support DFS; consumer routers often skip it.
A NAS listing shows 'transcoding capable' in specs.

Hardware or software transcoding for media streaming. Video reformats on the fly for client devices. Requires capable CPU or GPU. Transcoding is important for media servers (Plex, Jellyfin). Hardware transcoding uses GPU/dedicated silicon; software transcoding uses CPU. Higher-tier NAS have Intel QuickSync or dedicated hardware for efficient transcoding.
A monitor listing shows 'G-Sync' or 'FreeSync' as a feature.

Variable refresh-rate support. G-Sync = NVIDIA, FreeSync = AMD (also VESA Adaptive Sync). Reduces tearing and stutter in games. Variable refresh rate matches monitor refresh to game frame rate, eliminating tearing. G-Sync (NVIDIA proprietary, hardware module), FreeSync (AMD, open standard), Adaptive Sync (VESA industry standard). G-Sync Compatible = FreeSync-branded that works with NVIDIA.
A phone listing photo shows 'Titanium' as the frame material.

iPhone 15 Pro/Pro Max or newer premium Android. Titanium frames introduced on iPhone 15 Pro line (2023). Lighter and stronger than aluminum, more premium feel. Titanium frame indicates premium tier device (2023+). iPhone 15 Pro was first Apple use; some Android premium phones followed. Titanium is more expensive to work than aluminum, hence the premium positioning.
You are buying a used MacBook Pro at market rate. Battery health shows 88%, but the About screen shows 'Service Recommended.'

Service Recommended on a battery with 88% health is unusual — usually appears at 80% or below. Cell may be unbalanced or a specific fault detected. Battery replacement recommended before purchase. Battery Service Recommended alert can appear for reasons other than low capacity — cell imbalance, fault detection, temperature history. 88% health with the alert is a yellow flag; investigate before buying.
You buy a phone that boots to Setup Assistant. Language, region, WiFi complete, then 'Sign In With Apple ID' shows a specific email as required.

Activation Lock — previous owner didn't remove device from their Apple ID. Contact seller for remote removal or reject the device. Activation Lock persists across factory reset. Previous owner must remove the device from their Find My devices list (from iCloud web or another Apple device signed into the same account). Only they can do it.
A laptop's About screen shows unified memory: 16GB, and you can see two Chrome windows, Slack, and VS Code open with Memory Pressure yellow-approaching-red.

16GB is being pushed hard by the workflow shown. This user's typical workload requires 32GB+ for comfortable operation. Advise buyer whether their workload fits. Modern workflows (multiple browser windows, Slack, IDEs) consume RAM aggressively. 16GB is entry-level for professional use in 2026. Advise buyer to consider RAM needs vs the machine's fixed RAM.
A phone listing shows 'True Depth camera not working' in condition notes.

Face ID hardware failure. Face ID becomes unusable. iPhone still functional for other purposes but a major feature lost. Repair cost varies; not always economical. True Depth camera failure disables Face ID. Repair requires replacement of the entire True Depth camera assembly plus calibration. Apple charges hundreds; third-party repair often not possible due to calibration requirements.
A laptop bottom-case photo shows a Torx-security-head screw at the RAM/SSD access panel.

Security screw indicates device was originally opened by service tech with special tool. Non-standard access requires tools; user likely didn't open. Torx security (with center pin) requires special driver. Presence indicates OEM installation or authorized service. User modifications usually leave Phillips or standard Torx screws. Consider that internal components are likely OEM.
A phone listing photo shows 'Do Not Sell' or 'iCloud Lost Mode' visible on lock screen.

Stolen device flagged in Find My. Do not buy. Reporting to authorities recommended if you can identify the source of the listing. Lost Mode messages indicate the device is reported stolen or lost. Buying such a device is receiving stolen property in most jurisdictions. Report to platform and to law enforcement if possible.
A laptop photo shows the About screen with 'macOS 12 Monterey' on hardware that supports macOS 14+.

Older-OS-on-newer-hardware. Ask if a specific software requires older macOS (some pro audio, older CAD software). Alternatively, seller may be uninformed. Deliberately kept older OS versions usually mean the user relies on specific software with compatibility issues. Not necessarily bad; ask which apps. Buyer's own workflow needs may or may not require upgrading.
A camera listing shows 'shutter count: 500' on a 6-year-old body.

Very lightly used. Either barely-used or shutter has been replaced. Ask; if replaced, ask when and why. Extremely low shutter count on an old body = barely used OR shutter recently replaced. Replaced shutter is a positive (fresh part) if disclosed. Undisclosed replacement is suspicious.
A lens listing photo shows the rear element with a distinct fingerprint.

Rear-element fingerprint is more damaging to image than front-element scratches. Clean with proper lens-cleaning technique before assessing damage. Rear-element damage/dirt affects image more than front (rear is closer to sensor plane). Clean carefully with proper cleaning materials — never touch rear element with fingers repeatedly.
A phone listing shows 'Face ID not available due to depth camera issue' in Settings.

True Depth camera failure. Face ID disabled. Repair cost significant; some cases not economical. True Depth camera issues are typically hardware — either the projector, receiver, or associated circuitry. Third-party repair usually requires OEM parts due to calibration; Apple repair is standard fix. Cost varies.
A laptop system-report shows 'GPU: NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3080 Laptop with 8GB VRAM.'

RTX 3080 Laptop GPU with 8GB VRAM = 130W-165W-TGP-tier configuration. Manufacturer can configure the same GPU at different TGP (Total Graphics Power) so real performance varies by laptop model. NVIDIA laptop GPUs are configurable by TGP — same GPU name can run at very different power/performance levels depending on the laptop's cooling. Look up the specific laptop's TGP configuration for realistic performance expectations.
A phone listing shows 'Charge port replacement recommended' in the diagnostics screen.

Charge port hardware issue detected. Replace before continued use; ignoring may lead to loss of charging capability. Apple's on-device diagnostics can flag charge-port issues. Ignoring means eventual charging failure. Replacement is a straightforward repair — port assembly plus disassembly labor.
A tablet listing photo shows a small dent at the corner where the camera module sits.

Impact damage near camera assembly. Camera may be off-alignment or damaged internally. Test camera specifically before buying. Impact at camera-area is worse than impact elsewhere — camera modules are precisely aligned. Test-shoot before buying; check for asymmetric focus, dust in optics, autofocus lag.
A phone listing shows the ID label with an FCC ID '2ABCD-XXXXXX' — but the model number stamped elsewhere doesn't match Apple's naming convention.

Suspicious for counterfeit. Real iPhones have specific FCC IDs matching model numbers. Third-party FCC IDs suggest non-Apple manufacturer. Every FCC-approved device has a unique FCC ID. Apple's iPhones have Apple's FCC prefix (typically 'BCG'). Non-Apple FCC IDs on a device claimed to be iPhone = counterfeit indicator.
A laptop listing shows 'Recent battery replacement (Apple service)' in description with receipt photo.

Positive — recent Apple battery replacement means high remaining battery life plus verified genuine part. Adds value to older machine. Documented battery replacement with genuine Apple service is a strong value-add on an older machine. Verify receipt authenticity (Apple format). Adds maybe 15-30% to a machine's value depending on age.
A laptop listing shows the About screen with 'Serial Number Not Available.'

Suspicious. Serial number should always be available on genuine hardware. Possibly a logic board replacement not properly configured, or possibly a fake. 'Serial Number Not Available' shouldn't appear on genuine, properly-configured hardware. Occasionally happens after logic-board replacement if the serial wasn't programmed correctly. Also possible on some fakes. Verify with Apple support.
A phone listing photo shows the box's serial number matching the phone's IMEI but the phone's serial number in About is 'F2LX...' while the box shows a phone-serial starting with 'D7QW...'

Box-and-device don't match. Different phones. Either box swap (usually for value inflation) or seller error. Box and device serials must match. IMEI matches (from IMEI printed on both) but device serials differ = someone swapped devices between boxes. If seller can't explain, reject.
A phone shows 'This iPhone has been unlocked' notification on setup.

Carrier unlock message. Positive — carrier has released the phone for any network. Verify by IMEI lookup with carriers to confirm. Carrier unlock notification confirms the device is not carrier-locked. Verify by IMEI on carrier websites (each major carrier has a checker). Unlocked commands premium over locked.
A laptop listing shows 'MacBook Pro 16-inch M3 Max with 128GB unified memory.'

Top-tier configuration. Base 16-inch MBP has 16-36GB RAM options; 128GB is the max config. Priced very high; used market may still show significant premium. Max-config MBPs (128GB RAM, 4-8TB storage, M3 Max) are workstation-tier machines. Priced high new ($5000-7000+ depending on options). Even used, still command premium prices. Verify config from About screen.
A camera listing shows 'lightly used' but the shutter-count is 187,000 on a body rated for 150,000 shutter actuations.

Past rated shutter life. Shutter mechanism could fail at any time. 'Lightly used' claim contradicts data. Discount accordingly or reject. Shutters can and often do last past rated cycles, but wear accelerates past rating. Description inconsistency with data is a red flag. Consider price accordingly — shutter replacement varies $200-600 depending on body.
A drone listing photo shows the drone with a battery status LED showing 'Cell imbalance error.'

Battery pack has failing cell(s). Battery replacement required before flight. Cells can worsen — potentially safety issue. Lithium-polymer battery cell imbalance is a warning sign. Continuing to use can lead to full pack failure or fire. Replace immediately. Drone batteries are model-specific; genuine or high-quality third-party.
A phone listing shows 'Battery replaced by non-Apple service' in About screen.

Non-Apple battery replacement. Battery may work fine but iPhone shows 'genuine part unverified' warning. Health measurements may be unreliable. Non-Apple batteries trigger warnings on newer iPhones. Battery function usually fine but service metrics (health percentage) may be unreliable. Apple can replace with genuine and remove warnings.
A phone listing shows 'Screen replaced by non-Apple service' in Settings.

Aftermarket screen. May be OEM-quality or lower. Face ID may still work (with calibration) or may not. True Tone often disabled. Third-party screen replacement varies from OEM-quality parts and calibration (near-Apple experience) to cheap parts with limited feature support (True Tone off, brightness issues, no Face ID). Ask shop about quality.
A laptop system-info shows 'Boot Camp partition present.'

Intel Mac with Windows dual-boot capability. Only Intel Macs support Boot Camp; Apple Silicon does not. Dates the machine to pre-2020. Boot Camp = Apple's Intel Mac dual-boot Windows solution. Not supported on Apple Silicon. Presence dates machine to Intel era. Windows alternatives on Apple Silicon: Parallels Desktop, VMware Fusion, UTM (all virtualization, not native).
A phone listing shows 'Apple Intelligence compatible.'

Modern iPhone (16 series, or 15 Pro/Pro Max). Requires A17 Pro or A18/A18 Pro chip. Older iPhones cannot run Apple Intelligence. Apple Intelligence launched with iOS 18 (2024). Requires A17 Pro or newer chip. This limits AI-enabled iPhones to 15 Pro line and 16 series onwards. Standard 15 doesn't qualify.
A camera lens listing shows 'first-run production' in the description.

Marketing claim without specific meaning unless the lens had a known first-run vs later-run quality difference. Verify claim. First-run vs later-run production differences are real for some specific lenses/products (early production may have specific fixes or problems addressed later). Verify claim against known-issue databases. Sometimes 'first-run' is marketing fluff.
A lens listing shows 'made in Japan' vs 'made in Taiwan' — same model.

Some lens lines have Japan and Taiwan manufacturing variants. Japanese production sometimes commands slight premium but quality can be comparable. Certain lens lines (particular Sigma, Canon variants) have Japan and Taiwan (or other) manufacturing. Consumer perception often favors Japan; actual quality difference varies. Premium is 5-15% typically.
A monitor listing shows 'true 10-bit panel' vs '8-bit + FRC.'

True 10-bit is superior for professional color work. FRC (Frame Rate Control) uses temporal dithering to simulate 10-bit on an 8-bit panel — visible artifacting in some content. Panel color depth: 6-bit + FRC (budget), 8-bit + FRC = 'faux 10-bit' (mid), 10-bit true (professional). FRC uses temporal dithering — for critical color work, native 10-bit is preferred.
A camera listing shows 'PRND (Pre-capture Recording via NDL DRAM).'

Some modern high-end cameras support pre-capture — recording is held in buffer, and you save what happened BEFORE you pressed shutter. Sports/wildlife feature. Pre-capture (variously named per manufacturer — Pre-Burst, Pre-Capture, ProCapture) records a rolling buffer to memory. When you release shutter, it saves frames from before + after. Excellent for unpredictable action.
A laptop listing shows the About screen with 'Storage: 512GB APFS + 512GB APFS.'

Two storage volumes visible. Either two internal drives (rare in modern MBPs), or a partitioned single drive. Verify actual drive count. Modern MacBook Pros have soldered storage — no user-serviceable drives. Two APFS containers on one drive is common (Mac partition + backup or Windows via Boot Camp). Actual drive count matters for repair and upgrade calculations.
A phone listing shows 'Verizon-specific model' in the notes.

US carrier-model phones sometimes have carrier-specific features (network locking to Verizon CDMA/LTE bands historically). Newer phones (iPhone 12+) are universal. US carrier-specific iPhones existed pre-iPhone 12 due to CDMA vs GSM technology split. iPhone 12+ are universal. Verify with model number lookup — older Verizon-specific models may not work on other carriers without unlocking.
A laptop listing shows the About screen with 'This device is enrolled in a business/education organization.'

Device is DEP (Device Enrollment Program) enrolled. Wiping the device re-enrolls it automatically. Cannot escape without organization's removal. DEP enrollment (Apple's automatic enrollment system) is enterprise-managed. Even after wiping, device re-enrolls on setup and MDM re-applies. Only the enrolled organization can un-enroll. Effectively unusable personally.
A laptop listing shows 'Custom BIOS install' or 'custom firmware' in description.

Non-standard firmware. May be unable to update through normal channels. Consider carefully; some third-party firmware improves function, some breaks it. Custom firmware ('coreboot,' 'libreboot') exists for some laptops. Can add features, remove proprietary lockdown. But warranty voided, official updates may not apply. Assess before buying.
A phone listing shows 'jailbroken' in description.

iOS security restrictions removed. Reduces resale value. Some banking/gaming apps refuse to run. Not all buyers want this state. Jailbroken iPhones have modified iOS. Advantages: broader app selection, deeper customization. Disadvantages: banking apps often refuse, some enterprise apps refuse, warranty voided. Resale market is narrow. Discount 20-40%.
A camera lens listing shows 'oil on aperture blades' in description.

Aperture blades have oil migration from focus/aperture assembly. Slow aperture stopping — image inconsistency. Cleaning is a professional service. Aperture-oil migration is a common older-lens issue. Aperture blades stop slowly, giving inconsistent exposure. Cleaning restores function ($100-300 depending on lens); worth doing on higher-value lenses only.
A camera lens listing photo shows a small crack in the front element.

Front-element crack. Significant image quality reduction (flare, contrast, sharpness). Replacement front element is often more expensive than the lens's used value. Front-element cracks are usually not economically repairable — replacement front element cost approaches lens value. Small hairline crack in a very expensive lens might justify repair; small crack in a budget lens does not.
A laptop system-info shows 'CPU throttling: 68% average TDP over 5 minutes.'

Thermal throttling. CPU is running at 68% of rated power over sustained load. Fan or thermal paste issue; cleaning/paste replacement may help. Thermal throttling limits performance under sustained load. Common causes: dust in fans, dried thermal paste (5+ year old machines), fan degradation. Cleaning + repaste is a $50-150 service if DIY-comfortable, less if DIY. Restores full performance.
A phone listing photo shows 'This iPhone has been repaired with parts that cannot be verified as genuine Apple parts.'

Aftermarket repair. Function may be fine but Apple's parts verification shows non-genuine. May affect some features (True Tone, Face ID). Reduces resale value. The 'cannot be verified' warning on newer iPhones is Apple's parts-verification. Third-party parts (screen, battery, camera) trigger it. Function may still work — but resale value is reduced 10-30% depending on part.
A laptop listing photo shows the About screen with '3.5 GHz Quad-Core Intel Core i7' with 16GB RAM on a 2018 MacBook Pro.

2018 15-inch MacBook Pro (or similar) with i7 processor. Coffee Lake generation. About 7 years old at this writing. Still capable but slower than modern equivalents. 2018-2019 MacBook Pro (Intel) is now 6-7 years old. Still runs current macOS (as long as supported). Performance is adequate for many tasks; struggles with modern heavy workloads. Battery capacity a common concern by this age.
A phone listing shows 'iPhone SE (3rd generation)' as model.

iPhone SE 2022 — small-form-factor iPhone with A15 Bionic chip, home button, single camera. Latest SE model. iPhone SE 3rd generation (2022) has iPhone 8 form factor with modern A15 chip. Small, cheaper than flagship. Home button remains (last iPhone with one). Suitable for users wanting small phone.
A camera body photo shows a red stripe along the front handle.

Canon EOS 5D or 1D-series body (or similar) — Canon flagship/prosumer visual marker. Canon EOS 5D and 1D series bodies have red accent stripes. 5D = prosumer, 1D = flagship. Visual marker for higher-tier Canon DSLRs. Specific model requires body-shape assessment.
A camera body photo shows 'Nikon Z9' in the model badge.

Nikon Z9 flagship mirrorless. 45.7MP, 8K video, no mechanical shutter (electronic only). Professional sports/action tier. Nikon Z9 launched 2021 as Nikon's flagship mirrorless. No mechanical shutter — all-electronic for high speed. Integrated grip design like DSLR flagships. Direct competitor to Sony A1 and Canon R3/R5.
A laptop listing shows 'ARM64 Windows' in system info.

ARM-based Windows PC (Qualcomm Snapdragon X-based). Newer generation. Native ARM apps run best; x86 apps run via emulation. Windows on ARM (WoA) laptops became mainstream with Qualcomm Snapdragon X Elite/X Plus (2024). Native ARM apps run best; x86 apps via emulation. Better battery life than x86 typically. App compatibility improving but not universal.
A phone listing shows 'Refurbished by Apple' notice AND 'Serial number in warranty database.'

Verified Apple Refurbished device with active warranty. Highest tier of used phone. Priced accordingly. Apple Certified Refurbished carries 1-year Apple warranty. Priced typically 10-15% below new. Verify via checkcoverage.apple.com — device serial in warranty database confirms authenticity.
A MacBook Pro listing shows the About screen with 'macOS Ventura' running but the About shows M3 chip.

M3 chip supports macOS 14 (Sonoma) onward. macOS 13 Ventura shouldn't run on M3 natively. Verify — either older macOS installation predates upgrade, or something is unusual. macOS versions have hardware compatibility. M3 chips launched with macOS Sonoma (2023); Ventura (2022) shouldn't run natively on M3 hardware. Unusual — verify OS version and hardware match.
A camera listing photo shows the LCD screen with a small blue dot in the center that doesn't disappear when viewing different images.

Stuck pixel on the LCD. Cosmetic on the review screen — doesn't affect image capture. Cannot be fixed by software; hardware replacement only. LCD stuck pixels are cosmetic — they only affect the review/menu screen, not captured images. Rare on cameras compared to phones. Replacement is expensive relative to camera value.
A laptop listing photo shows the About screen with 'macOS 26 Beta' installed.

Beta OS install. Cannot use for critical work — beta OS can be unstable. Before selling as normal machine, best to reinstall stable OS. Beta macOS installations can have bugs, missing features, or compatibility issues. If purchasing, plan on clean OS reinstall. Sellers should typically remove beta before selling — indicates possibly technical seller.
A monitor listing shows 'IPS Black' as panel type.

Second-generation IPS with improved contrast ratio (2000:1 vs standard IPS 1000:1). LG-manufactured; premium tier IPS panel. IPS Black is an improved IPS variant with better contrast — a specific LG panel type. Not universally available; identifies premium tier monitors with high contrast requirements (photo editing, video production). Premium pricing.
A laptop listing shows the About screen with 'This Mac is signed into iCloud' and 'Find My Mac is enabled.'

Not fully prepared for sale — Find My Mac must be disabled by owner before erase to allow next user. If seller can't provide credentials, walk away. Find My Mac activation lock behaves like iCloud Activation Lock on iPhones. Sold devices with Find My Mac enabled can't be fully re-provisioned. Seller must disable via System Preferences before wiping.
A camera listing shows 'in-body dual card slots' and 'CFexpress + SD.'

Pro-tier camera with dual card slots. CFexpress (fast, expensive) + SD (universal, slower). Common in modern pro bodies. Redundant recording, burst capacity. Dual card slots enable redundant recording (write same file to both cards for safety) or overflow (fill one, then next) or file-splitting (RAW to one, JPEG to another). Pro-tier feature. CFexpress + SD is common modern config.
A laptop listing shows 'RAM 96GB soldered' in specs.

Non-user-upgradeable RAM. Modern MBP Max configurations reach 128GB. Verify config from About screen. Cannot upgrade later. Apple Silicon Macs have unified memory soldered to the SOC. Not upgradeable. RAM config is a one-time decision. 96GB is a mid-high config; typical M-Max range is 32-128GB depending on chip variant.
A phone listing shows 'iOS 26 beta' installed on iPhone 16.

Beta iOS install. Cannot use for critical apps (banking, some enterprise). Restore to stable iOS before serious use or resale. iOS beta has bugs, potential app incompatibilities. Restore to release iOS for critical use. Restoring from beta to release requires iTunes/Finder computer connection with IPSW file.
A camera listing shows 'stacked BSI CMOS' in sensor specs.

Modern high-end sensor architecture. Faster readout (better rolling shutter behavior), higher speed. Latest-generation flagship cameras. Stacked BSI CMOS = back-illuminated sensor with processing layer stacked underneath. Faster readout speeds enable higher burst rates and better rolling-shutter behavior. Latest-generation feature.
A laptop listing shows the About screen with 'macOS Recovery available.'

System has recovery partition available. Not remarkable — modern Macs always have this. Normal state. macOS Recovery is standard. Boot with Command+R (Intel) or hold power button (Apple Silicon) to access. Enables reinstall, disk repair, firmware password. Presence is normal, not a special feature.
A phone listing shows 'Face ID has been reset' in Settings.

Face ID data cleared. Previous face data removed. New user can enroll their face without conflict. Positive sign — seller prepared properly. Resetting Face ID data (Settings > Face ID > Reset Face ID) removes stored face data. Should be done before resale. Along with iCloud sign-out, iMessage deregistration, and factory erase — standard resale preparation.
A camera listing shows 'PRE ULTRA' in the model name (fictional but common structure).

Marketing suffix. Doesn't correspond to a hardware specification alone — verify actual capabilities from real specs. Marketing model names include suffixes that may or may not correspond to hardware differences. Verify actual specs from data sheet, not from name alone. 'Pro,' 'Ultra,' 'Max,' 'Elite' etc. vary in real meaning by brand.
A gaming console listing shows a heavy-modded external cooler attachment.

Aftermarket cooling. May indicate the seller had thermal issues (leading to modding) OR is an enthusiast preferring cooler operation. Ask about original thermal history. Aftermarket console cooling accessories exist. Some users prefer them for noise reduction or thermal management. May suggest previous thermal issues (leading to modding). Ask about history.
A laptop listing shows the About screen with 'Boot Camp Windows partition disabled' warning.

Boot Camp partition still exists but Boot Camp assistant hasn't been used to remove it properly. Some disk space is inaccessible; needs proper cleanup. Boot Camp partitions must be removed via Boot Camp Assistant to properly reclaim disk space. Direct partition deletion via Disk Utility leaves the partition table in an inconsistent state. Reformat may be needed.
A phone listing shows 'This iPhone can be tracked and locked remotely by owner' in the initial setup screen.

Activation Lock active. Cannot bypass. Contact seller for iCloud removal or reject. Setup screen showing Activation Lock message means device is Apple-ID-locked. Only signed-in Apple ID with password can remove. Once removed by owner (from iCloud.com > Find Devices), device shows Setup Assistant clean.
A phone listing shows 'Enterprise MDM enrollment' in initial setup.

DEP-enrolled by enterprise. Cannot be un-enrolled by user. Effectively unusable personally. Automatic Device Enrollment (DEP) applies MDM configuration when device connects to internet during setup. Only the enrolled organization can remove. Deregistering requires their action. Unusable personally.
A laptop listing shows a specific serial number that Apple's warranty database returns 'End-of-life' status.

Apple has retired the model from service. Battery replacements may not be available at Apple. Third-party service available; parts availability variable. 'Vintage' means limited service (5-7 years old). 'Obsolete' means no Apple service (7+ years old). Third-party service can still handle common repairs. Parts availability depends on model popularity.
A camera lens listing shows a small chip on the mount side.

Mount damage. Can affect mounting security and communication with body. Attempted mounting may damage body's mount ring. Repair by lens service center. Lens mount damage is more serious than optical damage — can affect body compatibility, focus accuracy, and physical security of mounting. Have lens serviced before mounting on valuable body.
A laptop listing photo shows the SSD-info screen with 'Wear Level: 90% remaining.'

Solid SSD health. 90% remaining is well within useful life. NVMe SSDs typically show 100% new; slow decline over years. SSD wear-level indicates remaining write endurance. Modern NVMe SSDs typically rated for large write endurance (hundreds of TBW). 90% remaining is essentially new. Below 50% consider replacement; below 20% imminent replacement.
A drone listing photo shows the propeller motors with slight vertical play when pressed by finger.

Motor bearing wear or damage. Affects flight stability. Motor replacement or service needed. Drone motors should have minimal vertical play. Any noticeable movement indicates bearing wear or damage. Affects hover stability and can lead to failure in flight. Motor replacement is a straightforward repair on prosumer drones.
A camera lens listing shows 'AF-D' in the model name (Nikon).

Older Nikon AF lens with autofocus via body's mechanical screw drive. Doesn't work on Nikon Z-series or bodies without focus motor (D40, D60, D3xxx, D5xxx). Nikon AF (screw-drive) lenses require body-driven focus. Only Nikon bodies with a focus motor can autofocus. Lower-end D40/D60/D3xxx/D5xxx bodies can only manual-focus AF-D lenses. AF-S (with lens-internal motor) works everywhere.
A phone listing shows 'This iPhone has been updated to iOS 19' — but the phone is iPhone 8.

iPhone 8 hardware doesn't support iOS 19 officially. Either seller misstated (probably iOS 16 max) or the phone is unofficially patched. Verify version. iOS version compatibility: iPhone 8 officially caps at iOS 16 (with security updates continuing). Claims of newer iOS versions on older hardware are either seller error or unofficial patches. Verify via About screen.
A camera listing shows a shutter count that exactly matches a common milestone (10,000, 50,000, 100,000).

Coincidence unlikely for real shutter counts. Suspicious of shutter-count reset or model-year inflation. Verify with independent count tool. Shutter counts should be arbitrary numbers. Suspiciously round milestones may indicate manual reset (some third-party tools can reset shutter counts) or false reporting. Verify with independent shutter-count read utility.
A monitor listing shows 'Delta-E < 2 factory calibration certificate.'

Color-accurate monitor with individual factory calibration report. Professional tier for color-sensitive work (photo editing, video color grading). Delta-E measures color accuracy. Delta-E < 2 is imperceptible to most viewers; < 1 is high-end professional. Individual calibration certificates are premium tier — factory measures the specific unit before shipping.
A laptop listing shows 'refurbished by manufacturer' with 1-year manufacturer warranty.

Manufacturer refurbished with warranty. Highest confidence in refurb quality. Priced typically 10-20% below new equivalent. Manufacturer refurbished (by the OEM) with warranty is the highest-confidence refurbished tier. Devices are typically fully inspected, tested, and warrantied like new. Cheaper than new; higher quality than generic refurbished.
A phone listing shows 'Enterprise unlock' as a feature.

Enterprise-provisioned phones sometimes have specific unlock states — the phone is unlocked but still enterprise-provisioned. May have limitations vs consumer unlock. 'Enterprise unlock' is an unclear marketing term. Verify what specifically is unlocked (carrier vs MDM vs both) and what restrictions might remain. Ask specific questions.
A camera lens listing shows 'radioactive Thorium glass' in the description.

Older lens (1950s-1980s) with Thorium glass in optics. Slightly radioactive; not dangerous to normal use. Yellowing over time is a known feature. Thorium glass was used in some 1950s-1980s lenses for high refractive index. Slight radioactivity; not dangerous to owner (external contact is fine). Yellowing over decades is a known trait; UV exposure can reverse. Collector interest exists.
A phone listing shows the initial-setup screen with a specific language pre-loaded (Chinese, Korean, Japanese).

Region-specific device. Language can be changed, but some features are region-locked (payment methods, some app availability, App Store region). Region-specific iPhones are locked to that region's App Store for certain purposes. Sold-in-Japan iPhones often can't disable shutter sound. Sold-in-China iPhones don't have FaceTime audio (China regulatory). Some features are permanent region-based.
A laptop listing shows 'IceBerg system' or 'liquid metal thermal interface' in description.

Advanced thermal management. Liquid metal thermal interface is more thermally conductive than paste but requires careful application. High-performance gaming/workstation tier. Liquid metal thermal interface material (LMTIM) is a gallium-based alloy with far higher thermal conductivity than paste. Used in high-end applications. Requires special application to prevent damage (can short components, corrode aluminum). Premium tier.
A phone listing shows 'ProMotion 120Hz' in specs.

High-refresh-rate display. Standard on iPhone 13 Pro/14 Pro/15 Pro/16 Pro line. Regular iPhones remain at 60Hz. ProMotion is Apple's 120Hz variable-refresh-rate display technology. Only on Pro-tier iPhones (13 Pro and later) and iPad Pro (2017 and later). Adaptive up to 120Hz for smooth scrolling, down to 10Hz for battery savings.
A laptop listing shows 'Mini-LED' in display specs.

Mini-LED backlit LCD display. Between LCD and OLED in image quality. High peak brightness, thousands of local dimming zones. Used on MacBook Pro (2021+) and other premium laptops. Mini-LED = LCD with mini-LED backlight. Between traditional LCD and OLED in image quality. Much better contrast than plain LCD due to local dimming; higher peak brightness than OLED. No burn-in risk. MacBook Pro 14/16-inch (2021+) uses this.
A phone listing shows 'Type-C USB 2.0 only' in port specs.

USB-C port with USB 2.0 speeds only. Slower than USB 3.x. Common on standard iPhone 15 (Pro models have USB 3.x). iPhone 15 standard has USB-C port limited to USB 2.0 speeds (480 Mbps). iPhone 15 Pro/Pro Max have USB 3 speeds (10 Gbps) — differentiated by USB spec, not port shape. Matters for external drive transfers.
A laptop listing shows the About screen with '96GB unified memory + Neural Engine.'

M-series high configuration with unified architecture. Unified memory shared between CPU, GPU, and Neural Engine. Efficient for AI/ML workloads. Apple Silicon uses unified memory architecture — CPU, GPU, and Neural Engine all address the same memory pool. Advantage for AI workloads (can allocate large models to GPU/NE without discrete VRAM limits). Disadvantage: RAM is fixed at purchase.
A camera listing photo shows a small green LED near the shutter button that's staying on.

Card-access LED. Camera is writing to card. Do not remove card until LED off. Cameras usually block card removal via door interlock. Green LED near shutter = card-access indicator. Removing card during writing risks data corruption. Wait for LED to extinguish. If LED persists longer than expected, could indicate card issue or camera issue.
A phone listing shows 'MagSafe compatible' as a feature.

Modern iPhone with MagSafe magnetic ring. iPhone 12+ (2020 onward). Enables MagSafe accessories: wireless charger, mounts, wallets. MagSafe on iPhone launched with iPhone 12 (2020). Magnetic array in the back for accessories. Wireless charging faster with MagSafe (15W) than standard Qi (7.5W). Extensive accessory ecosystem.
A laptop listing shows the About screen with 'Apple Silicon M3, 8GB, 256GB' — priced at $600.

Base MacBook Pro M3 configuration used, at 60-70% of new price. Reasonable used price. Verify all functions before purchase. Base MacBook Pro M3 was ~$1600 new. $600 used = 62% depreciation. Verify all functions: battery health, screen (no burn-in — LCDs don't burn in but check for stuck pixels), all ports, keyboard, camera, speakers, WiFi/Bluetooth.

Super 100s/120s/150s = fineness of wool fiber (higher = finer)

what's chambray?

what's denim?

what's raw-denim?

what's sanforized?

what's reactive-dye?

what's aniline-dye?

what's vegetable-tanned?

what's chrome-tanned?

what's full-grain-leather?

what's top-grain-leather?

what's corrected-grain?

what's tuxedo?

what's cardigan?

flat-felled seam — durable construction, denim and workwear standard
i see: seam with frayed edges serged over

i see: seam fully encased in fabric tube
French seam — high-quality construction, delicate fabrics

i see: hem with visible cover stitch

A jacket label reads '100% wool.' The interior lining feels shiny and slightly plasticky.
Wool exterior with polyester lining. Common mid-tier construction. Wool + polyester lining is the mid-tier convention. Not luxury but not budget. Premium tier uses Bemberg/cupro lining.
A shirt label reads '100% cotton.' The fabric wrinkles heavily when creased.

Consistent — natural cotton wrinkles. Non-wrinkle claim on 100% cotton usually means resin treatment. Cotton naturally wrinkles. Non-iron cotton has resin treatment which alters the hand and can trigger allergies. Untreated cotton is more comfortable but requires ironing.
A sweater label reads '100% cashmere.' The fabric feels warm but coarse.

Suspicious — real cashmere is exceptionally soft. Coarse feel suggests wool blend or lower-grade fiber. Cashmere quality varies. High-grade cashmere is butter-soft; low-grade is scratchy and pills easily. Some 'cashmere' is heavily blended or mislabeled.
A T-shirt label reads '95% cotton, 5% spandex.' Fabric stretches and recovers.

Cotton-blend with spandex for stretch and recovery. Modern casual T-shirt standard. Small percentages of spandex (2-5%) add stretch to cotton knits without changing fabric appearance. Standard modern T-shirt and polo construction.
A jean label reads '100% cotton' with visible white edge along the inseam.

Selvage denim — 100% cotton, shuttle-loom construction. Premium denim tier. Selvage denim has finished (non-serged) edges on the fabric, visible at the inseam cuff. Slower shuttle-loom weaving. Premium tier — usually $150+ retail.
A jacket buttons are heavy for their size, with subtle variation in the surface pattern.

Real horn buttons — traditional high-quality tailored garment marker. Horn buttons show natural variation and have some weight. Plastic buttons are uniform and light. Real horn is a mid-to-high quality garment signal.
A jacket buttons are shiny plastic, uniform, and light.

Plastic buttons — mass-market construction. Doesn't necessarily mean bad, but marker of lower price tier. Plastic buttons are cheap and durable. Standard on inexpensive garments. Some brand-name garments use plastic buttons too; it's one signal among many.
A jacket button shows iridescent white with color-shifting pearlescent surface.

Mother-of-pearl (shell) button — mid-to-high quality garment marker. Mother-of-pearl buttons show natural iridescence from shell. Weight is between plastic and horn. High-quality shirt/blouse button standard.
A jacket lapel shows tiny stitching along the edge, slightly irregular.

Pick-stitching — hand-worked detail. Bespoke or high-quality ready-to-wear marker. Pick-stitching (also called pickstitching) is a decorative hand-worked edge detail on jackets. Traditional bespoke marker. Some high-end RTW also has it. Modern faux pick-stitching is machine-made.
A jacket sleeve buttons have real buttonholes that open.

Surgeon cuffs — mid-tier and up tailored jacket feature. Functional (working) buttonholes on jacket sleeves = surgeon cuffs. Original medical-tradition detail. Mid-tier and up. Cannot alter sleeve length without disturbing the buttons.
A jacket sleeve buttons are decorative — no functional buttonholes.

Sham cuffs — standard mass-market construction. Sleeve length can be altered without disturbing buttons. Decorative (non-functional) sleeve buttons are standard on mass-market suits/blazers. Practical for alteration. Sleeves can be shortened significantly without visible button issue.
A jacket interior label reads 'Fully Canvassed.'

Full canvas construction — highest tier suit/blazer construction. Priced accordingly. Full canvas = internal chest canvas hand-shaped to the jacket. Highest tier. Half-canvas is next. Fused is bottom tier. Fully canvased is a valuable claim; verify by feeling the chest — canvas has different drape than fused.
A trouser label reads '90% wool, 10% cashmere.'

Wool-cashmere blend. Premium trouser fabric. Warm and soft; drapes well. Wool-cashmere blends are common in premium trousers. Cashmere adds softness; wool adds durability. Higher-end blends push cashmere to 20% or higher.
A dress label reads '100% silk.' Fabric is cool to the touch, slightly shiny.

Silk garment. Requires hand-wash or dry-clean care. Value depends on silk quality (mulberry, tussah, or blends). Silk is cool to touch, has slight sheen, drapes beautifully. Care required — most silk is dry-clean-only. Different silks have very different properties (mulberry = smooth uniform, tussah = coarser variable, dupioni = slubs).
A knit sweater has visible cable pattern running vertically.

Cable-knit sweater. Traditional knit pattern. Aran-tradition Irish knit if intricate; simple cables are commodity. Cable patterns are traditional knit design. Complex Aran patterns are Irish tradition; simple cables are widely produced. Hand-knit cables have slight asymmetry; machine cables are uniform.
A knit sweater feels warm and slightly fuzzy, but fabric is very lightweight.

Consistent with cashmere or fine merino. Lightweight + warmth is the cashmere signature. Cashmere's key property is warmth-to-weight ratio. Fine merino also has this trait. Heavy scratchy wool warmth-to-weight is much lower. Feel the difference; test with label.
A shoe with all-leather uppers and stitched (not glued) sole.

Goodyear welt or Blake stitch construction. Repairable, higher tier shoe. Stitched-sole shoes (Goodyear welt, Blake stitch) can be resoled. Glued (cemented) shoes cannot be economically resoled. Stitched construction = higher tier + longer usable life.
A shoe with glued (cemented) sole and synthetic uppers.

Mass-market shoe. Cannot be resoled. Life ends when sole wears out. Cemented construction is the mass-market standard. Cannot economically resole. Shoe life is finite. Not necessarily bad — modern comfort shoes use this construction — but not repairable.
A handbag has a small metal plaque with brand engraved inside.

Brand-marker plaque. Verify authenticity — plaques are counterfeited. Genuine plaques have specific fonts, spacing, positioning. Brand plaques on handbags are common. Also commonly counterfeited. Genuine plaques have precise engraving, specific font, correct placement, matching finish. Reference-photograph before buying.
A handbag lining is soft-nap fabric with brand pattern woven in.

Fabric lining. Verify pattern is genuine — repeat exactly matches brand's specification. Branded lining patterns are counterfeited. Genuine patterns have precise repeats, correct colors, correct scale. Verify against reference photos.
A dress shirt collar shows a soft interlining when the collar folds.

Fused collar — mass-market standard construction. Fused collars use adhesive interlining. Standard on most shirts. Premium shirts use canvas interlining or unfused construction. Both work; canvas lasts longer and drapes better over decades.
A shirt collar shows two distinct fabric layers when folded, no adhesive interlining.
Unfused (French) construction — premium shirt tier. Unfused collars use fabric interlining without adhesive. Softer drape, longer life, no risk of bubbling after washes. Premium shirt tier.
A dress shirt cuff has plastic collar stays inside.

Removable collar stays for collar shape. Standard on dress shirts. Collar stays are small plastic or metal inserts that keep collar points crisp. Removable for washing. Standard on formal shirts. Higher-tier uses brass or mother-of-pearl stays.
A jacket has an interior label reading 'Made in Italy' with detailed brand markings.

Italian-made garment. Cross-verify brand authenticity — Italian-manufacture doesn't authenticate brand alone. Country-of-origin labels are legally required. 'Made in Italy' is a quality marker but doesn't authenticate brand. Verify all authentication signals for luxury pieces.
A knit garment label reads '100% polyester' but feels like wool.

Modern engineered polyester. Some synthetics closely mimic natural fibers. Care much easier than wool; less warmth-to-weight. Modern polyester engineering closely mimics wool hand. Care much easier (machine washable). Warmth-to-weight lower than real wool. Environmental concerns (microplastic shedding).
A pair of jeans has fading concentrated at thighs, back pockets, and behind knees.

Natural wear-fading — organic pattern from actual wear. Natural denim fade patterns come from actual wear points — legs sitting, wallet in back pocket, knees bending. Not counterfeit-able easily; distinctive of long-worn jeans.
A pair of jeans has extreme fading in perfect symmetrical patterns — factory-produced look.

Distressed/pre-faded denim. Factory-produced wear pattern. Marketing aesthetic, not real wear. Distressed denim is factory-produced to look worn. Symmetrical fade patterns give it away vs organic natural fade. Both are legitimate styles; buyer preference varies.
A wool coat has small round holes in random spots.

Moth damage. Very common in stored wool. Small holes repairable by reweaving; large or numerous holes reduce value significantly. Moth larvae eat wool fibers. Small round holes are the signature. Prevention: cedar, lavender, sealed storage. Repair: professional reweaving ($30-100 per hole) or invisible mending.
A wool coat's under-arm area has yellow discoloration.

Sweat and antiperspirant stain. Often permanent. Discount heavily; some improvement possible with specialized cleaning. Underarm staining is a stubborn permanent-appearing damage. Specialized cleaning may reduce (not eliminate). Consider practical wearability — dark colors hide better; light colors show it clearly.
A dress has a small pull in the fabric where a snag has run.

Snag or pull — thread pulled out from weave. Sometimes fixable with careful thread-pulling technique; sometimes visible after repair. Snags happen on knits and delicate weaves. Pull the loose thread through to the wrong side, gentle stretching sometimes recovers. Visible after repair on some fabrics; nearly invisible on others.
A shirt has a small hole with slightly frayed edges near the underarm seam.

Stress hole from wear at high-stress point. Common on well-worn shirts. Reparable with darn or patch. Stress holes appear at wear points — underarms, elbows, shoulders. Reparable with darning or patches for casual garments; less practical for dress shirts.
A wool sweater has small pilling balls on the surface where sleeves rub against sides.

Standard pilling from wear. Removable with fabric shaver or pilling comb. Pilling occurs when short fibers work loose and tangle. High-friction areas pill first. Can be removed with fabric shavers, sweater stones, or pilling combs. Long-staple wools pill less than short-staple.
A jacket lapel has a small stain that appears greasy.

Grease stain. Depending on age and how set, may be difficult to remove. Dry cleaner assessment recommended. Grease stains benefit from prompt treatment. Aged grease stains are harder to remove. Home treatments (dish soap, cornstarch, talc) sometimes work. Dry cleaner has more options.
A leather bag has a scratch on the surface exposing lighter-colored leather underneath.

Full-grain leather with dyed surface. Scratch shows undyed color. Can be conditioned/re-dyed by leather-repair professional. Surface dye on full-grain leather scratches to expose the natural leather color. Repair options: leather conditioner (may darken), leather dye (color-match required), professional refinishing. Scratches add character on some bags (patina).
A dress shirt has yellowing along the front placket where buttons attach.

Age discoloration or previous cleaning residue. Some improvement possible with oxygen-based cleaning; some permanent. Yellowing on white shirts is common in older or heavily-washed pieces. Chlorine residue from bleach can cause. Oxygen-based cleaners sometimes improve; permanent yellow may not respond.
A coat has one button missing but the button-thread is visible.

Button off. Save the thread anchor — attach a matching button. Original buttons may be available from garment maker or resale. Missing buttons are common minor issues. Match remaining buttons; some brands sell replacement buttons. Sometimes an interior spare button is sewn behind the hem or lining.
A pair of jeans has a hole with fraying edges near the knee.

Wear hole from knee stress. Reparable with patch or reinforcement stitching for casual jeans; some prefer distressed look. Knee-hole in jeans is a wear pattern. Can be repaired (denim patches, decorative mending) or left as-is depending on style. Some brands sell 'distressed' denim with intentional holes at this location.
A shirt collar shows fraying at the fold.

Collar wear from repeated folding at the same crease. Generally not economically reparable on dress shirts. Collar wear at the fold is common on long-worn shirts. Repair (collar swap or turn) is possible but not economical on affordable shirts. Higher-end shirts sometimes justify collar replacement.
A silk scarf has small water spots that dried leaving rings.

Water damage on silk. Sometimes ironable out; sometimes permanent water rings. Professional silk-cleaner assessment. Silk shows water spots because minerals in water can leave rings. Some spots iron out; some are permanent. Silk is dry-clean-only usually; water contact should be avoided.
A wool jacket smells strongly of mothballs when unfolded.

Storage smell — mothball preservation. Airs out over weeks. Not permanent damage; discount modestly. Mothball smell in wool garments is common from long-term storage. Airing out (outdoor, no direct sun) for days to weeks removes it. Some vintage sellers specialize in removing storage smells.
A garment smells strongly of cigarette smoke.

Smoke-exposure damage. May require multiple cleanings; sometimes permanent. Significant discount. Smoke smell in garments is difficult to remove. Ozone treatment sometimes works; multiple washings; vinegar rinse. Some smells are permanent. Non-smoking buyers usually reject.
A dress has an alteration mark — the hem has been let out with visible original crease.

Alteration visible. Some buyers accept; some don't. Discount modestly if visible; more if the alteration is professional. Alteration marks (visible original hem crease, extra stitch holes) are common on garments that were hemmed and let out. Professional alterations minimize; DIY often shows. Common on children's clothes and vintage garments.
A jacket size label reads '42R.'

US menswear sizing: 42 chest, regular length. Standard notation. Sizing convention: chest measurement + length (S/R/L/XL). US menswear sizing includes chest and length codes. Sizes 34-52+ are common. Length codes: Short (S), Regular (R), Long (L), Extra Long (XL). European sizing uses different numbers.
A dress label reads 'Size 8 US / 12 UK / 40 EU.'

Cross-references sizes across US/UK/EU standards. Same garment, different regional numbers. Women's dress sizing varies by region. US 8 = UK 12 = EU 40. Some labels list multiple; some don't. Vanity sizing (numbers decreasing over decades) makes historical size comparisons unreliable.
A jacket brand label reads 'Made in Bangladesh.'

Bangladeshi manufacturing. Not a brand statement; production origin. Bangladesh is a major garment-manufacturing country. Country-of-origin labels are legally required. Bangladesh, Vietnam, China, Turkey, India are major manufacturing origins. Doesn't authenticate brand alone; combines with other signals.
A jacket brand label has a small 'RN' number followed by digits.

US textile regulation identifier. RN = Registered Number identifying the responsible company. Verifiable in US FTC database. US textile products must display RN (Registered Number) or WPL (Wool Products Label) number identifying the responsible company. Lookup on US FTC website. Doesn't authenticate brand but verifies the party responsible for compliance.
A silk tie label reads 'Made in Italy — 100% Silk.'

Italian-made silk tie. Verify by construction quality (7-fold, hand-rolled edges) and typography of label. Italian silk tie manufacturing is a quality benchmark. Construction (fold count, hand-rolled edges, tipping fabric quality) distinguishes tiers. Label typography helps verify brand authenticity.
A tie label reads 'Hand-Rolled.'

Higher-quality tie construction. Hand-rolled edges have softer drape than machine-sewn edges. Marker of tier. Hand-rolled tie edges are a traditional quality marker. Softer roll, better drape, less bulk. Machine-rolled is functional; hand-rolled is craft-tier.
A tie label reads '7-Fold.'

Premium tie construction — silk folded 7 times to make the tie body without interlining. Heaviest, most luxurious tier. 7-fold ties use only silk, folded 7 times to form the tie body — no interlining. Heaviest, thickest, most luxurious tier. 3-fold or 4-fold with interlining is standard.
A T-shirt has a printed graphic that has cracked along fold lines.

Print aging or heavy wear. Printed graphics crack over years of washing. Cannot be repaired; part of used-T-shirt aesthetic for some. Printed T-shirt graphics crack with washing and wear. Vintage T-shirt collectors sometimes value the cracking as authenticity marker. Modern buyers usually see it as damage.
A hoodie has extreme pilling in the underarm area.

Wear pattern. Underarm pilling is a high-friction wear point. Removable with pilling shaver; cosmetic only. Underarm pilling is common on hoodies and sweaters. Pilling shavers restore appearance. Doesn't indicate garment failure; long-staple fibers pill less over life.
A dress shirt has a discreet contrasting-color thread stitched into the collar underside.

Brand-signature detail. Some brands use hidden contrasting-color stitching as a subtle marker. Verify against reference photos for brand. Brand-signature details (contrasting-thread stitching, small labels, gauntlet button details) are subtle authentication markers. Not universal; brand-specific. Reference photos help identify.
A wool sweater label reads '100% Cashmere — Made in China.'

Chinese-made cashmere. Not automatically lower quality; some fine Chinese cashmere. Verify by hand feel and pilling test. China produces cashmere at all quality tiers — from mass-market blended-and-mislabeled to fine studio-grade. Quality within Chinese cashmere varies enormously. Feel and label together.
A pair of shoes has visible glue seepage around the sole-upper joint.

Cemented (glued) construction. Standard on affordable footwear. Cannot be resoled economically. Glue seepage indicates cemented sole. Standard on inexpensive shoes. Cannot be resoled. Life ends when sole wears or separates.
A pair of shoes has a visible ridge of stitching around the perimeter where sole meets upper.

Goodyear welt construction. Repairable — resolable multiple times. Higher tier shoe. Goodyear welt = strip of leather ('welt') stitched between upper and outsole, allowing sole replacement without disturbing upper. Highest tier construction. Restorable multiple times.
A pair of shoes has stitching hidden between sole layers.

Blake stitch construction. Sole-through-insole stitching. More flexible, less waterproof than Goodyear welt. Repairable. Blake stitch runs through insole and sole. Flexible, thinner shoe profile. Repairable (special Blake stitch machine required at cobbler). More common in Italian shoemaking.
A handbag has hardware (buckles, closures) that magnet-sticks.

Steel or steel-plated hardware. Common on mid-tier bags. Brass or genuine metal is non-magnetic. Higher-tier handbags use solid brass hardware (non-magnetic). Mid-tier uses steel or steel with plating (magnetic). Not always a value differentiator; some brands use both.
A dress shirt has a placket that is fabric-covered.

Fly-front (French placket) shirt. Formal-style. Buttons hidden behind fabric flap. Higher formality tier. Fly-front (concealed placket) shirts hide the button placket behind a fabric flap. Formal-style, typical of dress shirts. More common on modern shirts than vintage.
A dress shirt has a placket with visible buttons but no separate fabric flap.

Standard front-placket shirt. Casual to business style. Buttons visible. Standard front placket has buttons visible. Casual to business shirts. Fabric flap either present (French placket) or absent (visible buttons). Standard style.
A wool coat lining shows a printed brand pattern repeated.

Branded lining pattern. Verify pattern accuracy against reference. Counterfeit-friendly detail — genuine pattern is precise. Branded lining patterns (e.g., house tartan for coat brands) are counterfeited. Genuine patterns match brand specifications precisely — repeat, colors, scale. Verify.
A jacket lapel is exactly 3.5 inches wide.

Standard classic lapel width. Timeless proportions, not fashion-era-specific. Lapel widths trend with fashion eras. 3-3.5 inches = classic. Under 2.5 inches = skinny (2005-2015 trend). Over 4 inches = 1970s or wide-lapel trend (2019-onward).
A jacket lapel is 2 inches wide.

Skinny lapel — 2005-2015 fashion era. Now dated. Priced accordingly on resale. Skinny lapels peaked around 2010. Current fashion (2020s) has returned to wider lapels. Skinny-lapel suits look dated; resale value affected. Some buyers still prefer for their aesthetic.
A jacket lapel is 4.5 inches wide.

Wide lapel — 1970s-1980s or modern wide-lapel revival (2019+). Wide lapels peaked in 1970s. Currently returning to fashion (2019+ trend). Dating by lapel width requires cross-reference to other era markers (fabric, cut, construction).
A jacket has notched lapels.

Notch lapel — most common lapel style. Casual to business formal. Notch lapels are the standard for business suits, sport coats, and blazers. Not formal enough for tuxedos (which use peak or shawl). Timeless choice.
A jacket has peaked lapels pointing sharply upward.

Peak lapel — formal or slightly more elegant business style. Tuxedos, double-breasted jackets. Peak lapels are more formal than notch — traditional on tuxedos and double-breasted suits. Modern single-breasted suits with peak lapels are a slight upgrade from notch styling.
A jacket has shawl lapels — smooth curve, no notch or peak.

Shawl lapel — formal, typical of tuxedos and smoking jackets. Shawl collar/lapel is a smooth continuous curve. Traditional for tuxedos, smoking jackets, and dinner jackets. Elegant, evening-wear-focused.
A jacket buttons open double-breasted (6-on-2 pattern).

Double-breasted jacket. Classic 6x2 button pattern common. More formal than single-breasted; British/naval-influenced. Double-breasted button patterns: 6x2 (six buttons total, two-button closure), 6x1, 4x1, 4x2. Higher formality than single-breasted. Currently trending after 2015+.
A wool suit has a slight sheen when tilted under light.

Worsted wool. Standard business suit fabric. Sheen from tight worsted weave. Worsted wool has slight sheen from tightly-twisted long-staple wool yarns. Standard business suit fabric. Warmer worsted has less sheen; lightweight has more.
A wool suit has visible slubs and rough texture.

Woolen (as opposed to worsted). Rougher texture, more casual. Tweed and flannel are examples. Woolen (woolen-spun) uses shorter staple wool, resulting in rougher texture. Not less warm; often warmer. Common in tweeds, flannels, and country cloths.
A shirt has visible pinstripes in a very fine pattern.

Pinstripe shirt. Common formal-business fabric. Distinctive of banker-tradition. Pinstripe patterns come in many scales. Fine (banker) pinstripes are subtle; chalk stripes are wider and often on darker fabric. Both formal-business tradition.
A wool label reads 'Super 120s.'

Wool fiber fineness — 120s = 18.75 micron average. Fine tier suitable for lightweight fine suits. Super numbers grade wool fineness. Super 100s = 19.5 micron. Super 120s = 18.75. Super 150s = 17.5. Super 180s = 16.5. Higher numbers = finer + softer + more delicate. Everyday wear tier stops around Super 130s.
A wool label reads 'Super 180s.'

Very fine wool — 16.5 micron. Delicate. High-end tier but wears out faster than lower super numbers. Super 180s is very fine wool — luxurious hand but delicate. Wrinkles more, wears faster, requires careful maintenance. Ideal for special occasions rather than daily wear.
A jacket label reads 'Loro Piana Fabric.'

Premium Italian wool mill fabric. Not brand of jacket, but fabric supplier. Adds to value if the jacket is well-constructed. Loro Piana is a premium Italian wool mill (also a garment brand). Their fabrics are used by many maker-brands. Combined with premium construction = highest tier. Fabric alone doesn't authenticate the whole garment.
A cashmere label reads '2-ply.'

Cashmere ply count — 2 yarns twisted together. Stronger, more durable than single-ply. Standard for quality knitwear. Cashmere ply count: single-ply (loose weave, light), 2-ply (standard quality), 4-ply and higher (heavier, more durable, luxury). Higher ply = better durability.
A cashmere label reads '10-ply.'

Very heavy cashmere knit. Luxury tier — dense, warm, expensive. Rare in most price ranges. 10-ply cashmere is extremely luxurious — dense knit, exceptional warmth and hand. Correspondingly heavy and expensive. Rare in commercial market outside luxury brands.
A jacket label reads 'Made in Italy, Naples region.'

Neapolitan tailoring tradition. Soft-shouldered construction, minimal padding. Traditional Neapolitan makers include several premium tailoring houses. Neapolitan tailoring is a specific tradition — soft shoulders, unstructured chest, comfortable drape. Distinct from British (structured), American (moderate structure). Some premium tailoring houses base in Naples region.
A jacket has minimal shoulder padding — soft draped shoulder line.

Neapolitan or unstructured tailoring. Casual to smart-casual style. Comfortable to wear; less formal appearance. Shoulder structure: British (heavily structured, pronounced shoulder), American (moderate), Neapolitan (unstructured, soft). Choice affects formality and comfort. Modern casual trend favors softer shoulders.
A jacket has heavily structured shoulders with distinct roped seam at sleeve head.

British/Savile Row-influenced tailoring. Formal, structured, traditional. Higher formality than soft-shouldered. Roped shoulder (with pronounced seam ridge) is British tradition. Structured chest, padded shoulders. Formal traditional style. Suitable for formal occasions and city business dress.
A jacket collar shows a small triangle of contrasting fabric visible at the collar underside.

Boutonnière loop or brand-marker detail. Some brands use color-marker as authenticity signal. Some tailoring brands include distinct color-marker details as brand signatures — boutonnière loops, contrasting-thread stitches, small labels. Verify against brand references.
A trouser has a Milanese buttonhole (rolled edge, keyhole shape).

Traditional high-quality buttonhole style. Rolled edge, keyhole (round + slit). Modernized in some contemporary tailoring. Milanese buttonhole is a specific traditional style — rolled edge, keyhole shape. Hand-worked traditionally; machine-worked in modern equivalents. Higher quality garment marker.
A suit has one working buttonhole on the lapel but the button is missing.

Buttonhole for boutonnière (flower). Traditional detail. Genuine hand-worked buttonhole with rolled edge is a quality marker. The lapel buttonhole is for a boutonnière (flower). Traditional formal detail. Genuine hand-worked buttonhole is a quality marker. Function still valid — decorative or ceremonial.
A jacket has a full-canvas construction claim, but the chest feels flat and boardlike when pressed.

Suspicious — full canvas feels supple and molded. Boardlike feel suggests fused construction. Verify with additional inspection. Full canvas construction has a specific supple, three-dimensional feel. Fused chest is flat and boardlike. If claim doesn't match feel, one is wrong. Feel wins.
A shirt collar interlining is soft and drapes naturally when unbuttoned.

Unfused (soft) collar. Higher quality construction. Better drape than fused. Unfused (soft, unfused) collars use woven interlining without adhesive. Natural drape, ages well, no bubbling. Higher-tier shirt construction. Mass-market shirts almost always fused.
A silk tie has a diagonal repeat pattern of small emblems.

Regimental or club tie. Pattern may indicate specific school, military unit, club. Not appropriate to wear without membership in some traditions. Regimental ties have specific patterns tied to schools, military units, clubs. Traditional English etiquette says wear only ties from clubs you're a member of. Some patterns are open (no meaning); others are strictly associated.
A leather jacket has visible pinhole pattern along all edges.

Perforated leather ventilation. Common on motorcycle jackets, some casual leather jackets. Perforated leather (small pinholes) provides ventilation in warm weather. Common on motorcycle jackets, some casual designs. Distinct look; not damage.
A shoe upper has small stitched wingtip pattern with pinhole punching.

Brogue shoe. Traditional Scottish/Irish origin (originally for water drainage in bog work). Now standard dress shoe style. Brogues have punched hole patterns on the upper. Full brogue = wingtip with punching. Semi-brogue = simpler wingtip. Longwing = wingtip extending back to heel. Broguing was originally functional (water drainage) now decorative.
A shoe has visible stitching where sole meets upper — thicker leather layer between them.

Goodyear welt construction. Highest tier stitched-sole construction. Resolable multiple times. Goodyear welt uses a leather strip (welt) sewn between upper and insole, then to outsole. Sole can be replaced by removing bottom stitching without disturbing upper. Highest quality tier.
A dress shoe has a smooth stitchless-appearing sole-upper joint.

Cemented (glued) construction. Cannot resole. Standard on affordable and some fashion-oriented dress shoes. Cemented shoes have glue-bonded soles. Cannot be economically resoled. Standard on affordable footwear and many modern dress shoes. Aesthetically clean; short life.
A dress shoe has a stitched sole-upper joint plus stitching visible on the sole running around the perimeter.

Goodyear welt with visible outsole stitching. Traditional formal shoe construction. Very durable. Goodyear welt with visible outsole stitching is the traditional formal shoe construction. Extremely durable. Repairable multiple times. Higher weight than cemented shoes.
A handbag interior has a small square metal placard reading 'MADE IN FRANCE — DATE CODE C.'

French-made handbag with date code. Verify authenticity — date code correlates to year of manufacture in many premium brand databases. Premium handbag brands use date codes to track manufacturing year. Verify code format matches brand's system. Codes are counterfeited but often incorrectly (wrong format, wrong year for style).
A silk scarf has hand-rolled edges — small even rolls at the perimeter.

Hand-rolled edges — quality silk scarf construction. Softer than machine-hemmed. Time-consuming to produce. Hand-rolled edges are a labor-intensive traditional finish for silk scarves. Even, small rolls at the perimeter. Machine-hemmed is faster/cheaper but harder edge. Hand-rolled = premium tier.
A wool sweater has visible ribbing pattern at cuffs, hem, and neckline.

Ribbed cuff/hem construction. Standard knit finish for shape retention. Higher-tier uses tighter or more elaborate ribbing. Ribbing at cuffs, hems, and necklines helps knits retain shape. 1x1 rib (finest), 2x2 rib (standard), broader ribs (chunky). Higher-tier sweaters have tighter ribbing that recovers better after wear.
A knit sweater has color fields transitioning subtly across the piece.

Marled yarn (dyed multi-color) or specific dyeing technique. Design-tier feature; not mass-market standard. Marled yarns are multi-color twisted yarns. Space-dyed yarns have color transitions. Both are design details more common in higher-tier or artisan knits.
A polo shirt has a woven collar (not knit).

Higher-tier polo shirt construction. Woven collar drapes cleaner than knit collar. Some premium brands use this construction. Standard polo shirt has knit collar (same knit as body). Higher tier uses woven collar for cleaner drape. Slightly less flexible but more formal appearance.
A polo shirt has 'made in Peru' with 'Pima cotton' label.

Premium polo shirt fabric. Peruvian Pima cotton is a long-staple cotton — softer, more durable, less pilling than short-staple cotton. Peruvian Pima cotton is a premium long-staple cotton. Longer fibers = smoother yarns, less pilling, softer hand. Egyptian, American Supima, and Peruvian Pima are the premium cotton categories.
A leather bag has a stamp reading 'Vegetable Tanned.'

Traditional tanning method. Ages beautifully to develop patina. Higher-tier leather goods construction. Premium price tier. Vegetable tanning uses plant tannins (oak bark historically). Traditional, slow, produces leather that ages beautifully with a natural patina. Higher-tier than chrome tanning (modern, faster, most leather).
A leather bag has a stamp reading 'Full Grain Leather.'

Highest tier of leather — natural surface, most durable. Premium leather goods marker. Leather tiers: full-grain (natural surface, most durable, luxury), top-grain (sanded, more uniform, mid), corrected grain (heavily processed, low), bonded leather (glued pieces, lowest 'real leather'), synthetic (not real leather). Full grain is the premium marker.
A leather bag has a stamp reading 'Genuine Leather' but the surface is uniform embossed grain.

Genuine leather is a specific low-tier designation — actually the lowest 'real leather' category. Uniform embossed grain is common. 'Genuine leather' sounds premium but is actually a specific low-tier grade — split leather or corrected-grain leather. Embossed uniform grain is standard. Priced accordingly.
A leather bag has plasticky uniform texture and cool synthetic feel.

PU (polyurethane) or PVC synthetic — plastic, not leather. Legally not 'leather.' Synthetic 'leathers' (PU, PVC, vegan leather) are plastic. Legally cannot be labeled 'leather' in most jurisdictions. Sometimes higher quality than corrected-grain leather in durability terms.
A jacket label reads 'RN 12345' inside the collar.

US textile-compliance registration number. Verifiable on US FTC database. Identifies responsible company. RN numbers (Registered Numbers) identify the responsible company for US textile products. Lookup at ftc.gov identifies the actual maker or importer. Sometimes reveals surprising info (brand X is actually made by manufacturer Y).
A dress has care symbols only (no written care instructions).

European or international garment. Care symbols follow ISO standards. Written instructions required by US law for US-market garments. US law requires written care instructions on labels. European garments often have only symbols. Symbol-only care may indicate non-US-market garment. Standard ISO care symbols cover wash, dry, iron, bleach, dry-clean.
A dress has written care instructions in multiple languages.

International or multi-market garment. Standard for larger brands with global distribution. Multi-language care labels indicate international-market garment. Global brands standardize across markets. Not specifically a tier indicator; more of a market indicator.
A cotton shirt has 'yarn dyed' as a construction claim.

Yarn-dyed fabric — dye applied to yarn before weaving. Deeper, more durable color. Higher-tier construction than piece-dyed. Yarn-dyed fabric has color woven in (yarns dyed before weaving). Piece-dyed has color applied to finished fabric. Yarn-dyed has deeper, more durable color; often reveals color on the back of fabric matching the front. Higher-tier construction.
A cotton shirt has faded color on the back that doesn't match the front.

Piece-dyed with washing/wear fade. Common on mass-market cotton. Higher-tier yarn-dyed doesn't fade unevenly this way. Piece-dyed fabric can fade unevenly (light fades one way; the interior remains original color). Yarn-dyed fabric doesn't have this issue — color is uniform throughout the yarn.
A pair of jeans has selvage edge visible at rolled cuff.

Selvage denim — shuttle-loom construction. Premium denim tier. Cuff often rolled to display the selvage as style feature. Selvage denim has finished edges from shuttle-loom weaving. Often rolled at cuff to display the colored selvage line. Premium denim tier — usually $150+. Non-selvage denim has overlocked/serged edges.
A pair of jeans has raw (dark) unwashed denim.
