Raw denim. Will fade and shape to wearer over months of wear. Premium and enthusiast denim tier. Raw denim starts dark and rigid. Fades over 6+ months of daily wear to a personalized pattern. Enthusiast tier — some collectors don't wash for a year to maximize contrast. Higher-tier construction usually.
A pair of jeans has fabric composition 'cotton with 2% elastane.'

Stretch denim. Standard modern jean fabric. More comfortable than pure cotton but less durable long-term. Stretch denim (with 1-3% elastane/spandex) is the modern standard. Comfort improvement over pure cotton. Elastane can fatigue over years, causing loosening or breakdown. Pure-cotton denim (100% cotton) is enthusiast/traditional tier.
A T-shirt fabric is thick and heavy compared to typical T-shirts.

Heavyweight T-shirt — 6-8oz+ fabric. Premium T-shirt construction. More durable, holds shape better. T-shirt fabric weights: lightweight (3-5oz), midweight (5-6oz), heavyweight (6-8+oz). Heavyweight is premium — more durable, better drape, holds shape. Cost more, wear longer.
A T-shirt fabric is very thin and sheer.

Lightweight T-shirt fabric. Common in inexpensive or fashion-oriented tees. Less durable, less shape-holding. Very lightweight T-shirt fabric is common in mass-market and fashion tees. Less durable, quicker to become sheer at high-wear points, less shape retention. Priced accordingly.
A sweatshirt fabric has soft brushed interior surface.

French terry or fleece interior. Warm comfortable construction. Loopback (un-brushed) is alternative style. Sweatshirt interior: loopback (un-brushed, textured, casual look) or fleece (brushed, soft, warm). Fleece is warmer; loopback is more breathable. Both are legitimate constructions.
A leather jacket has visible seam-tape reinforcing all interior seams.

Higher-tier leather jacket construction. Seam-tape prevents seam stretching over years of wear. Interior seam reinforcement is a higher-tier leather jacket construction detail. Prevents seam stretching, adds structural longevity. Common on premium leather; often absent on affordable.
A leather jacket has stitching that is inconsistent — some tight, some loose along a single seam.

Low-tier construction. Inconsistent stitching indicates rushed production or low quality control. High-quality leather goods have consistent, tight, uniform stitching. Inconsistency = production quality issue. Not necessarily immediately noticeable to a buyer but degrades faster.
A wool coat has an interior label reading 'MADE IN ITALY, Biella region.'

Biella is a premier Italian wool-manufacturing region. Wool coat from Biella is likely premium construction. Biella, Italy is world-renowned for wool textile manufacturing. Premium mills (Loro Piana, Cerruti, Zegna, Vitale Barberis Canonico) are based there. Biella-made wool garments are typically premium tier.
A silk tie has 'six-fold' construction claim in the label.

Higher-quality tie construction — six folds without interlining. Between traditional 3-fold+interlining and premium 7-fold. Tie fold counts: 3-fold with interlining (standard), 3-fold no interlining (better), 6-fold (premium), 7-fold (top). No-interlining folds require heavier silk to maintain body.
A silk tie has 'made in Como, Italy' label.

Como, Italy is a major silk-manufacturing region. Como-made ties are typically premium. Como, Italy has been a silk manufacturing center for centuries. Premium silk fabrics and finished silk goods often originate here. Como-made ties or silks are usually premium tier.
A wool suit has a small tag reading 'Extra Fine Merino, Grade AAA.'

High-grade merino wool. AAA grade is premium tier. Extra fine indicates fiber diameter is very fine (18-19 micron). Merino wool grading: AAA is premium, followed by AA, A, B. Extra fine merino has fiber diameter 18-19 micron. Combined with Super numbers gives detailed fabric specification.
A dress shirt has small mother-of-pearl buttons and hand-stitched buttonholes.

Premium dress shirt tier. Mother-of-pearl buttons + hand buttonholes = high-tier construction. Combination of premium buttons + hand-worked buttonholes is a premium shirt tier signal. Typical of bespoke or high-tier ready-to-wear. Higher price point.
A dress shirt has plastic buttons and machine buttonholes.

Standard mass-market construction. Not tier indicator alone; combined with fabric quality tells whole story. Plastic buttons + machine buttonholes = mass-market default. Common on affordable shirts. Fabric quality, construction, brand together determine final tier.
A trouser waistband has a curtain-lining (extra fabric strip inside the waistband).

Curtain-lined waistband — higher-tier trouser construction. Keeps shirt tucked, provides structure. Curtain waistband has an interior extension of lining fabric that hangs down inside the trouser. Keeps shirt tucked in, provides polish and structure. Higher-tier trouser construction.
A trouser has vertical inseam that opens easily when the leg is spread.

Traditional loose-fit trouser construction. Common on classic tailored trousers. Traditional trouser construction has a specific inseam that permits stride. Modern slim-cut trousers have tighter inseams. Loose inseam allows greater range of motion but has less-tailored appearance.
A dress has a hand-picked stitch along the zipper attachment.

Traditional couture construction technique. Hand-picking prevents zipper puckering. High-end tailoring. Hand-picking a zipper (attaching by hand with tiny stitches on the exterior side) prevents puckering and gives a couture appearance. Very time-consuming; typical of high-end dress construction.
A dress has invisible zipper along back seam.

Standard modern dress construction. Invisible zipper is nearly seamless; specific machine stitching required. Invisible zippers are the modern dress standard — the zipper coil is hidden by fabric folds, appearing seamless. Requires specific machine foot for installation. Standard on modern dresses; less common on vintage.
A dress has a visible metal zipper along back seam.

Older or utilitarian dress construction. Visible zippers were standard pre-invisible-zipper (mid-1960s development). Visible metal zippers were standard on pre-1965 dresses. Some modern designs use visible zippers as design feature (utility, moto-jacket style). Vintage dresses typically have visible zippers.
A skirt has a slit at the back seam.

Kick pleat or vent — allows walking room in fitted skirts. Standard construction detail. Back vents and kick pleats provide walking room in fitted skirts. Standard construction; different styles have different appearances. Some slits are functional; some are style-only (higher).
A handbag closure is a spring-loaded metal turn-lock.

Turn-lock closure. Traditional handbag construction. Common on structured leather bags. Higher-tier brands often use premium metal. Turn-lock closures are traditional. Various brands use them; quality varies from cheap stamped metal to solid brass or gold-plated. Premium versions have detailed engraving.
A handbag has a zipper closure only.

Modern casual construction. Simpler than turn-lock or flap-with-lock. Not tier-indicator alone. Zipper-only closures are simpler and more casual. Modern crossbody bags, backpacks. Not automatically low-tier — many premium modern bags use zipper closures for functionality.
A leather bag has an interior key-hook attached with brass hardware.

Interior utility hardware. Key hook keeps keys attached and findable. Modern convenience feature. Interior utility (key hooks, phone pockets, laptop sleeves) are common modern conveniences on handbags. Presence indicates thoughtful design; absence doesn't indicate lack thereof.
A handbag closes with a magnetic snap under the flap.

Magnetic snap closure. Convenient — one-handed opening. Common on casual bags. Magnetic snaps are convenient one-handed openings. Common on casual crossbodies. Less secure than turn-locks (theft-prone in urban settings). Consumer preference varies.
A silk scarf has hand-drawn design with brushwork visible.

Painted or hand-drawn silk scarf. Higher-tier tradition (some historic French houses; some artisan makers). Priced at higher tier. Hand-painted silk scarves are labor-intensive premium products. Brushwork visible under close inspection; slight variation from piece to piece. Traditional craft. Priced significantly above screen-printed scarves.
A silk scarf has screen-printed pattern with even color fields.

Screen-printed silk scarf. Standard construction for most branded silk scarves. Consistent colors. Screen printing on silk produces uniform, precise pattern reproduction. Most branded silk scarves are screen-printed. Efficient, high-quality possible. Not craft-tier but excellent quality.
A shoe has a leather footbed with visible stitching around perimeter.

Leather footbed construction. Higher-tier shoe interior. Molds to foot over time. Leather footbeds are higher tier than fabric or foam. Mold to foot over weeks of wear. More durable, more expensive to replace. Premium shoe standard.
A shoe has a fabric-covered insole with foam padding underneath.

Standard shoe insole construction. Common on mid-tier and mass-market. Comfortable but less durable than leather. Fabric-over-foam insoles are the standard for many affordable-to-mid shoes. Comfortable, replaceable easily. Less durable than leather; may compress over time.
A dress shoe has a heel with visible layered leather buildup.

Leather-stacked heel construction. Traditional dress shoe standard. Can be resoled/replaced. Leather-stacked heels are traditional dress-shoe construction — layers of leather stacked and shaped. Traditional, repairable, ages nicely. Rubber heels (fully rubber block) are alternative construction.
A dress shoe has a solid rubber heel.

Modern casual or work-shoe construction. More durable outdoors than leather heel; less traditional appearance. Rubber heels are modern and durable. Common on work shoes, walking shoes, and modern dress shoes. Less traditional appearance than leather-stacked heel. Some hybrid heels have leather cap on rubber body.
A wool sweater has decorative hand-embroidered detail at the neckline.

Higher-tier or artisan sweater. Hand-embroidery is time-consuming decorative detail. Priced accordingly. Hand embroidery is a craft-tier detail. Distinct from machine embroidery (uniform, precise, faster). Adds significant value; premium tier or artisan product.
A polo shirt has an embroidered logo at the chest — thick uniform stitching.

Machine-embroidered logo. Standard branded polo construction. Neat and consistent. Machine-embroidered logos are standard on branded polos and shirts. Precise, uniform, high-quality possible. Distinct from screen-printed logos (thinner, less durable) and woven-in logos (part of fabric).
A shirt cuff has a placket with a small button below the main cuff button.

Gauntlet button. Traditional shirt detail — prevents forearm exposure when sleeves rolled or arms extended. Higher tier construction. Gauntlet buttons (also called sleeve placket buttons) are small buttons on the sleeve placket. Traditional dress shirt detail; prevents the placket from gapping open. Higher-tier shirts include them; mass-market often omit.
A shirt cuff shows two buttons at the wrist — one aligned, one offset.

Adjustable cuff. Two buttons at slightly different positions allow the wearer to choose a tighter or looser fit. Adjustable cuffs have two buttonholes and one or two buttons offset. Wearer chooses tighter or looser wrist fit. Higher-tier shirts include this; mass-market usually have single-position cuffs.
A jacket has a small piece of contrasting fabric at the interior collar seam.

Under-collar tape or brand-signature detail. Some brands use contrasting fabric here as signature. Some tailors use as construction detail. Under-collar details (contrasting fabric, colored thread) are sometimes signature details on high-tier brands. Not universal; brand-specific. Verify against brand references.
A dress has a bustier top with visible boning through the fabric.

Structured dress with internal boning. Formal or vintage construction. Provides shape and support. Boning (originally whalebone; now plastic or metal) provides structure and shape in dresses. Bustier construction. Formal or corset-style dresses. Visible outline through fabric is normal.
A jacket has a working boutonnière loop under the lapel — small thread loop for stem insertion.

Traditional bespoke or high-end tailoring detail. Functional flower-stem loop. Marks premium tier. Boutonnière loops are functional loops under the lapel for holding a flower stem. Traditional detail; typical of bespoke and premium ready-to-wear. Small hand-worked loop or small metal ring.
A jacket has a functional ticket pocket above the right hip pocket.

Traditional British sporting-jacket detail. Small ticket-holder pocket. Adds tier signal; often on higher-quality jackets. Ticket pockets are a small pocket above the standard hip pocket, traditionally for train tickets. British tradition. Common on higher-tier tailored jackets. Adds tailoring cost.
A jacket has swelled edges (visible seam allowance at lapel edge).

Swelled edge = specific tailoring detail with wider edge stitching for visual weight. Traditional British detail. Premium tailoring. Swelled edges have wider stitching along lapels and edges (typically 1/4 inch inside the edge). Traditional detail giving visual weight. Common on higher-tier tailored jackets, especially British and Italian traditions.
A jacket interior has a small pocket labeled 'pen pocket' — narrow slot inside the breast pocket.

Traditional bespoke or premium tailoring detail. Small dedicated pen pocket. Adds utility and tier signal. Interior utility pockets (pen pocket, glasses pocket, phone pocket) are common on higher-tier tailored jackets. Custom addition on bespoke; standard on some premium ready-to-wear brands.
A jacket has a specific number of interior pockets — six visible.

Feature-rich construction. More than the standard 2-3 interior pockets. Suggests bespoke or premium construction. Standard jacket has 2-3 interior pockets. 4-6 pockets suggests custom or premium construction (bespoke wants pockets for wallet, phone, glasses, tickets, business cards, etc.). Higher tier.
A trouser hem shows French tack (small chain-stitched thread between lining and trouser hem).

Higher-tier tailoring detail. French tack allows lining to move independently of trouser, preventing pulls. French tack (also called swing tack) is a small chain-stitched thread connecting lining to trouser hem. Allows lining independent movement, preventing pulls. High-quality tailoring detail. Rare on affordable trousers.
A tuxedo has satin-covered buttons on the front.

Traditional tuxedo construction. Satin covers the buttons to prevent visual disruption. Formal detail. Tuxedo buttons are traditionally satin-covered (same fabric as lapel facing). Preserves the formal aesthetic. Modern tuxedos may deviate. Traditional construction is more formal.
A jacket has a soft, drooping shoulder line with visible sleeve-head puckering (con rollino).

Neapolitan tailoring signature — 'spalla camicia' (shirt shoulder) with 'con rollino' puckering. Traditional detail. Neapolitan spalla camicia has a soft shoulder with the sleeve head slightly higher than shoulder. Con rollino is intentional puckering at the sleeve seam. Signature of Neapolitan tailoring tradition.
A jacket has a very slim silhouette with three-button-two-roll closure (lapel rolls to hide top button).

Modern tailoring style — three-roll-two. Contemporary aesthetic; higher-tier or fashion-oriented. Three-roll-two = three buttons on jacket, but lapel rolls to hide the top button (only middle button used). Modern aesthetic; less formal than two-button, more contemporary than three-button. Premium/fashion tier.
A tie shows a subtle geometric pattern with 6-8 colors in the weave, no printing.

Woven tie (jacquard). Higher-tier construction than printed. Colors are in the yarn, not on the surface. Very durable. Jacquard-woven ties have the pattern in the weave (multiple colors of yarn interlaced). Higher-tier than printed ties. Colors are integral to the fabric, not on the surface. Very durable and premium tier.
A tie shows a pattern with color solidly on the surface but visible weave-white on the back.

Printed tie. Color applied to surface only; back shows the base fabric color. Lower-tier than woven. Printed ties have the design applied to the surface (usually silk twill), leaving the back showing base fabric color. Lower-tier than jacquard-woven ties. Still can be premium if the silk is high-quality.
A dress has boning that follows the natural body curves — subtle, invisible from outside.

Custom/bespoke or couture dress. Precise boning placement invisible externally is a high-end detail. Boning that follows body curves invisibly is a couture-level construction. Contrast with visible or crude boning on lesser dresses. Very high-end construction; either bespoke or top-tier ready-to-wear.
A dress has multiple layers of underlining, canvas, and finishing at the bodice.

Structured couture dress construction. Multiple internal layers provide shape and support without external boning. High-end dress construction can include multiple internal layers (interlining, underlining, canvas, boning, finishing) for shape. Couture-tier dresses have detailed internal architecture. Craft-tier signal.
A silk dress has a French seam finish throughout (all seams fully enclosed in fabric tubes).

Very high-end dress construction. French seams throughout is labor-intensive; typical of couture or high-end ready-to-wear. French seams enclose all raw edges within a fabric tube. On lightweight fabrics (silk, chiffon), this is the highest-quality construction. Very time-consuming; typical only of couture or premium tier.
A wool coat has hand-basted stitching visible along the interior canvas.

Bespoke or premium ready-to-wear construction. Hand-basting the canvas is a marker of high-end tailoring. Interior canvas hand-basting (small, slightly-visible thread securing the canvas to fabric) is a bespoke or high-end tailoring marker. Fully-machined canvas exists but hand-basting is craft-tier.
A jacket has a Milanese buttonhole on the lapel — rolled, dense, precise.

High-end bespoke or ready-to-wear detail. Milanese buttonhole (hand-worked, rolled edge, precise) is a craft-tier marker. Milanese buttonhole is a specific traditional hand-worked buttonhole style. Rolled edge, dense stitching, keyhole shape. Hand-worked is craft-tier; machine-done Milanese-style exists but is a mimicry.
A jacket has 'canvas visible under lapel' — buyer can lift lapel and see internal canvas.

Full-canvas construction confirmable by lifting lapel. Premium tier marker. Full-canvas construction can be verified by feeling and lifting the lapel — canvas is visible/felt as separate from the fabric. Fused construction won't show canvas because it's glued to the fabric. Definitive test.
A dress shirt has a two-piece back yoke.

Higher-tier shirt construction. Two-piece back yoke has a seam in the middle, allowing pattern matching or fit shaping. Premium tier. Back yoke construction: one-piece (mass-market) vs two-piece (higher tier). Two-piece allows better fit shaping over shoulders and pattern-matched checks/stripes. Premium shirt tier.
A dress shirt has pattern matching at the pocket — pocket pattern aligns exactly with the body pattern.

Higher-tier shirt construction. Pattern matching at pockets is labor-intensive; requires larger fabric use. Premium tier. Pattern matching (aligning stripes, checks, plaids exactly across pocket seams) is a labor-intensive detail. Requires more fabric and precision. Premium and bespoke tier. Mass-market shirts don't pattern-match.
A jacket has hand-stitched lapel roll — the lapel folds precisely with subtle roll from hand shaping.

Bespoke or premium tier — hand-shaped lapel roll is a craft detail. Precise, subtle, well-executed. Hand-shaped lapel roll (via hand-pressing and internal canvas work) creates a specific 3D lapel curve. Distinguishes bespoke from mass-produced. Feel and appearance both signal craft-tier.
A cashmere sweater label reads 'inner Mongolia' as origin.

Prime cashmere origin region. Inner Mongolia produces premium cashmere fiber. Well-executed sweaters from this region are top tier. Inner Mongolia is a primary cashmere production region — the climate produces very fine goat undercoat. Not all Inner Mongolia cashmere is premium; construction quality matters. But origin is a positive signal.
A cashmere sweater has fiber diameter noted as '15 micron.'

Very fine cashmere — 15 micron is exceptional. Baby cashmere or high-grade tier. Premium market. Cashmere fiber diameter: standard 16-19 micron. Baby cashmere 13-15 micron. 15 micron is very fine, close to baby cashmere. Extraordinary softness. Premium price.
A cashmere sweater label reads 'baby cashmere.'

Ultra-premium tier. Baby cashmere is from goat kids' first shearing — finest and rarest cashmere. Baby cashmere comes from the first shearing of goat kids (before adult coat develops). Very fine fiber (13-15 micron), extremely soft. Extremely rare. Priced accordingly — several times standard cashmere.
A silk garment has a subtle sheen that changes as the fabric moves.

Consistent with mulberry silk. Mulberry silk has a characteristic changing sheen. Premium silk tier. Mulberry silk (from silkworms fed mulberry leaves) is the premium silk. Fine, smooth, characteristic changing sheen. Tussah silk (wild silk) is coarser, less sheen, less uniform.
A silk garment has visible slubs (irregular thick spots) in the weave.

Dupioni silk or tussah silk. Slubs are characteristic. Different silk category from mulberry. Rougher texture, distinct look. Dupioni silk has visible slubs from irregular yarn. Distinct textured look. Not lower-quality automatically; different aesthetic. Popular in decor and fashion contexts wanting texture.
A wool blazer feels heavier than expected for its size.

Substantial wool fabric — heavier fabric is often (not always) higher tier. Could also indicate synthetic-blend or heavily-lined construction. Wool fabric weight varies by intended use. Winter wool coats and heavier blazers are naturally heavy. Extra weight can also come from synthetic blends (polyester adds weight without warmth) or heavy lining. Not weight-alone as tier indicator.
A wool blazer feels very lightweight compared to typical wool blazers.

Lightweight wool — Super 130s or higher. Suitable for warmer climates or transitional seasons. Premium tier. Lightweight wool (Super 130s+) is used for warm-climate wearing or transitional seasons. Delicate, luxurious hand. Premium tier construction. Not for cold weather; not everyday-wear durability.
A jacket has 'MTM' or 'made to measure' label.

Made-to-measure garment — customized to specific measurements from an existing pattern. Between ready-to-wear and bespoke tiers. MTM (made-to-measure) uses existing patterns adjusted to individual measurements. Higher tier than ready-to-wear; lower tier than bespoke (which is fully custom pattern). Common at premium tailoring houses.
A jacket has 'bespoke' claim in interior label.

Fully custom construction from custom pattern. Highest tier. Verify by examining hand-work indicators. Bespoke = fully custom pattern + fitting + hand construction. Highest tailoring tier. Some brands misuse the term for MTM. Verify by hand-work markers (pick stitching, hand buttonholes, canvas).
A shirt cuff has round French-cuff construction — folded double-cuff with cuff-link holes.

French cuff shirt — formal tier. Requires cuff-links to wear. Higher formality than barrel cuff. French cuff (double cuff) is folded and secured with cuff-links. Formal tier. Traditional business/formal shirt style. Contemporary casual shirts don't use French cuffs.
A shirt cuff has convertible construction — can be worn as barrel or French cuff.

Convertible cuff. Versatile — barrel or French cuff. Compromise style; less formal than dedicated French, more formal than barrel. Convertible cuffs have buttonholes for both button and cuff-link options. Versatile — casual button use or formal cuff-link use. Compromise construction; less traditional than dedicated French cuff.
A silk tie has an interlining that is very thick and firm.

Traditional wool or silk-blend interlining. Contributes to knot shape and drape. Higher tier tie. Traditional tie interlining is wool or wool-blend fabric. Provides body and shape retention. Firmer interlining = firmer knot, better drape. Higher-tier ties have thicker interlining.
A silk tie has a thin, flexible interlining.

Standard mass-market tie construction. Modern preference for softer, flexible drape. Modern thin interlining creates a more flexible drape. Common on affordable-to-mid ties. Some premium designers prefer this style. Not a bad construction; different aesthetic.
A silk tie has no interlining.

Unlined tie. Very soft drape; unique aesthetic. Some premium designers prefer this; others consider it lower-tier. Unlined ties have no interlining. Very soft drape, floppy knot. Aesthetic choice. Some premium designers use unlined for a specific look; some low-end ties are unlined for cost. Context-dependent.
A wool sweater has moth damage — small hole near shoulder seam.

Damage. Small hole reparable with darning or invisible mending. Discount modestly; more if multiple holes. Single moth hole is typically reparable ($30-100 professional invisible mending). Multiple holes suggest active moth infestation OR long-past infestation; verify. Cedar and lavender protect against moths.
A leather bag has visible marks from a magnet clasp indicating repeated contact wear.

Wear pattern from clasp. Not surprising on leather bags with magnetic closures. Some patina; assess overall condition. Magnetic clasps eventually wear leather at contact points. Small dark marks develop. Not damage per se — patina. Context-dependent whether desirable or issue.
A dress shirt has all details premium (buttons, buttonholes, construction) but the fabric is thin and cheap.

Inconsistent construction — premium details on cheap fabric. Possibly refurbished by adding better details; possibly manufacturer inconsistency. Inconsistency in construction level (premium details on cheap fabric) is unusual. Could indicate the shirt was refurbished (upgraded details on cheap base) or the manufacturer inconsistent. Ask before assuming.
A jacket has a bespoke-quality construction throughout but the fit is clearly wrong for typical body types.

Bespoke jacket made for a specific individual. Fit reflects that person's measurements. Alterations may improve fit but limits how much. Bespoke garments are made for specific individuals. Fit-testing before buying is important — bespoke doesn't necessarily fit anyone else well. Some alterations possible but limited. Buy only if fits well or close-to-fits.
A trouser has 'grosgrain waistband' construction.

Grosgrain fabric on interior waistband — provides structure and prevents shirt slippage. Premium trouser tier. Grosgrain (heavy ribbed silk or synthetic) waistband material provides structure and grip. Higher tier than plain fabric waistband. Common on higher-tier trousers.
A trouser has 'no split rear seam' (single-piece rear panel).

Requires more fabric — higher tier construction. Also allows for different fits. Rear seam construction: single-piece panel (higher tier, no back seam) vs two-piece with center-back seam (standard). Single-piece requires more fabric; cleaner appearance. Premium construction.
A shirt has removable collar stays made of brass.

Higher tier — brass stays are more durable than plastic. Some very-high-end shirts use mother-of-pearl stays. Collar stay materials: plastic (standard), brass or metal (higher tier — more durable, more expensive), mother-of-pearl or bone (very-high-end, rare).
A shirt has stitched-in collar stays that can't be removed.

Fixed collar stays. Alternative construction. Some higher-tier shirts use this to prevent stay loss. Others use fixed as cost-saving. Fixed collar stays are either cost-saving (mass-market don't want stays to be lost) or premium anti-loss (fixed stays never lost, cleaner collar line). Context determines which.
A shirt has a placket construction where the underside placket is folded into 6+ layers of fabric.

Higher-tier construction. Multi-layer underside placket adds durability at high-stress button area. Multi-layer underside placket construction adds body and durability where buttons attach. Higher tier construction. Simple two-layer underside is standard mass-market.
A jacket has 'quarter-lined' construction — lining only in shoulders and upper back.

Very casual or warm-climate construction. Interior structure visible. Not tier signal in either direction. Quarter-lining is minimal — lining only in shoulders and upper back. Common on casual jackets, warm-climate garments, deconstructed jackets. Aesthetic and functional choice; not tier signal.
A knit garment has 'fully-fashioned' construction — the pieces are knit to shape, not cut from knit fabric.

Higher tier knitwear. Fully-fashioned means the sweater pieces are knit in their final shape (with shaping stitches at seams). No cutting waste; superior finish. Fully-fashioned knits are shaped as they're knit (versus cut-and-sewn where knit fabric is cut and sewn like woven fabric). Superior finish, no fraying, better fit. Premium tier construction.
A knit sweater shows cut-and-sew construction — knit fabric edges visible at seams.

Standard mass-market knit construction. Fabric knit then cut and sewn. Standard for T-shirts and affordable knit garments. Cut-and-sew knits use pre-knit fabric cut into pieces. Standard for T-shirts, casual sweatshirts, mass-market knits. Faster and cheaper than fully-fashioned. Standard tier.
A dress has multiple layers — inner slip, base fabric, decorative overlay.

Complex layered dress construction. Premium tier or couture. Higher retail price. Layered dress construction (slip + base + overlay + trim) is time-consuming. Common in premium tier and couture. Higher retail price. Verify all layers are in good condition.
A wool coat has 'melton' fabric — dense, felted, water-resistant.

Traditional coat fabric — melton is a heavy felted wool. Water-resistant, warm. Naval and military traditional; still used in premium coats. Melton is a dense felted wool fabric. Water-resistant, warm, traditional. Peacoats and naval coats often melton. Higher-tier winter coats use melton. Very durable.
A wool coat has 'boiled wool' fabric — thick, felted, unique texture.

European tradition (Austrian, German, Scandinavian). Warm, distinctive texture. Common in loden-tradition coats. Boiled wool is felted (shrunk and matted intentionally) creating dense, distinctive texture. Warm, water-resistant, traditional. Loden coats (Alpine tradition), some cardigans and jackets. Distinct aesthetic.
A camel-hair coat has visible fine cream-colored hair fibers throughout.

Genuine camel-hair coat. Warm, luxurious, distinctive color. High tier winter coat. Camel hair (from Bactrian camel undercoat) is warm and luxurious. Distinctive cream/tan color. High tier winter coat fabric. Some 'camel-hair' is blended; check label for percentage.
A silk garment has 'charmeuse' construction — one side matte, one side shiny.

Charmeuse silk — satin-weave with one glossy face and one matte face. Common in lingerie, dresses, blouses. Premium silk category. Charmeuse silk has satin weave — glossy on one side, matte on reverse. Common in evening wear and lingerie. Fluid drape, slippery hand. Premium silk category.
A silk garment has 'crepe' texture — slightly pebbled surface.

Crepe silk — silk with twisted yarns producing pebbled texture. Elegant drape; common in dresses. Premium silk category. Crepe silk has slight pebbled or grainy surface from twisted yarns. Elegant drape, less shiny than satin/charmeuse. Common in dresses and blouses. Premium silk category.
A cotton garment has visible slub yarns randomly through the fabric.

Slub cotton — decorative yarn variation. Common in bohemian and premium casual garments. Style choice. Slub cotton yarns have intentional thick/thin variations. Bohemian and premium casual style. Not defect. Adds visual texture and character to plain fabrics.
A wool coat label reads '100% cashmere' with high tag price and visible chemical smell.

Suspicious — chemical smell could indicate treatment residue OR could indicate the garment isn't real cashmere. Investigate. Chemical smells on garments can indicate: dye residue (normal for new garments), formaldehyde treatment (some wool processing), or fake fiber. Real cashmere doesn't have strong chemical smell. Investigate.
A dress shirt has functional interior placket with a small metal support to keep the placket flat.

High-tier detail. Interior metal support prevents placket puckering. Craft-tier construction. Interior placket supports (small metal strips or firm interlining) prevent placket puckering and maintain crisp appearance. Craft-tier construction; common on bespoke shirts.
A cashmere sweater is unusually stretchy for cashmere.

Suspicious — pure cashmere has limited stretch. May be a wool-cashmere blend or synthetic-blend not disclosed. Investigate. Pure cashmere has limited stretch. Excessive stretch suggests blend with wool (limited stretch increase) or synthetic (major stretch increase). Verify label; consider fabric burn test if legally possible.
A jacket lining has a subtle brand pattern woven in that matches a specific known brand's reference.

Authentic-consistent — verifiable brand identifier. Reference-check for exact match on pattern scale, colors, weave. Brand-signature linings (specific patterns, colors, weaves) are authentication signals. Verify exactly against reference photos — pattern scale, color, thread count, weave direction all matter. Off by even small amounts = counterfeit.
A leather bag has stitching on the strap that's a slightly different color than the body of the bag.

Standard leather goods construction. Straps often have different color stitching (for durability visibility). Not authenticity issue. Different stitching colors on straps vs body are standard leather goods construction. Strap stitching is often more visible (heavier thread for strength). Not authenticity issue. Compare to brand standards.
A dress has hem finished with hand-worked bias binding.

Very high-tier construction. Hand-bound hem is couture-level construction. Priced accordingly. Hem-binding by hand is a very time-consuming couture-level construction. Bias binding hides the raw edge with a strip of fabric folded and hand-stitched over it. Couture-tier detail.
A jacket has 'hand-tacked lining' — lining attached at multiple points to prevent shifting.

Premium construction. Hand-tacked lining prevents shifting inside the jacket over time. Common on premium bespoke or ready-to-wear. Hand-tacked lining uses small hand-worked stitches at multiple points to attach lining. Prevents shifting and lengthens jacket life. Premium bespoke and ready-to-wear construction. Common on higher-tier pieces.
what's earthenware?
porous fired clay, low temperature (900-1150C), needs glaze for water resistance
what's stoneware?
vitrified clay, medium-high temperature (1150-1300C), non-porous
what's porcelain?
translucent white clay, high temperature (1200-1400C), vitreous
what's bone-china?
porcelain with 30-45% bone ash, translucent, strong, English tradition
what's hard-paste?
true porcelain — kaolin + feldspar, fired high, Chinese/European tradition
what's soft-paste?
earlier European porcelain substitute, lower firing, less durable
what's creamware?
cream-colored refined earthenware, 1760s+ British innovation
what's pearlware?
creamware with cobalt-blue tint, 1780s-1830s British
what's ironstone?
hard white stoneware, 1813+ Staffordshire, durable dinnerware
what's majolica?
tin-glazed earthenware with colorful decoration, Italian tradition
what's delftware?
Dutch tin-glazed earthenware, blue-and-white, 1600s+
what's faience?
French tin-glazed earthenware
what's yellow-ware?
yellow earthenware, mostly utility, 1830s-1900 US and UK
what's redware?
red earthenware, mostly utility, early American
what's salt-glaze?
stoneware glazed by throwing salt into kiln, orange-peel texture
what's ash-glaze?
stoneware glazed with wood/plant ash, natural variation
what's celadon?
green-glazed stoneware or porcelain, Chinese/Korean/Japanese tradition
what's raku?
Japanese low-fire tea-ceremony ware, rapid cooling, crackle glaze
what's shino?
Japanese white feldspathic glaze, subtle blush and crawling
what's tenmoku?
Japanese black iron glaze, tea-bowl tradition, oil-spot variant
what's wood-fired?
kiln fueled by wood, natural ash deposits and flame markings
what's gas-fired?
modern kiln fuel — uniform reduction/oxidation possible
what's electric-fired?
modern kiln — oxidation-only atmosphere, uniform temperature
what's reduction-fired?
low-oxygen atmosphere alters glaze color (celadon, tenmoku need this)
what's oxidation-fired?
normal-air firing, standard atmosphere
what's transfer-print?
printed pattern applied via transfer paper, uniform mechanical
what's hand-painted?
decoration applied by brush, visible variation, higher tier
what's decal?
printed decoration applied as sticker, fired on, visible edge
what's overglaze?
decoration applied over glaze, fired at lower temperature, wears with time
what's underglaze?
decoration applied under glaze, durable, must survive glaze firing
what's inglaze?
decoration sinking into glaze during firing, permanent
what's gilding?
gold decoration, fired at low temperature, wears with washing
what's sprigging?
small applied decoration (Wedgwood jasperware tradition)
what's sgraffito?
scratched decoration through colored slip revealing body
what's slip?
liquid clay used for decoration or joining
what's kiln-mark?
unglazed spots where kiln stilts supported piece during firing
what's stilt-mark?
specific kiln support marks, sometimes 3 dots on unglazed foot
what's spur-mark?
sharp kiln support marks, older tradition
what's crazing?
network of fine cracks in glaze — thermal-expansion mismatch
what's flow-blue?
cobalt-blue transfer decoration that has run/flowed during firing, blurred outlines
what's plate?
flat serving dish
what's bowl?
concave vessel for food or decor
what's cup?
small drinking vessel
what's saucer?
small dish under a cup
what's mug?
large drinking vessel with handle
what's teapot?
vessel with spout and handle for tea service
what's coffee-pot?
taller narrower pouring vessel
what's creamer?
small pouring vessel for cream
what's sugar-bowl?
lidded vessel for sugar
what's gravy-boat?
elongated pouring vessel with handle
what's serving-platter?
large flat serving surface
what's tureen?
large lidded serving vessel for soup
what's vase?
decorative vessel for flowers or display
what's urn?
traditional-form decorative vessel
what's jar?
cylindrical storage vessel with lid
what's canister?
kitchen storage jar
what's figurine?
decorative ceramic figure
what's bust?
sculptural head-and-shoulders figure
what's wall-plate?
decorative plate designed for hanging
what's cachepot?
decorative pot for holding houseplant containers
what's pitcher?
large pouring vessel
what's punch-bowl?
large decorative serving bowl
what's compote?
footed dish for serving fruit or dessert
what's chalice?
footed drinking vessel, often ceremonial
what's tea-caddy?
lidded jar for storing tea leaves
what's biscuit-jar?
cookie jar with lid
what's trinket-box?
small lidded box for small items
what's planter?
container for growing plants
what's candle-holder?
vessel for holding candles
what's wall-pocket?
hanging pocket vessel
what's hanging-lamp?
suspended lighting fixture
what's tile?
flat ceramic square for surfaces
what's trivet?
heat-resistant plate for hot dishes
what's spoon-rest?
small dish for kitchen spoons
what's coaster?
small round or square for drink protection
i see: white body thin translucent under light
porcelain or bone china — hold to light, real translucency
i see: white body opaque thick no light
earthenware, stoneware, or opaque porcelain — thicker body
i see: cream body visible in foot ring unglazed
creamware or later earthenware — foot color diagnostic
i see: red body visible in foot ring
redware earthenware — early American or European folk pottery
i see: gray body visible in foot ring
stoneware — non-porous, vitreous, mid-range firing
i see: rings when tapped clear and sustained
vitreous body (porcelain, bone china, stoneware)
i see: thuds when tapped dull and short
porous body (earthenware) or damaged/cracked piece
i see: network of fine cracks across glaze
crazing — thermal-expansion mismatch, common on old pieces or reduces value
i see: intentional fine crackle in glaze with color emphasized
intentional crackle glaze (Chinese, Japanese tradition) — decorative not defect
i see: orange peel texture across body
salt-glaze stoneware — early American and English utility tradition
i see: smooth uniform shiny glaze
modern industrial-glazed piece, common mass-market
what's matte-satin-finish?
modern designer glaze or specific artisan style
i see: pooling of glaze where thick drips visible
hand-glazed studio pottery — imperfection is character
i see: underside unglazed with clean cut foot
machine-thrown or slip-cast piece, modern production
i see: underside unglazed with hand turned foot ring
hand-thrown piece, studio or older tradition
i see: flat bottom with 3 small glaze gaps around perimeter
stilt marks from kiln support — modern industrial firing
i see: flat bottom with 3 small holes through glaze
kiln stilt marks penetrated glaze — older tradition, hand-fired
i see: mark with blue underglaze crossed swords
Meissen or Meissen-tradition mark — investigate maker, extensive counterfeiting
i see: mark with red overglaze lion and crown
British-tradition, likely Royal Doulton, Wedgwood, or similar — verify
i see: mark with anchor symbol
possibly Chelsea (early), Bow (early), or various later marks — cross-reference
i see: mark with crossed arrows or crossed baguettes
possibly Sèvres or Sèvres-style tradition — extensive fakes exist
i see: mark that is impressed not printed
usually older or studio-tradition, mark stamped into wet clay before firing
i see: mark that is hand painted signature
studio pottery or higher-tier decorated piece, verify artist
i see: mark with 'Made in Occupied Japan'
post-WWII 1945-1952 Japanese exports, specific collector market
i see: mark with 'Nippon' only
Japanese pre-1921 export mark, before 'Japan' required
i see: mark with 'Japan' only
Japanese post-1921 export mark, pre-Occupied period 1921-1945
i see: mark with country of origin in english
post-1891 US import (McKinley Tariff Act required country marking)
i see: mark with no country of origin
possibly pre-1891 (or non-US market or unmarked artisan)
i see: mark with 'Bone China' and crown
British bone china, likely 20th century, verify maker
i see: mark with 'Ironstone' and crown
Staffordshire ironstone, 19th-20th century
i see: decoration with visible brush strokes
hand-painted — higher tier than transfer or decal
i see: decoration with uniform pattern and visible edge
decal — printed sticker fired on, mass-market decoration
i see: decoration with uniform pattern and no edge
transfer print — engraved copper plate to paper to ceramic, 1750s+ tradition
i see: decoration with raised gold that flakes
overglaze gilding — wears with washing, don't dishwash
i see: decoration with color under clear glaze
underglaze decoration — durable, dishwasher-safe, common blue and white
A white plate is thin and shows shadow when held to a bright light.

Porcelain or bone china — translucent body. Translucency is the primary distinguishing feature of porcelain and bone china from other ceramic bodies. Hold to bright light; if you can see silhouettes through the piece, it's translucent.
A white plate is thick and opaque, doesn't transmit any light.

Earthenware, stoneware, or opaque porcelain (rare). Not translucent. Opaque bodies are earthenware, stoneware, or opaque porcelain (which is unusual). Most white opaque plates are stoneware or ironstone (a specific hard earthenware tradition).
A cup is very lightweight for its size and rings clearly when tapped.

Porcelain or bone china. Vitreous body rings; light weight suggests thin fine wall. Fine porcelain and bone china are both light and ring musically. Two-second musical ring vs one-second thud distinguishes fine ware from earthenware.
A cup is heavy for its size and thuds when tapped.

Earthenware or stoneware, or possibly a cracked/damaged vitreous piece. Heavy + thud = usually earthenware. Confirmed if opaque and thick. Damaged/cracked pieces of any body may thud due to the crack.
The bottom of a plate shows an unglazed foot ring where the body is white.

White-bodied ceramic — porcelain, bone china, or ironstone. Foot color indicates body color. The foot ring shows body color because glaze is scraped from where the piece sits in the kiln. White body could be porcelain, bone china, or white-bodied earthenware/ironstone.
The bottom of a plate shows an unglazed foot ring where the body is cream-colored.

Creamware or later cream-bodied earthenware. Common British tradition. Cream body under a white glaze is creamware. Wedgwood pioneered creamware in the 1760s. Continued in various forms through the 20th century.
The bottom of a plate shows an unglazed foot ring where the body is gray.

Stoneware — non-porous vitreous body. Higher fire than earthenware. Gray body is characteristic of stoneware. Common in utility ware, salt-glaze pieces, and modern studio pottery. Non-porous, food-safe.
The bottom of a plate shows an unglazed foot ring where the body is red-orange.

Redware — early American or folk-tradition earthenware. Redware is a classic folk-pottery tradition. Early American and European folk pottery, generally utility ware. Not the tier of porcelain but historically significant.
The bottom of a piece has a mark 'MADE IN JAPAN' with a maker symbol.

Post-1921 Japanese export. 'Japan' mark required after 1921 for US import. US McKinley Tariff Act 1891 required country of origin on imports. Japan initially marked 'Nippon' until 1921, then 'Japan.' Post-WWII (1945-1952) marked 'Made in Occupied Japan.'
The bottom of a piece has a mark 'NIPPON' only.

Pre-1921 Japanese export. 'Nippon' was the required mark 1891-1921. 'Nippon' marks date pieces to 1891-1921. Collectible category — 'Nippon porcelain' has an active collector market for fine hand-painted pieces.
The bottom of a piece has 'MADE IN OCCUPIED JAPAN.'

1945-1952 Japanese export. Specific post-WWII marking. Collector category. 'Made in Occupied Japan' was required 1945-1952 during US occupation. Distinct collector market for this narrow window of production.
A cup and saucer set has a printed pattern that looks identical on all pieces.

Transfer print — engraved plate to paper to ceramic. Mechanical reproduction. 1750s+ tradition. Transfer print revolutionized ceramic decoration. Copper plate engraved with design, printed to tissue paper, applied to ceramic, fired. Uniform pattern reproduction across pieces.
A plate has a pattern with visible small brush marks and slight color variation.

Hand-painted decoration — higher tier than transfer or decal. Each piece slightly unique. Hand-painted pieces have visible brush strokes and small variations. Higher decorative tier than mechanical methods. May be signed by decorator.
A plate has a decorative pattern with visible edge where the pattern ends abruptly.

Decal — printed sticker applied and fired. Mass-market decoration technique. Edge visible under close inspection. Decals are printed sheets applied to the ceramic surface then fired. Uniform pattern reproduction; visible edge distinguishes from transfer print (which has no distinct edge).
A teapot has raised gold decoration that shows wear at the handle and spout.

Overglaze gilding. Wears with washing and use — common wear pattern on gold-decorated ceramics. Overglaze gold is fired at lower temperature than the base glaze. Sits on top and wears away over time and washing. Don't dishwash gold-decorated pieces.
A blue-and-white plate has cobalt-blue decoration in a chinoiserie pattern.

Blue and white porcelain — could be Chinese export, Dutch Delft, English willow-pattern, or many other traditions. Reference specific pattern to identify. Blue and white is a huge category with many traditions. Chinese export (Ming, Qing), Dutch Delft (tin-glazed earthenware), English (Willow pattern is most famous), Japanese, and others.
A plate has a network of fine cracks visible across the glaze surface.

Crazing — thermal-expansion mismatch. Common on old pieces or reduces value on modern pieces. Crazing is a network of fine cracks in the glaze (not through the body). Caused by expansion mismatch between glaze and body. On antique pieces sometimes considered part of character; on modern pieces reduces value.
A plate has intentional decorative crackle — larger cracks emphasized with color.

Intentional crackle glaze. Chinese, Korean, Japanese tradition. Decorative feature not defect. Intentional crackle glaze is a specific technique. Chinese Ge ware, Korean celadon, Japanese raku all have crackle traditions. Cracks are often emphasized by rubbing ink or pigment into them.
A large stoneware jar has an orange-peel texture across its surface.

Salt-glaze stoneware. Early American and English utility tradition. Rock salt thrown into hot kiln vaporizes and glazes the surface. Salt-glazed stoneware has a characteristic orange-peel or dimpled texture from sodium chloride vapor reacting with the clay surface. Traditional American and English stoneware technique. 18th-19th century tradition largely, some modern revival.
A vase has a green glaze with pooled darker areas where glaze collected.

Celadon or celadon-tradition piece. Green glaze with natural pooling variation. Chinese/Korean/Japanese tradition. Celadon glazes are green-to-blue-green from iron oxide reduction-fired. Pool naturally in crevices, creating darker green areas. Traditional Chinese (Longquan), Korean, and Japanese. Modern studio potters also use celadon.
A tea bowl has black glaze with small silver-oil-spot patterns.

Tenmoku (Japanese) or Chinese jian ware. Iron-glaze technique. Tea-ceremony tradition. Tenmoku glazes are iron-rich, appearing black with variable crystals. Oil-spot patterns are metallic-silver crystals that form on the surface. Traditional Chinese jian ware and Japanese tenmoku.
A plate has a large 'W' mark in blue on the underside.

Possibly Wedgwood or similar. Wedgwood marks vary by era; verify by full research. Simple letter marks are common but sometimes ambiguous. Wedgwood used 'WEDGWOOD' impressed, various printed marks, and different logos over centuries. Reference lookup essential.
A plate has a mark 'WEDGWOOD' impressed into the clay (not printed).

Wedgwood — impressed mark suggests older piece (impressed marks are 18th-19th century Wedgwood tradition). Wedgwood used impressed marks (pressed into wet clay before firing) for centuries. Combined with other identifiers (body color, glaze, form), dates piece within Wedgwood production era.
A blue-and-white plate has slightly blurred outlines on the design.

Flow-blue — cobalt-blue transfer decoration that flowed during firing. Distinct 19th-century category. Flow-blue is a specific 19th-century decorating technique. Cobalt blue was made to 'flow' or blur during firing by adding volatile compounds to the kiln. Highly collectible category.
A figurine has a very detailed hand-painted face with subtle skin tones.

Higher-tier figurine, possibly artist-decorated. Hand-painted faces are labor-intensive; usually mid-to-high tier. Figurine facial painting quality is a strong tier indicator. Basic decal or transfer faces are mass-market. Hand-painted with subtle shading and detail are studio or premium tier.
A vase has a smooth uniform shiny glaze with no visible variation.

Modern industrial-glazed piece. Even coverage and consistency indicate mechanical spray-application or dipping. Modern mass-market pieces have uniform mechanical glaze application (spray, dip). Studio pieces often show more variation. Not automatically lower tier — many premium pieces are also industrially glazed.
A bowl has a matte white glaze that appears almost dry-looking.

Matte glaze or 'satin' finish. Modern designer or specific artisan tradition. Matte glazes are a modern designer aesthetic. Some Japanese-tradition glazes are matte (specific white or celadon variants). Not necessarily lower tier; often distinctive.
A large planter has a rough exterior surface with mineral flakes visible.

Textured stoneware — decorative surface treatment. Modern studio or garden-ware tradition. Textured stoneware is common for garden and decorative pieces. Rough surface can be part of clay body or applied decoration. Studio or commercial tradition.
A cup has 'Made in England' mark with a small crown symbol.

British ceramic export. Post-1891 (McKinley Tariff Act). Crown suggests possibly royal warrant or specific maker mark. British ceramics with 'Made in England' are post-1891. Crown symbols vary — some royal warrants, some specific maker marks. Reference lookup identifies specific maker.
A saucer has three tiny unglazed spots around the underside perimeter.

Stilt marks — kiln support marks. Modern industrial firing standard. Small three-point support marks. Modern industrial firing uses kiln stilts to prevent pieces from sticking to shelves. Small three-point marks are standard. Older pieces had different support methods (spurs, sagger placement).
A cup has visible little pinholes through the glaze.

Pinholes are minor glaze defects. Common in mass-market. Not damage-critical for use; visible defect. Pinholes in glaze come from gases escaping during firing. Common minor defect in industrial production. Doesn't affect food-safety but visible defect. Would be rejected in strict quality control.
A plate has a small chip out of the edge.

Chip damage. Sometimes reparable (grinding down chip edge or filling); often reduces value significantly. Chip damage is a common ceramic issue. Small edge chips can sometimes be ground smooth for continued use; visible chips reduce value 30-50%+. Large or multiple chips reduce more.
A teapot has a hairline crack visible running from rim down the body.

Hairline crack. Can leak; may propagate with thermal cycling. Value reduced significantly; usable for display only. Hairline cracks are structural defects. Even small cracks can leak liquids and expand under thermal stress (hot tea in a cracked teapot risks the crack extending). Display-only for most purposes.
A vase has a large chip repaired with visible glue and painted-over color.

Restoration — noticeable repair reduces value significantly. Some professional restoration is nearly invisible; visible repair reduces further. Ceramic restoration ranges from museum-quality invisible repair to obvious amateur glue work. Museum-quality can preserve considerable value; obvious repair reduces to a fraction of original.
A figurine has been restored — one arm has visible seam from reattachment.

Restored figurine. Visible restoration reduces value 40-70% depending on quality and rarity. Restored figurines are common. Value reduction depends on rarity of piece, quality of restoration, and how visible the repair is. Rare pieces still hold value; common pieces heavily discounted.
A teacup has a small hairline crack in the handle where it meets the body.

Structural weakness. May fail with normal use. Handle-body joint cracks are common failure points. Handle-body cracks are structural. Can fail during use (handle breaks off, dropping hot liquid). Value is display-only. Repair possible but expensive relative to piece value usually.
A large decorative platter has slight brown staining in the crazing.

Aged crazing with tea/coffee staining. Cosmetic issue; usually removable with peroxide soak. Reduces value slightly. Aged pieces with crazed glaze often stain with tea, coffee, food residues. Hydrogen peroxide soaks (30% peroxide, 24-48 hours) can remove much staining. Cosmetic issue.
A plate has a maker's mark 'IRONSTONE — MASON'S — PATENT.'

Mason's Ironstone — Staffordshire tradition. Started 1813 as patent ironstone. Multiple later variations. Mason's introduced patent ironstone in 1813 — durable hard white earthenware. Marks vary over time. Mason's became Ashworth, then various successors. Reference lookup for specific mark identifies era.
A plate has a mark 'BONE CHINA' with a crown and text 'ENGLAND.'

English bone china, post-1891. Bone china requires bone ash — English tradition since 1748. English bone china dates from 1748 (Thomas Frye). By 20th century it's a major English tradition. Various makers use 'Bone China' with maker marks. Specific maker requires cross-reference.
A cup has 'MEISSEN' or crossed swords mark on the underside.

Meissen or Meissen-tradition. Extensive counterfeiting exists. Verify authenticity with detailed reference. Meissen crossed-swords mark dates from 1720s. Extensively copied and counterfeited. Reference mark database to verify against known authentic Meissen marks. Multiple styles exist across centuries.
A saucer has 'SÈVRES' mark or crossed arrows on the underside.

Sèvres or Sèvres-tradition. Extensively counterfeited. Verify with detailed reference. Sèvres was France's premier porcelain manufacturer. Extensively counterfeited — many 'Sèvres-style' pieces exist. Genuine Sèvres marks vary by era and had strict production controls.
A plate has 'LIMOGES' printed on the underside with a decorator name.

French Limoges porcelain. Multiple makers use Limoges mark; sometimes maker mark + decorator mark. Verify specific maker. Limoges is a French region with many porcelain makers. 'Limoges' alone doesn't identify specific maker — reference to specific maker mark identifies. Sometimes decorator marks are separate from factory marks.
A vase has 'ROSENTHAL' with 'BAVARIA' or 'GERMANY.'

Rosenthal — major German porcelain maker. 20th century designs range from traditional to modernist. Rosenthal (founded 1879) is a major German porcelain maker. Excellent 20th-century modernist collaborations. Traditional and modernist lines. Value varies dramatically by line and era.
A cup has 'ROYAL COPENHAGEN' with three wavy lines symbol.

Royal Copenhagen (Denmark) — the three wavy lines represent Denmark's three straits. Recognized worldwide. Royal Copenhagen's three wavy lines are iconic. Blue Fluted pattern is their signature. Founded 1775, still active. Various tiers within Royal Copenhagen production.
A plate has 'ARABIA' printed on the underside with 'FINLAND.'

Arabia — major Finnish ceramic manufacturer. Excellent 20th-century modernist tableware and art pottery. Arabia (founded 1873, Helsinki) is Finland's major ceramic maker. Excellent modernist tableware designs. Some series (Kaj Franck, Rut Bryk) are collector-tier.
A plate has 'RÖRSTRAND' printed on the underside with 'SWEDEN.'

Rörstrand — major Swedish ceramic maker. Excellent modernist Scandinavian tradition. Rörstrand (Sweden, founded 1726) is one of Europe's oldest continuous ceramic makers. 20th-century modernist output is highly regarded (Hertha Bengtson, Marianne Westman, others).
A cup has 'MOTTAHEDEH' or similar Persian-sounding brand mark.

Mottahedeh — American maker of high-end reproductions and licensed patterns. Not antique but high-quality reproductions. Mottahedeh (American, founded 1924) produces high-end reproductions and licensed patterns for museums. Distinct from antique originals; contemporary high-tier.
A large ceramic bowl has 'Made in China' with detailed decoration.

Chinese production. Quality ranges widely. Assess by decoration technique and construction quality, not by 'Made in China' alone. 'Made in China' spans from mass-market to premium studio. Chinese porcelain tradition is ancient and continues to produce excellent pieces. Assess by workmanship, not by country of origin alone.
A tea bowl has an intentional single unglazed spot on the interior.

Japanese tea-ceremony piece. Some traditions include specific unglazed spots for wear or tradition. Japanese tea ceremony pottery has specific traditions — some pieces have intentional unglazed spots, deliberate imperfections (wabi-sabi aesthetic), or specific glaze effects. Not defects; intentional.
A plate has visible three small dots on the underside where three tiny stilts supported it during firing.

Three small dots or unglazed spots = modern kiln stilt marks. Standard industrial firing. Modern kiln stilts have three points to support pieces during firing. Small three-dot marks are standard for modern industrial ceramics. Older kilns used different support methods (spurs, sagger stacking).
A large plate has an unglazed rim where the glaze pulled back from the edge.

Glaze pull-back — normal in some ceramic bodies. Not defect; part of production process. Some ceramic bodies have glazes that pull back slightly from edges during firing. Common in certain earthenware and stoneware. Not defect; intentional or accepted characteristic.
A vase has a bright glossy overglaze color painting.

Overglaze enamel decoration. Wears with time and washing. Common on decorative pieces. Overglaze enamels are colorful and vibrant but wear over time. Fired at lower temperature than the base glaze. Common on high-decoration pieces; care in washing preserves.
A plate has a blue color that appears under the clear surface glaze.

Underglaze cobalt decoration. Very durable — must survive glaze firing. Traditional blue-and-white technique. Underglaze decoration is applied to the biscuit-fired piece and covered with clear glaze before final firing. Very durable. Cobalt-blue underglaze is the classic blue-and-white technique across many traditions.
A plate has a rusty brown streak visible in the body when viewed from the foot ring.

Iron content in the clay body. Natural feature of many stoneware and earthenware bodies. Iron content varies in clay bodies. Some deliberately incorporate iron for color; some have natural iron inclusions. Foot rings show body color and any iron inclusions.
A large stoneware crock has a raised decorative sprig applied to the surface.

Sprigged decoration. Small pre-cast decoration applied to the wet body. Wedgwood jasperware tradition; also folk pottery. Sprigged decoration is small pre-cast ornaments applied to the ceramic body. Wedgwood jasperware (colored bodies with white sprigs) is the classic example. Folk pottery also uses sprigging.
A plate has a decoration scratched through a colored slip layer revealing the body color underneath.

Sgraffito decoration. Traditional folk-pottery technique; also modern studio use. Sgraffito ('scratched') is a technique where a layer of colored slip is applied then scratched through to reveal the body color. Traditional Italian, English, and American folk pottery technique.
A large bowl has a hand-thrown asymmetry — one side slightly higher than the other.

Hand-thrown studio pottery. Small asymmetries are characteristic of hand-thrown vs mass-market wheel or slip-cast production. Hand-thrown pieces have subtle asymmetries — one side thicker, height slightly variable, tool marks. These characteristics distinguish from mass-produced pieces. Studio tradition or older utility ware.
A plate is perfectly symmetrical with clean unmarked foot.

Slip-cast or machine-thrown mass production. Perfect symmetry and clean features indicate industrial production. Slip-cast pieces (liquid clay poured into plaster molds) and machine-thrown pieces have perfect symmetry and uniform features. Standard for mass-market. Not automatically lower tier; many premium modern pieces are slip-cast.
A plate has a hand-painted decoration signed with initials in one corner.

Artist-decorated plate. Signature adds provenance value if artist is identifiable. Artist signatures on hand-decorated pieces add value if the artist is identifiable. Research the signature. Some famous decorators' work commands significant premiums.
A vase has 'Roseville' or similar recognizable American art-pottery brand.

American art pottery. Roseville, Weller, Rookwood, McCoy, Fulper, etc. are American art-pottery categories. Collector market varies by maker and line. American art pottery (late 1800s to mid-1900s) is a major collector category. Roseville, Weller, Rookwood, McCoy are prominent names. Values vary dramatically by maker, line, and condition.
A porcelain figurine has extensive lace-like detail formed of thin porcelain strips.

Dresden or Dresden-tradition lace figurine. Real lace was dipped in porcelain slip and fired away, leaving porcelain lace. Fragile and delicate. Dresden porcelain-lace figurines are made by dipping real lace fabric in porcelain slip, then firing. The fabric burns away, leaving porcelain lace. Signature Dresden and German tradition. Very fragile — pieces often show damage.
A tea set has 'Hutschenreuther' with a crown mark and 'Bavaria.'

Hutschenreuther — major German porcelain maker. Bavarian tradition, 19th-20th century. Various lines from traditional to modernist. Hutschenreuther (founded 1814) is a major German porcelain manufacturer. Extensive production across centuries. Values vary by era and line — 19th century pieces often collectible, mid-century modernist popular.
A tea set has 'AYNSLEY' with 'ENGLAND' and 'BONE CHINA.'

Aynsley bone china — English tradition, 1775+. High-end English bone china. Aynsley (founded 1775) is an English bone china maker. Known for fine tea sets, cabinet china, and commemoratives. Higher tier English tradition. Marks vary by era.
A tea set has 'MINTON' with 'ENGLAND' and various period marks.

Minton — major English tradition, founded 1793. Very wide production range. Minton is a major English pottery and porcelain maker. Founded 1793 in Staffordshire. Wide range from utility to luxury. Some lines (Sèvres-style, majolica, encaustic tile) are collector-tier.
A tea set has 'ROYAL WORCESTER' with the Worcester mark.

Royal Worcester — historic English porcelain, founded 1751. Royal Worcester is one of the oldest continuous English porcelain makers (founded 1751). Various hand-painted lines and decorative pieces are collector-tier.
A tea set has 'SPODE' with 'ENGLAND.'

Spode — English bone china tradition, founded 1770 in Staffordshire. Spode (Josiah Spode) invented English bone china formula. Founded 1770. Various signature patterns (Blue Italian, Christmas Tree). Historic English tradition.
A tea set has 'PARAGON' with 'ENGLAND' and 'BONE CHINA.'

Paragon bone china — English maker with royal-family associations. Higher tier English. Paragon (English, various dates) produced fine bone china often associated with royal family commissions. Signature patterns and commemoratives. Higher tier English tradition.
A tea set has 'SHELLEY' with 'ENGLAND' and 'FINE BONE CHINA.'

Shelley — English bone china (1925-1966). Art Deco period specialization. Collector market for Deco-period pieces. Shelley china (1925-1966) has significant collector following for Art Deco period pieces. Specific shapes (Queen Anne, Vogue, Mode) and patterns command premium prices.
A tea set has 'BELLEEK' with tower or harp symbol and 'IRELAND.'

Belleek — Irish porcelain, founded 1857 in County Fermanagh. Distinctive very-thin body, iridescent glaze. Belleek Irish porcelain (founded 1857) has distinctive characteristics: very thin body, subtle iridescent (mother-of-pearl) glaze, delicate designs. Mark evolution over time helps date pieces.
A tea set has 'HAVILAND' with 'LIMOGES' and 'FRANCE.'

Haviland — American-founded Limoges factory. High-end French porcelain production. Wide production range. Haviland (founded by David Haviland, American, moved to Limoges 1842) produces French porcelain for American market. Wide range from traditional to decorative. Various marks by era.
A tea set has 'NORITAKE' with M inside a wreath.

Noritake — major Japanese porcelain maker, founded 1904. Extensive US export market. Noritake (founded 1904 in Nagoya, Japan) has been a major Japanese porcelain maker. Extensive US export production. Older Noritake marks (early 20th century) are more valuable than mid-to-late century.
A tea set has 'FUKAGAWA' or 'Fukugawa' mark with detailed Japanese style.

Fukagawa Seiji — high-end Japanese porcelain maker, Arita. Traditional and decorative pieces. Fukagawa (established 1894 in Arita) is a fine Japanese porcelain maker. Traditional imari-style and decorative pieces. Higher tier Japanese porcelain.
A tea set has 'IMARI' or 'ARITA' style patterns — heavy blue-red-gold decoration.

Imari style — Japanese porcelain tradition from Arita region. Style widely copied throughout Europe (English, French, Dutch imari). Imari (from Arita, Japan) porcelain has bold cobalt-blue, iron-red, and gold decoration. Style copied extensively in Europe (English Imari, French Imari, Dutch Imari). Distinguish original from copy by construction quality.
A tea set has 'KUTANI' or 'YAMAKI' with Japanese overglaze decoration.

Kutani ware — Japanese porcelain tradition from Kutani region. Distinctive overglaze enamel decoration in specific color palettes. Kutani (from Kaga province, Japan) porcelain has specific decorated traditions — Ao-Kutani (blue-green palette), Ko-Kutani (five-color early), various later styles. Historic and modern production both exist.
A large jar has 'CANTON' mark or Canton-style blue-and-white.

Canton ware — Chinese export porcelain, popular for European and American markets. 19th century. Canton ware is a 19th-century Chinese export porcelain, blue-and-white decoration, produced for Western markets. Cheaper tier of Chinese export. Modern reproductions exist.
A large jar has 'ROSE MEDALLION' or Rose Medallion-style decoration.

Rose Medallion — Chinese export style, mid-19th century onward. Distinctive rose-and-figure pattern. Rose Medallion is a specific Chinese export decoration pattern — medallions of figures, birds, and roses, overglaze enamels. Popular in 19th century. Modern reproductions exist.
A large ginger-jar-shape vase has blue-and-white Chinese scholar decoration.

Chinese scholar or bird-and-flower style. Various eras — Kangxi (1662-1722), Qianlong (1735-1796), and modern reproductions. Detailed reference needed for dating. Chinese porcelain reign marks (Kangxi, Qianlong, others) are extensively copied. Genuine pieces command extraordinary prices; reproductions are common. Authentication requires expert review.
A stoneware vase has a wood-fired natural ash glaze — no maker mark.

Studio pottery — wood-fired tradition. Contemporary artisan work. Value by maker recognition and workmanship. Wood-fired stoneware with natural ash deposits is a contemporary studio-pottery tradition. Anagama (Japanese-tradition) and other wood kilns. Value depends on maker recognition and quality.
A tea bowl has crawling texture and orange-peel surface — clearly hand-thrown.

Contemporary or traditional Japanese-tradition tea bowl. Handling marks and glaze character are intentional. Value by maker. Japanese tea ceremony aesthetic values imperfection and character. Studio tea bowls have intentional handling marks, uneven glaze, hand-thrown asymmetries. Maker's reputation drives value.
A large earthenware jug has folk-art decoration and slip-trailed design.

Folk pottery — American or European tradition. Slip-trailed designs are traditional decoration technique. Some folk pieces are collector-tier. Folk pottery traditions (American Southeast, English slipware, French utilitarian) use slip-trailing (piped-on liquid clay decoration). Traditional and studio revivals both exist. Historic pieces often collector-tier.
A plate has 'HEREND' with distinctive Herend blue-fishnet-scale pattern.

Herend — Hungarian porcelain, founded 1826. Highest tier Hungarian porcelain. Fishnet-scale pattern is signature. Herend (Hungarian, founded 1826) is a premier porcelain maker. Fishnet-scale pattern (Vieux Herend) is signature. Highly collectible; wide product range. Verify authenticity with reference.
A tea set has 'AUGARTEN' with 'AUSTRIA' — usually a small dashed shield.

Augarten — historic Austrian porcelain maker. Vienna tradition. High-tier. Augarten porcelain (Austrian) continues the Vienna porcelain tradition. Distinctive small dashed-shield mark. Higher tier Austrian tradition.
A tea set has 'NYMPHENBURG' with distinctive shield mark.

Nymphenburg — Bavarian royal porcelain factory, 1747+. High-tier German tradition. Nymphenburg (founded 1747 in Bavaria) is a royal porcelain factory. High-tier German tradition. Various historical and contemporary pieces. Distinctive shield mark.
A tea set has 'VOLKSTEDT' with crossed pitchforks mark.

Volkstedt — German porcelain, various makers using crossed-pitchforks mark. Thuringian tradition. Volkstedt (Thuringian) porcelain uses crossed-pitchforks mark. Historic and modern production. Various tiers within Volkstedt production.
A tea set has 'DRESDEN' with crown mark.

Dresden porcelain — trade name; multiple German makers used Dresden mark. Verify specific maker with detailed reference. 'Dresden' is a trade name used by many Dresden-area decorators. Multiple factories and studios used various marks. Specific maker identification requires reference. Value varies dramatically.
A saucer has 'FURSTENBERG' with F mark in specific script.

Fürstenberg — German royal porcelain factory, 1747+. High-tier German tradition. Fürstenberg porcelain (founded 1747) is a royal factory in Braunschweig. High-tier German tradition. Various historical periods; modern production continues.
A tea set has 'BING & GRØNDAHL' or B&G mark.

Bing & Grøndahl — Danish porcelain (1853-1987, merged with Royal Copenhagen). Christmas plates most famous. Bing & Grøndahl (Danish) produced fine porcelain 1853-1987, when merged with Royal Copenhagen. Famous for annual Christmas plate series (1895+). Various tiers.
A large plate has 'GIEN' or 'GIEN FRANCE' with maker mark.

Gien — French earthenware maker, founded 1821. High-tier French earthenware, extensive decorative tradition. Gien (French, founded 1821) is a fine earthenware maker. High-tier French tradition with extensive decoration. Various patterns including some Sèvres-style hand-painted work.
A stoneware bowl has 'THROWN BY [ARTIST NAME]' and location mark.

Studio pottery. Artist attribution adds value if artist is identifiable. Verify with reference or gallery lookup. Studio pottery with artist attribution can range widely in value based on artist reputation. Reference lookup identifies market value. Famous studio potters (do not name-drop) command premium.
A tea set has 'PORTMEIRION' with 'ENGLAND.'

Portmeirion — English tableware, founded 1960. Signature Botanic Garden pattern very popular. Portmeirion (English, founded 1960) is a major tableware maker. Signature Botanic Garden pattern (1972+) very popular. Various other patterns. Modern tradition.
A tea set has 'DENBY' with 'ENGLAND' and country-style stoneware.

Denby — English stoneware, founded 1809 in Derbyshire. Utility and casual dinnerware tradition. Denby (English, founded 1809) is a stoneware maker. Casual dinnerware tradition. Various signature patterns (Greenwheat, Studio Craft). Mass-market to mid-tier.
A tea set has 'MOORCROFT' with signed mark and William Moorcroft signature style.

Moorcroft — English art pottery, distinctive tube-lined slip decoration. Signed and dated pieces. Collector market. Moorcroft (English, 1897+) is signature art pottery. Tube-lined slip decoration is distinctive. Various eras; W. Moorcroft period (1897-1945) most valuable, later production also collectible.
A large plate has 'CLARICE CLIFF' or Bizarre pattern signature.

Clarice Cliff — English Art Deco potter (1899-1972). Bizarre range highly collectible. Extensive fakes. Clarice Cliff's Bizarre range (1929+) is highly collectible Art Deco pottery. Extensively faked. Genuine pieces have specific hand-painted characteristics. Reference authentication essential.
A large plate has 'SUSIE COOPER' with signature or brand mark.

Susie Cooper — English 20th-century designer. Extensive tableware production. Some pieces collector-tier. Susie Cooper (1902-1995) is an English 20th-century pottery and tableware designer. Various companies (Susie Cooper Pottery, later Wedgwood collaboration). Some designs collector-tier.
A tea set has 'IITTALA' with distinctive shape.

Iittala — Finnish glass and ceramic maker. Modernist tradition. Some ceramic collaborations with Arabia. Iittala (Finnish, primarily glass) has also produced ceramics through collaborations with Arabia. Modernist Scandinavian tradition. Various product lines.
A stoneware pot has natural celadon-green with visible reduction firing character.

Contemporary or traditional celadon studio piece. Reduction-fired for signature blue-green. Maker-driven value. Celadon requires reduction firing (low-oxygen atmosphere). Traditional Chinese, Korean, and Japanese producers plus modern studio. Value depends on maker recognition, glaze quality, and body construction.
A tea bowl has hand-painted overglaze enamels in bright red-blue-green Chinese style.

Chinese or Chinese-tradition overglaze enameled piece. Multi-fired (glaze + enamel firings). Value by tier. Chinese overglaze enamels (wucai, doucai, famille rose, famille verte) are traditional multi-fired decoration techniques. Various historical periods and modern production.
A tea bowl has hand-painted overglaze famille-rose (pink dominant) decoration.
