A wrench stamped 'SNAP-ON' with model number SW706.

Snap-On combination wrench, model SW706 = 3/16 inch open/box combination. Premium brand. Snap-On wrenches command premium resale (3-10× commodity). Model numbers identify exact tool. Verify authenticity — counterfeit Snap-On exists.
A wrench stamped 'CRAFTSMAN' with 'USA' underneath, older cross-hatch pattern.

Older Craftsman USA-made. Higher tier than modern Craftsman. Lifetime warranty tradition. Older Craftsman (USA-made, pre-2010ish) has higher resale value than modern China-made Craftsman. Cross-hatch pattern and USA mark date the piece.
A cordless drill in a case with battery and charger, brand 'DEWALT.'

Complete DeWalt cordless drill set. Verify battery voltage and condition; charger included is a plus. Complete cordless-tool sets (tool + battery + charger + case) have higher resale than tool-only. Battery voltage matters — 12V, 18/20V, 60V are not cross-compatible.
A cordless drill without battery, brand 'MILWAUKEE M18.'

Milwaukee M18 platform (18V nominal, 20V max). Bare tool. Add cost of 18V battery ($100+ for genuine, $50+ third-party). Bare-tool listings are common on cordless tools. M18 batteries are widely available. Discount from complete-set price by battery cost.
A cordless drill with an obsolete battery format — 14.4V NiCad.

Obsolete platform. 14.4V NiCad batteries limited availability. Tool value primarily for parts or specific compatibility needs. Older cordless-tool platforms (14.4V, some 12V older) use batteries no longer widely manufactured. Newer lithium-ion batteries incompatible. Tool value limited.
A socket set with sockets marked '6-point' or hexagonal recess.

6-point sockets — professional preference. Better grip on rounded or damaged fasteners. 6-point vs 12-point sockets: 6-point (hex recess) grips flat surfaces of hex fasteners; 12-point (double-hex) more versatile but less grip on rounded/damaged fasteners. 6-point preferred by professionals.
A socket with black oxide finish, thicker walls than standard sockets.

Impact-rated socket. Safe to use with impact wrench. Chrome (shiny) sockets can crack under impact use. Impact sockets have thicker walls and are heat-treated for impact use. Black oxide finish indicates impact rating. Chrome sockets are hand-tool only — using with impact drivers is dangerous (can shatter).
A hand-plane with 'STANLEY BAILEY' cast into the body, wood tote handle.

Vintage Stanley Bailey hand-plane. Various models (No. 3-8). Highly collectible, still functional. Stanley Bailey hand-planes (early 1900s onward) are collector-tier vintage tools. Model number identifies size. Various eras; pre-WWII generally higher tier.
A chisel with 'MARPLES' stamped into the blade, boxwood handle.

Marples Blue Chip or similar — quality English chisel. Boxwood handle is traditional. Marples chisels are quality English tools. Blue Chip series is common. Boxwood handles are traditional and higher tier than plastic handles.
A hammer with 'ESTWING' cast into head, one-piece steel construction with leather-wrapped handle.

Estwing one-piece hammer. Premium American brand. One-piece forged steel; no handle-head separation ever. Estwing hammers are one-piece forged steel — head, shaft, and grip in one piece. Cannot separate. Leather-wrapped handle is signature. Premium brand.
A screwdriver with 'WERA' mark and Torx-tipped shaft.

Wera premium German screwdriver. Various models. Torx tip. Higher tier than commodity screwdrivers. Wera is premium German screwdriver/bit brand. Distinctive Kraftform handles. Torx variant is common. Higher tier than commodity brands.
A pair of pliers with 'KLEIN TOOLS' stamped, insulated red handles.

Klein linesman pliers, insulated. Electrician-standard. High resale value. Klein Tools are the American electrician standard. Insulated handles (typically red or yellow) indicate electrical-work rating. High resale.
A pair of channel-lock pliers with 'CHANNELLOCK' mark and blue handle grips.

Channellock brand slip-joint pliers. American tradition. Various sizes. Premium tier. Channellock is the original American slip-joint pliers brand. Blue handles are signature. Model 420 (9.5-inch) is most common. Higher tier than commodity 'channel lock' pliers.
A miter saw with 'DEWALT' and 12-inch blade capacity.

DeWalt 12-inch miter saw. Professional/prosumer tier. Verify functionality — motor, laser, blade guard. DeWalt is a major power-tool brand. 12-inch capacity is mid-large miter saw. Professional/prosumer tier. Test all functions before buying.
A circular saw with 'MAKITA' and 7-1/4-inch blade capacity.

Makita 7-1/4 circular saw. Professional-tier saw. Standard blade size for framing. Makita is Japanese premium power-tool brand. 7-1/4 inch is standard framing circular saw size. Professional tier. Test motor, blade guard, adjustments.
A table saw with 'SAWSTOP' brand.

SawStop table saw. Premium tier with automatic blade-stop safety technology. Very high resale. SawStop table saws have unique safety technology (blade stops within milliseconds of contact). Highest tier of consumer/prosumer table saws. Extremely high resale value.
A wrench with visibly rounded corners at the box end.

Worn wrench. Will slip on fasteners. Reduced usable life; commodity value only. Worn box-end wrenches (rounded internal corners) slip on fasteners. Even premium brands eventually wear. Discard or use only where slipping is acceptable.
A ratchet that doesn't ratchet — internal mechanism worn or broken.

Broken ratchet mechanism. Repairable in some models (rebuild kits available); often more economical to replace. Ratchet mechanisms wear over time. Premium brands (Snap-On, some others) have rebuild kits. Commodity ratchets usually not economical to repair.
A saw with several teeth broken off the blade.

Damaged saw. Compromised cutting. Resharpen if possible; consider replacement of blade or entire saw. Missing/broken teeth compromise saw cutting. Some sharpening services can restore; others cannot. Replacement blades for common circular/miter/table saws are affordable.
A hammer head shows visible signs of chips at the striking face.

Damaged hammer face. Safety hazard — chips can spall further. Replace before use. Hammer face chips are safety hazards — flying metal chips during use can injure. Replace damaged hammers. Never use chipped or spalled hammers.
A chisel with mushroomed head (deformed and expanded from hammer strikes).

Struck repeatedly with hammer — mushrooming is normal wear. Regrind to remove mushroom before further use (spalls can shatter). Mushroomed chisel heads are common on hand-struck chisels. Grinding off the mushroom before use is standard safety practice. Prevents spalls.
A power cord on an angle grinder has been repaired with electrical tape.

Repaired cord. Safety concern. Replace cord or discard tool for safety. Repaired power cords are safety hazards. Insulation compromise risks shock. Replace cord (specialized tool cords available) or discard tool. Don't use taped cords.
A drill has a chuck key permanently attached to the cord (older tool).

Chuck-key drill. Older design. Chuck key must be present or drill can't be tightened. Older but functional. Older drills had removable chucks requiring keys. Losing the key renders the drill largely unusable. Keys are widely available replacement. Modern drills have keyless chucks.
A cordless tool battery is visibly swollen, bulging out of the housing.

Failed lithium battery. Fire/explosion risk. Disposal required (hazardous waste). Do not use or charge. Swollen lithium batteries are dangerous — can catch fire or explode. Never charge or use. Dispose at hazardous-waste facility or battery-recycling program. Replace with new battery.
A wrench set with '1/4 through 1 inch' or metric 6-24mm complete set.

Complete SAE or metric wrench set. Higher resale than partial sets. Full range covers most common fasteners. Complete tool sets have higher resale value. Missing sizes reduce value. Sets in original storage case sell better than loose.
A pair of vise-grip pliers with visible rust on the jaws but working mechanism.

Surface rust removable with wire brush and oil. Functional vise-grips are useful. Vintage vise-grips are collectible. Vise-Grip (original Peterson brand) locking pliers are workshop essentials. Surface rust is cosmetic; internal mechanism function matters. Vintage USA-made are collectible.
A hand-plane with visible pitting under the rust on the sole.

Restorable but reduced value. Pitting after rust removal shows on the working surface. Value below clean-body examples. Rust removal reveals pitting on the working surface. Deep pitting reduces plane function; light pitting is cosmetic. Assess after restoration.
A file has teeth still sharp when tested against a nail.

Functional file. Nail test — if file cuts nail, teeth are still sharp. Rusty files can sometimes be cleaned. File test: try cutting a nail with a file corner. Sharp file cuts easily. Dull file just slides. Rusty files can be cleaned (vinegar soak, wire brush); severely worn files should be replaced.
A drill has a variable-speed trigger that works smoothly through the full range.

Functional variable-speed drill. Standard modern drill feature. Test the trigger response. Variable-speed triggers should respond smoothly through the range. Sudden jumps or dead zones indicate switch failure. Trigger switches are replaceable.
A drill trigger sparks visibly when squeezed.

Trigger switch failing or brushed motor showing normal brush wear. Investigate. Sparking triggers indicate switch failure or brush wear. Brushed motors normally show some sparking at brushes (visible through inspection cover); visible sparking at trigger is switch failure.

Motor overload protection engaging. Could indicate normal (thick material) or motor issue (weak from wear). Motor overload protection stops the tool to prevent damage. Normal on high-load cuts in thick material. Frequent stalling on light cuts indicates motor weakness.
A circular saw blade guard doesn't retract fully when the saw is upright.

Sticky blade guard. Safety issue — guard must retract to allow cutting. Clean pivot mechanism, spring may need replacement. Blade guards should retract smoothly. Sawdust and pitch buildup can restrict movement. Cleaning solves most issues; spring failure requires replacement.
A drill has a hammer function switch (drill/hammer/screwdriver modes).

Combi drill (hammer drill). Can drill concrete/masonry in hammer mode. Regular drill in drill mode. Screwdriver mode limits torque. Combi drills combine multiple functions. Hammer mode for concrete/masonry; regular drill for wood/metal; screwdriver mode limits torque for fasteners. Full-featured drill.
A drill has 'brushless' printed on the housing.

Brushless motor drill. More efficient, longer life, less maintenance than brushed motor. Modern feature. Brushless motors are newer, more efficient, longer-lasting. No carbon brushes to replace. Higher tier than brushed motors typically. Common on modern cordless tools.
An angle grinder has a specific disc installed — thin cutoff wheel.

Cutoff wheel-equipped angle grinder. Wheel is a consumable — sold in packs. Grinder can accept various disc types. Angle grinders accept many disc types — cutoff wheels, grinding wheels, flap discs, wire brushes. Disc is a consumable. Verify wheel matches intended use and grinder RPM rating.
A router bit has visible chips or damage on the cutting edges.

Damaged router bit. Poor cut quality; potentially dangerous if bit fails during use. Replace. Router bits with damaged cutting edges produce poor cuts and can fail dangerously at high RPM. Replace damaged bits. Bit sharpening exists but not universally economical.
A miter saw base shows visible dust and pitch buildup on the pivot mechanism.

Needs cleaning. Common maintenance. Buildup restricts smooth operation. Miter saw pivot mechanisms accumulate sawdust and pitch. Regular cleaning maintains smooth operation. Compressed air, brush, and possibly light lubricant restore function.
A table saw fence doesn't lock parallel to the blade — visible offset.

Fence alignment issue. Requires calibration to be parallel with blade. Common maintenance issue; some fences easier to adjust than others. A shop vacuum has a permanent tear in the collection bag.

Bag replacement needed. Consumable item. Various bag sizes for different vacuum models. Shop vacuum bags are consumables. Torn or overfilled bags reduce suction and can release dust. Replace with correct size and type for the vacuum. Some models use HEPA bags.
A pair of side-cutting pliers is stiff, doesn't spring open when released.

Spring failure or corrosion in pivot. Clean pivot, add light oil; if spring failure, replace pliers. Side-cutting pliers rely on internal spring to return to open position. Corrosion or spring failure both cause stiffness. Clean pivot with penetrating oil first.

A drill press has a large table (18 inches or wider) and clear laser guide.

A welding machine says 'MIG 200A' with argon-gas hookup.

Matco Tools — professional brand tier, similar tier to Mac Tools. Slightly less resale than Snap-On but premium. Matco Tools is a professional brand (competing with Snap-On, Mac). Sold primarily through mobile trucks. Premium tier resale. Similar quality to Mac Tools.

SDS-Plus rotary hammer. SDS (Slotted Drive System) chuck for masonry bits. Various SDS variants (SDS-Plus, SDS-Max) for different bit sizes. SDS chuck systems: SDS-Plus (common, medium sizes), SDS-Max (larger bits, more power), SDS-Top (rare). Bits are not cross-compatible between systems.
A wood chisel has 'BLUE SPRUCE TOOLWORKS' or similar boutique maker signature.

Boutique premium chisel maker. Very high tier. High resale. Boutique chisel makers (Blue Spruce, Sterling, Barr, others) command premium prices in woodworking market. Very high tier. Individually made or small-batch. Very high resale.
A hand saw has 'BAD AXE' or 'WEN-ZLOFF' brand — modern boutique maker.

Boutique hand saw. Very high tier modern hand saw. Premium resale. Boutique hand saw makers (Bad Axe Tool Works, Wenzloff and Sons, others) make premium hand saws in the tradition of vintage saws. Very high tier. High resale.
A saw sharpening service has been done recently — teeth are sharp and set.

Recently sharpened hand saw. Ready to use. Sharp teeth, proper set. Premium user's or reseller's condition. Hand saw sharpening includes filing teeth to proper angle and setting alternating teeth left/right. Recently sharpened saw is ready for use. Sharpening services $15-30 for hand saws.
A miter saw has laser guide and shadow-cast dust-line feature.

Modern miter saw with advanced features. Prosumer/pro tier. Shadow-cast dust line is a specific Kapex-style feature. Shadow-cast dust lines (blade-shadow visible line) are more accurate than laser guides for cutting to line. Introduced by Festool Kapex; other brands added. Premium feature.
A power tool has 'M12' Milwaukee 12V compact platform mark.

Milwaukee M12 platform. Compact 12V. Multiple tools in platform. Batteries cross-compatible within M12. Milwaukee M12 = compact 12V platform. Various tools: drill, impact driver, sub-compact circular saw, band saw, right-angle drill, etc. Batteries share within platform. Distinct from M18.
A large workshop tool is a 'JOINTER' with 8-inch cutter head.

8-inch jointer for edge and face preparation of lumber. Serious woodworking tool. Verify motor and cutter head condition. Jointers prepare edges and faces of lumber for gluing. 6-inch = homeshop; 8-inch = serious woodworking; 12-inch+ = professional. Cutter head condition (blade sharpness) matters.
A large workshop tool is a 'PLANER' or 'THICKNESSER' with 20-inch capacity.

Large-capacity thickness planer. Handles wide boards. Professional or serious hobbyist tool. Motor and cutter head are key. Thickness planers reduce lumber to uniform thickness. Consumer sizes 12-13-inch; larger 15-20+ inch professional. Cutter head (straight blades vs helical) affects finish and blade maintenance.
A large workshop tool is a 'BANDSAW' with 17-inch or larger throat capacity.

Larger bandsaw. Handles thick resawing and larger workpieces. Prosumer or professional. Bandsaw throat capacity (distance from blade to column) determines maximum resaw width. 14-inch entry; 17-18-inch prosumer; 20+ professional. Larger requires more space and higher motor rating.
A vintage wood-working plane has 'RECORD' English mark.

Record — English plane maker, 20th century (also chisels, vises). Various tiers. Older Record often preferred to newer. Record (English) made hand planes and other woodworking tools. Older Record (pre-1990s) often preferred to newer production. Various models parallel to Stanley.
A hand tool marked 'PILING WEDGE' or specific specialized use.

Specialty tool. Value depends on collector market for that specific tool type. Reference lookup for niche tools. Specialty tools (piling wedges, spokeshaves, specialized planes, uncommon chisels) have narrow collector markets. Some highly valued by specific specialties. Reference lookup for niche tools.
A power tool platform is 'RYOBI' 18V+ or 'KOBALT' with battery.

Consumer tier cordless platforms. Adequate for occasional homeowner use. Lower tier than professional brands. Ryobi and Kobalt are consumer-tier cordless platforms. Adequate for occasional use. Lower tier resale than professional (Milwaukee, DeWalt, Makita). Ryobi has wide tool selection.
A jigsaw has 'BOSCH BARREL-GRIP' handle design.

Bosch premium jigsaw. Barrel-grip design. Precise cutting. High tier. Bosch jigsaws come in barrel-grip and top-handle designs. Barrel-grip preferred for detail work. Premium models offer variable orbital action, tool-less blade change. High tier.
A saw has aluminum body vs steel body construction.

Aluminum body is lighter, easier to handle for long use. Steel body heavier, potentially more durable. Depends on application. Modern circular saws often use aluminum or magnesium construction for weight reduction. Steel is more durable but heavier. Aluminum shoes/bases lighter but easier to bend.
A power tool has 'MADE IN GERMANY' or 'MADE IN JAPAN' origin.

Country of manufacture is a quality signal for older tools. Older Japanese and German tools often higher tier than same brand now made in China. Manufacturing location has shifted for many brands. Older Japan-made Makita, Germany-made Bosch, are often higher tier than newer China-made same brands. Verify by production era.
A cordless tool has 'brushless' AND 'lithium-ion' features.

Modern cordless tool. Both brushless motor (efficient, long-life) and lithium-ion battery (no memory effect, high energy density). Post-2010 standard. Modern cordless tools combine brushless motors with lithium-ion batteries. Higher efficiency, longer runtime, longer tool life. Standard on newer tools. Older NiCad/NiMH plus brushed motor is less desirable.
A cordless tool battery has 'Li-ion 5.0Ah' or higher amp-hour rating.

Higher-capacity battery. Longer runtime per charge. 5.0Ah is high end; 6.0-12.0Ah exist for demanding tools. Weighs more. Battery amp-hour ratings: 1.5-2.0Ah (compact), 4.0-5.0Ah (standard), 6.0-12.0Ah (high-capacity for demanding tools). Higher Ah = longer runtime, more weight, more cost.
A cordless tool battery is 'compact' size — same voltage but half normal size.

Compact battery. Reduces tool weight and size. Half runtime of standard. Useful for lightweight tasks. Compact batteries (M18 Compact, DeWalt Compact, etc.) offer half the runtime but reduced weight. Useful for overhead work or tools where weight matters more than runtime. Same voltage.
A cordless tool has 'HIGH OUTPUT' or 'MX FUEL' Milwaukee mark.

Milwaukee MX FUEL is a higher voltage platform (72V nominal) for concrete/utility trade tools. Higher tier than M18. Different battery. Milwaukee MX FUEL is separate high-voltage platform (72V) for demolition tools, portable power. Larger batteries. Not compatible with M18 or M12. Concrete and heavy-duty applications.
A cordless tool has 'FLEXVOLT ADVANTAGE' or 'FlexVolt' DeWalt marking.

DeWalt FlexVolt = 60V platform, batteries also work in 20V tools (variable voltage). DeWalt FlexVolt batteries are physically 60V (15 cells) but can act as 20V for older 20V tools. Cross-compatibility for battery investment protection. FlexVolt tools take advantage of full 60V.
A hand-plane has been recently sharpened — mirror-polish on the back.

Recently sharpened plane. Bevel and back both prepared. Ready to use. High-tier user prep. Hand plane sharpening includes flattening and polishing the back of the blade, then honing the bevel to sharpness. Mirror polish indicates thorough sharpening. Time-consuming preparation.
A vintage tool has 'PAT. PEND.' stamped.

Patent pending — early production before patent granted. Dating hint. Early production sometimes preferred by collectors. Patent pending stamps date tools to before patent grant. Early production numbers sometimes preferred. Combined with other dating features (medallions, mark styles), narrows era.
A vintage tool has 'PAT. [date]' stamped.

Patented [date]. Reference lookup identifies patent number and era. Adds provenance. Patent dates on tools identify when a specific feature was patented. Reference lookup identifies specific patent and often narrower production window. Adds provenance information.
A cordless drill has visible corrosion on battery contacts (both tool and battery sides).

Corrosion issue. Clean with contact cleaner and light abrasive. Some corrosion permanent. Battery contact corrosion is common on stored tools. Clean with contact cleaner (electronics-grade) and light abrasive (pencil eraser). Severe corrosion may require replacement.
A cordless drill's chuck won't tighten fully — bit slips when torque applied.

Worn chuck jaws. Some chucks can be tightened by hand-turning while trigger is held; others require replacement chuck. Chucks wear over years. Some tighten by holding trigger while manually rotating chuck (double-tighten). Persistent slipping needs replacement chuck ($20-100 depending on model).
A drill's variable speed clutch has visible torque-setting numbers 1-20+.

Clutch drill. Torque settings prevent over-driving fasteners. Modern drill feature. Higher numbers = more torque. Clutch drills slip at set torque, preventing over-driving. Settings vary; drill mode bypasses clutch for maximum torque. Modern drill feature. Number of settings varies (12-25 typical).
An impact driver has hex-shank quick-change chuck.

Standard impact driver. Hex bits (1/4-inch hex shank) snap in and out quickly. Different from drill's Jacobs chuck. Impact drivers use 1/4-inch hex-shank quick-change chucks. Accepts bits and adapter shanks. Different from drill's 3-jaw chuck. Bits interchange with impact-rated hex-shank drivers.
An impact driver has visible ring for anti-drop safety (attaches lanyard).

Modern impact driver with anti-drop safety feature. Common on tools sold for elevated-work applications. Some brands standard. Anti-drop rings (Milwaukee, DeWalt, others) allow lanyard attachment for elevated work. Prevents drops from height. Some brands include as standard; others as premium feature.
A large chop saw has 'DRY-CUT' metal-cutting label.

Dry-cut metal saw. Cuts steel without coolant. Special blade required. Different from wood-cutting chop saws. Dry-cut metal-cutting chop saws are dedicated for cutting steel. Different from wood chop saws (miter saws). Different from cutoff saws (abrasive wheel). Long carbide-tooth blade specific.
A large workshop tool is a 'CNC ROUTER' with dedicated software.

CNC router. Computer-numerically-controlled woodworking. Premium tier hobbyist to professional. Specific software (Vectric, others) may be required. CNC routers use computer control for automated cutting. Consumer/hobbyist ($1500-5000) to professional ($10000+). Software separate purchase typically. Learning curve for full utilization.
A large workshop tool is a '3D PRINTER' with FDM or SLA technology.

3D printer. Various technologies: FDM (filament), SLA (resin), SLS (powder). Consumer to professional. 3D printers: FDM (filament, common consumer) $200-3000; SLA (resin) $200-2000; SLS/DMLS professional. Various brands and quality tiers. Verify all functions and consumables.
A hand tool marked 'WESTCOTT' or 'FISKARS' with specific model.

Westcott or Fiskars are known brands for scissors, pruners, other cutting tools. Various tiers within each. Westcott and Fiskars make various cutting tools (scissors, garden pruners, snips). Some models premium tier; others commodity. Reference specific model for tier.
A tool has 'IRWIN' brand with various tool types (chisels, clamps, saws, drill bits).

Irwin — American tool brand across multiple categories. Various tiers within Irwin (Quick-Grip clamps, Marples chisels acquired, Speedbor drill bits). Irwin makes many tool types across tiers. Quick-Grip bar clamps are premium; some other Irwin lines mid-tier. Marples chisel line (acquired) retains premium status. Verify specific line.
A drill bit set has 'INDEX' organization — bits sorted by size in labeled slots.

Indexed drill bit set. Higher tier organization. Full range from 1/16 to 1/2 (SAE) or 1-13mm (metric) common. Indexed sets have organized storage — each size in labeled slot. Full sets (1/16-1/2 in 32nds, or 1-13mm in 0.5mm increments) more complete. Higher tier organization; drill bits still separately assessed for wear.
A drill bit is 'HSS' or 'HIGH-SPEED STEEL' marked.

HSS drill bit. Standard for drilling metal and wood. Various grades within HSS (M2, M7, cobalt variants). HSS (high-speed steel) is the standard drill bit material. Cobalt HSS (M35, M42) is harder — better for hardened metals. TiN or TiCN coatings extend life. Reference for specific alloy details.
A large drill press has 'JET' or 'POWERMATIC' brand marks.

Jet and Powermatic — American stationary-tool brands. Prosumer/pro tier. Verify all functions. Jet (mid-tier) and Powermatic (premium) are American stationary-tool brands (drill presses, lathes, planers, bandsaws). Powermatic is the higher tier within same corporate ownership.
A wood-lathe has 'ONEWAY' or 'ROBUST' brand — premium tier.

Oneway or Robust — premium American wood-lathe brands. Very high tier. High resale. Oneway (Canadian) and Robust (American) are premium wood-lathe brands. Very high tier. Preferred by serious wood-turners. High resale value.
A wood-turning chisel has 'SORBY' or 'CROWN' English tool mark.

Sorby or Crown — English wood-turning chisel makers. Premium tier. HSS blades. Sorby and Crown are premier English wood-turning tool makers. Various profiles (gouges, skews, scrapers). Premium tier. Sharpening with jigs preserves geometry.
A wood-turning tool has 'CARBIDE INSERT' cutter.

Carbide-insert wood-turning tool. Modern innovation. Replaceable inserts vs traditional HSS grinding. Both traditions have merits. Carbide-insert wood-turning tools (Easy Wood Tools, Hunter, others) replace traditional HSS chisels for some turners. Replaceable inserts; no grinding needed. Traditional HSS still preferred for many profiles.
A hand-plane blade steel is marked 'A2' or 'PM-V11' or 'HIGH-CARBON.'

Modern blade steels vary: A2 tool steel (holds edge longer, harder to sharpen), PM-V11 (Veritas modern, edge retention), high-carbon (traditional, easy to sharpen). Different tradeoffs. Blade steel matters for hand tools. High-carbon (O1): traditional, easy sharpening, good edge but less durable. A2 tool steel: harder, longer edge retention, harder to sharpen. PM-V11 (powder-metal): premium modern, best edge retention. Each has tradeoffs.
A vintage tool box has 'GERSTNER' brand — nice wooden construction with drawers.

Gerstner tool chest — premium American wooden tool chest. Extensive drawer organization. High tier. Gerstner (American, 1906+) makes premium wooden tool chests. Various sizes with multiple drawers. Machinist tradition. Higher tier storage; high resale value. Vintage Gerstner especially prized.
A hand plane has 'BEDROCK' style with locking cap.

Stanley Bedrock series or copy. Premium Stanley plane series with better frog-to-body registration. Modern makers copy this design. Stanley Bedrock series (605, 606, 607, 608 corresponding to Bailey numbers) had improved frog-body registration for reduced blade chatter. Premium Stanley tier. Modern makers (Lie-Nielsen, Wood River) use bedrock-style design.
A tool has 'RIGHT ANGLE' extension for drill applications.

Right-angle drill attachment. Fits between drill and bit for tight-space access. Standard accessory. Right-angle drill attachments convert straight drilling to 90-degree drilling. Access tight spots. Standard drill accessory. Some tools (right-angle drills) are dedicated designs.
A tool has 'FLEXIBLE SHAFT' extension for detail work.

Flexible-shaft extension for rotary tools (Dremel-style). Detailed carving, engraving. Standard accessory. Flexible-shaft extensions for rotary tools enable detailed work with pen-like handling. Common on Dremel-style tools. Standard consumable-tier accessory.
A large cordless tool is 'RIDING LAWN MOWER' battery-powered.

Battery-electric riding mower. Various brands. Modern quiet electric alternative to gas. Battery-electric riding mowers are increasingly available. Various brands (Ryobi, EGO, Ariens, others). Quiet, no emissions, but limited runtime and cost more upfront than gas equivalents.
A power tool has 'CABLE' rated for outdoor use with grounded plug.

Outdoor-rated power cord. GFCI outlet required for safety. Standard on outdoor power tools. Outdoor-rated power cords have specific insulation ratings. GFCI outlets required for outdoor use. Standard on many outdoor tools.
A router has 'PLUNGE BASE' feature.

Plunge router. Bit descends into workpiece from above. Alternative to fixed-base routers (bit height locked). Plunge routers have base that allows the bit to descend into the work from above. Useful for mortises, inlay pockets. Fixed-base routers have bit fixed at set height. Some routers convert between.
A router has 'FIXED BASE' with adjustable height ring.

Fixed-base router. Standard router configuration. Bit height set by adjusting ring. Fixed-base routers hold the bit at a set height. Standard configuration for edge profiles and general routing. Some kit routers include both plunge and fixed bases.
A router bit is '1/2 SHANK' vs '1/4 SHANK.'

Shank size matters. 1/2-inch shank routers have more torque and less runout than 1/4-inch. Bit selection must match router collet. Router bit shanks: 1/4-inch (compact routers, some fixed-base), 1/2-inch (larger routers, table routers). 1/2-inch has better torque, less runout, better cuts. Match bit shank to router collet.
A router bit has 'BEARING' at the tip or shank end.

Bearing-guided router bit. Bearing follows an edge template for consistent routing. Standard for edge profiles. Bearing-guided router bits use a small bearing to follow a template edge. Consistent routing along irregular templates. Common on edge-forming bits, pattern bits, flush trim bits.
A saw blade has 'ATB' or 'FLAT-TOP' tooth grind visible.

Tooth grind indicates cut type: ATB (Alternate Top Bevel) is common for wood cross-cuts; Flat-Top is for ripping wood. Various grinds for different uses. Circular saw blade tooth grinds: ATB (cross-cut wood, cleaner), Flat-Top (rip wood, faster), Combination (mixed use), Triple-Chip (metals, laminates, plastics). Match blade to material.
A shop is set up with 'DUST COLLECTION' network.

Central dust collection system. Prosumer/pro tier. Higher-end shops have dedicated system with individual gates per tool. Dust collection improves shop health and cleanup. Individual tool dust ports connect via flexible or rigid ducts to central collector. Gates per tool control airflow. Cyclones improve efficiency.
A shop dust collector has 'CYCLONE' pre-separator.

Cyclone dust collection. Pre-separates large debris from filter. More efficient than single-stage. Higher tier. Cyclone dust collectors have two-stage separation — cyclone pre-separator drops most debris before airstream reaches filter. Extends filter life, better airflow, more efficient. Higher tier.
A shop has 'COMPRESSED AIR' network with pipe distribution.

Professional or serious hobbyist shop. Compressed air network distributes to multiple tools. Pipe material and sizing matter. Distributed compressed air systems use pipe (copper, PEX, or steel) to distribute compressed air throughout shop. Pipe sizing matters (larger for less pressure drop). Quick-connect fittings at drops.
A large workshop tool is a 'WELDER' with three-phase power requirement.

Three-phase welder. Higher amperage capability than single-phase. Requires three-phase electrical service (commercial/industrial). Three-phase welders offer higher amperage and smoother output than single-phase. Requires three-phase electrical service (not standard residential). Common in industrial and commercial settings.
A welder has 'TIG' capability with tungsten electrode.

TIG (Tungsten Inert Gas) welder. Precise, clean welding. Different from MIG (Metal Inert Gas). Requires more skill. TIG welding uses non-consumable tungsten electrode plus separate filler rod. Very precise, clean welds. Steeper learning curve than MIG. Common for stainless, aluminum, precision work.
A welder is 'STICK' or 'SMAW' — electrode-holder welding.

Stick welding (Shielded Metal Arc Welding). Simple, versatile, outdoor-capable. Traditional welding method. Stick welding uses consumable electrode with flux coating. Simple equipment, outdoor-capable (flux provides shielding, not gas). Traditional method. Higher-amperage for thicker materials.
A metal-lathe has 'SOUTH BEND' brand — vintage American.

South Bend metal lathes. American, various eras. Vintage models often preferred for quality; some pre-WWII collectible. South Bend metal lathes (American, 1906+) are respected vintage machine tools. Various sizes and models. Pre-WWII pieces sometimes preferred. Modern South Bend brand exists under different ownership.
A machining chuck has 'JACOBS' mark with specific model number.

Jacobs — premier US drill chuck maker. Various models. Model number identifies exact tool. Higher tier. Jacobs (American) makes premium drill chucks. Various models identified by number. Higher tier than commodity chucks. Precision, longer life. Common on quality drill presses.
A large metal-working tool has 'BRIDGEPORT' vertical milling machine.

Bridgeport mill — the classic American vertical milling machine. Iconic. Many still in service after 50+ years. High tier vintage; still valuable. Bridgeport vertical milling machines (American, 1938+) are the iconic knee mill. Extensively copied by other makers. Vintage Bridgeports still in daily service; parts and manuals widely available. Very high tier.
A CNC mill has 'HAAS' brand — American CNC machine tool.

Haas Automation — American CNC machine tool maker. Various tiers within Haas. Widely used for CNC milling and turning. Haas Automation (American, 1983+) makes CNC machine tools. Various tiers (VF series, mini-mills, lathes). Widely used in production and educational settings. Some level of remote diagnostic support.
A hand tool has 'MADE IN TAIWAN' vs 'MADE IN CHINA' vs 'MADE IN USA.'

Country of manufacture is a signal — Taiwan often mid-tier, China varies widely, USA/Germany/Japan often higher tier for tool brands. Country of manufacture: Taiwan is often solid mid-tier; China varies from excellent to poor within same brand; USA/Germany/Japan often higher tier for tool brands. Brand-specific patterns.
A tool box has 'KLEIN LEATHER TOOL BAG' or similar leather trade bag.

Leather trade bag — professional electrician or utility-work standard. Klein makes signature designs. Leather ages well. Leather tool bags (Klein, Occidental Leather, others) are professional-tier trade bags. Wear well, age nicely. Higher tier than fabric or plastic bags. Higher resale.
A tool bag has 'OCCIDENTAL LEATHER' brand.

Occidental Leather — premium leather tool bags and belts. American made. High tier for trade professionals. Occidental Leather (American) makes premium leather tool bags, belts, and holsters. High tier for trade professionals. Various configurations for different trades.
A machinist tool set has 'BROWN & SHARPE' mark.

Brown & Sharpe — premier American precision tool maker. Various tools (mics, calipers, indicators, gauges). High tier. Brown & Sharpe (American, 1833+) makes precision measuring tools. Micrometers, calipers, gauges. High tier, similar tier to Starrett. Vintage Brown & Sharpe often preferred.
A hand tool has 'MATCHED SET' provenance — series with matching serial numbers.

Matched set. Higher tier organization; sometimes higher resale. Verify all pieces have matching serials or same production era. Matched sets (matching serials, same production run) sometimes command premium in collector markets. Verify all pieces match. Applies to some vintage tool sets, some machinist sets.
A tool box has 'ORIGINAL PACKAGING' and 'ALL PAPERS' — Snap-On box with catalog.

Complete provenance. Original box + papers add value. Especially valuable for collector-tier tools. Original packaging, receipts, manuals add value especially for collector-tier tools. Snap-On, Starrett, Brown & Sharpe with original documentation command premium. Vintage boxes especially valuable.
A tool set is 'NEW OLD STOCK' — never used but decades old.

NOS (New Old Stock). Unused despite age. Higher tier resale than used equivalent. NOS (New Old Stock) items are unused but old — from unsold inventory or personal storage. Higher tier than used equivalent. Sometimes command near-new price. Verify condition; boxes may show age but tool doesn't.
A vintage power tool has 'BLACK & DECKER' 1950s-1960s vintage industrial design.

Vintage Black & Decker professional tool. Older B&D was pro-tier before consumer focus. Collector interest. Vintage Black & Decker (1950s-1960s) was a professional tool brand before shifting to consumer focus. Distinctive industrial designs. Some collector interest for specific models.
A tool has 'REVERSIBLE THREAD' or 'LEFT-HAND THREAD' feature.

Left-hand thread. Loosens in opposite direction from standard. Used on specific applications (rotating shafts) to prevent self-loosening. Left-hand threads (loosen clockwise, tighten counter-clockwise) used on rotating shafts (bike pedals, some rotor-shaft fasteners) to prevent self-loosening. Match thread direction to application.
A tool set is complete in original case with all pieces confirmed by inventory.

Complete verified set. Premium tier condition. Full-set premium over partial or individual pieces. Verified-complete sets in original storage add value. Full-set premium over sum of individual pieces. Documented inventory (photograph all pieces) supports listing.

A chest drawer has small hand-cut dovetails with irregular spacing.

Pre-1860 or artisan-made piece. Hand-cut dovetails are a quality signal and age marker. Hand-cut dovetails have visible irregularity — pin sizes vary slightly, spacing isn't perfectly uniform. Pre-1860 (before machine dovetail cutters) or contemporary artisan work.
A chest drawer has uniform-spaced machine dovetails all identical size.

Post-1860 factory production. Machine-cut dovetails have exact uniform spacing and size. Machine-cut dovetails (post-Knapp dovetailer, 1860+) have exact uniform spacing. Standard on factory furniture from 1870s onward. Mid-tier quality signal.
A chest drawer has stapled corners with visible metal.

Modern mass-market construction. Lowest tier joinery. Common on flat-pack and budget furniture. Stapled drawer corners are the lowest tier of drawer construction. Common on flat-pack, IKEA-style, budget furniture. Function short-term; failure at joints over time.
A drawer bottom is a single solid wood panel sliding in grooves in the sides.

Traditional quality drawer bottom construction. Solid wood, groove-fit. Pre-1920 typical for quality furniture. Solid-wood drawer bottoms in grooves are traditional quality construction. Allows wood movement without cracking. Standard on quality older furniture.
A drawer bottom is a thin plywood panel.

20th century construction. Plywood bottoms replaced solid wood as plywood became widely available. Standard modern construction. Plywood drawer bottoms are 20th-century standard. Function reliably; don't crack from wood movement. Standard modern quality tier.
A drawer bottom is thin hardboard or MDF material.

Modern mass-market construction. Lower tier than plywood. Common on budget furniture. Hardboard or thin MDF drawer bottoms are modern budget-tier construction. Not as durable as plywood; can sag or crack. Common on flat-pack and budget furniture.
A chest back is horizontal solid wood boards fitted in tongue-and-groove joints.

Quality traditional construction. Pre-1900 typical. Individual boards allow wood movement. Solid-wood tongue-and-groove backs are quality traditional construction. Individual boards accommodate wood movement. Common on quality furniture pre-1900.
A chest back is a single plywood panel.

20th century construction. Standard for factory furniture 1920s+. Adequate quality. Plywood chest backs are 20th century standard. Provide structural stability and dust protection. Standard quality tier for factory furniture.
A chest back is cardboard or thin hardboard.

Budget or flat-pack construction. Very low tier. Little structural contribution. Cardboard or thin hardboard backs are the lowest tier construction. Common on flat-pack, IKEA-style, and cheap furniture. Little structural stability.
A table leg is joined to the apron with mortise-and-tenon.

Traditional quality joinery. Very strong. Common on quality traditional furniture. Mortise-and-tenon leg-to-apron joinery is traditional quality construction. Very strong, long-lasting. Standard on quality traditional tables.
A table leg is bolted to a metal corner brace.

Modern factory construction. Various tiers depending on brace quality and hardware. Standard on many mid-century modern tables. Metal corner braces are common on 20th-century tables. Function well; simpler assembly than mortise-and-tenon. Mid-century modern tables often use this construction.
A table leg is attached with plastic corner bracket and cam-lock fitting.

Flat-pack budget construction. Lowest tier joinery. Common on IKEA-style furniture. Plastic corner brackets with cam-locks are flat-pack construction. Function short-term; joinery loosens over time and disassembly cycles. Budget tier.
A drawer pull is hand-forged iron with irregular shape.

Pre-1830 or restoration piece. Hand-forged hardware has irregular shape. Age marker. Hand-forged wrought iron hardware pre-dates 1830s cast iron production. Distinctive irregular shape. Reproductions exist for restoration work.
A drawer pull is elaborately-shaped cast iron with intricate detail.

Victorian era, 1830-1880. Cast iron hardware with elaborate patterns. Cast iron hardware with elaborate Victorian patterns dates furniture to 1830-1880. Common on Renaissance Revival, Rococo Revival, other Victorian styles.
A drawer pull is uniform pressed brass with typical Golden Oak era design.

1880-1920 Golden Oak era. Pressed brass hardware standardized production. Pressed brass hardware standardized furniture production in 1880-1920. Common on golden oak furniture, Eastlake, and turn-of-century pieces.
A drawer pull is stamped steel with brass plating showing wear.

Mid-20th century mass-market. Brass-plated stamped steel replaced solid brass in budget furniture. Stamped steel with brass plating is common on mid-20th century mass-market furniture. Plating wears through to steel over time. Budget-to-mid tier hardware.
A drawer pull is plastic in wood-tone or brass-color.

Modern budget or flat-pack furniture. Plastic hardware is lowest tier. Plastic drawer hardware is lowest tier — flat-pack, budget, sometimes children's furniture. Function short-term; breaks under stress.
A table finish shows a network of fine cracks — 'alligatored' pattern.

Aged shellac or early lacquer finish. Alligatoring is common. Refinishing possible; sometimes restorable. Alligatored finish is aged and dried shellac or early lacquer. Refinishing removes old finish and applies new. Some collectors prefer original alligatored finish for provenance; others prefer refinished.
A table has visible white ring marks from wet glasses.

Shellac or lacquer finish damaged by moisture. Sometimes restorable with heat or oil. Refinishing often needed. Water-white ring marks are common on shellac and early lacquer finishes. Some are restorable with heat (hair dryer), oil, or careful sanding-and-refinish. Modern polyurethane resists moisture damage.
A table finish is glossy and hard with plastic-like appearance.

Modern polyurethane or catalyzed lacquer. Very durable. Standard modern finish. Polyurethane and catalyzed lacquer finishes are modern durable finishes. Resist moisture, heat, alcohol. Various sheens available. Standard on modern furniture.
A wood surface has grain pattern that continues from top to the edge seamlessly.

Solid wood construction. Grain continuous through edge and face. Solid wood shows continuous grain from face to edge — the edge is a cross-section of the same board. Distinguishes from veneer (where edge shows substrate).
A wood surface has grain that changes abruptly at the edge — showing different pattern underneath.

Veneer over substrate (plywood, MDF, or particleboard). Edge reveals the construction. Veneered surfaces have grain change at edges where the veneer meets the substrate edge. May be edge-banded (thin veneer strip on edge) or exposed substrate. Distinguishes from solid wood.
A chair leg is round in cross-section with a subtle taper.

Traditional turned chair leg. Common on Windsor chairs and traditional country furniture. Turned legs (round cross-section, various profiles) are traditional chair construction. Windsor chairs are classic examples. Country furniture and traditional styles.
A chair leg is square with tapered form.

Federal or Sheraton-style leg. American Federal period (1780-1830) or Sheraton style. Refined tapered form. Square tapered legs are Federal/Sheraton style (1780-1830). Later reproductions and revivals common. Delicate refined proportions.
A chair leg has cabriole (S-curve) shape with ball-and-claw foot.

Chippendale style (1750-1780) or reproduction. Cabriole leg with claw foot is signature Chippendale detail. Cabriole legs with ball-and-claw feet are signature Chippendale (1750-1780). Reproductions common in 20th century. Original Chippendale is museum-tier; reproductions vary.
A chair back has ladder-back slats.

Traditional country or Shaker chair. Ladder-back is 18th-19th century vernacular tradition. Ladder-back chairs are traditional country vernacular design. Various regional variations. Shaker ladder-backs are collector tier. Modern reproductions common.
A chair back has continuous curved horizontal bar (Windsor bow-back).

Windsor chair, bow-back style. Traditional 18th-19th century American. Various regional variants. Windsor chairs have distinctive construction: solid seat with legs, spindles, and back socketed into seat. Bow-back is one style; others include comb-back, fan-back, sack-back. American colonial through 19th-century tradition.
A dining table has one big top with pedestal base and no leaf extensions.

Pedestal table. Traditional design; various styles from Colonial through Victorian and modern. Pedestal tables have a single central support. Traditional design across centuries. Various tops (round, oval, rectangular). Some pedestal tables have leaves that pull open to extend.
A dining table has extension leaves stored underneath in specific slots.

Extension table with leaves. Common Victorian through mid-20th century construction. Extends for larger meals. Extension tables have leaves that pull out to extend the table. Table opens (either splits in middle or slides apart), leaves insert, table closes. Various mechanisms. Common quality construction.
A dresser has a mirror attached to a decorative frame at the top.

Traditional dresser with mirror. Various styles from Victorian through mid-20th century. Dressers with attached mirrors are common bedroom furniture. Various styles — Victorian, Golden Oak, Art Deco, mid-century modern. Mirror condition and frame style help date.
A large armoire is 7+ feet tall, closed on all sides, doors in front.

Armoire or wardrobe. Pre-built-in-closet era standard. Traditional furniture; European tradition especially strong. Armoires and wardrobes were standard bedroom furniture before built-in closets became universal (mid-20th century). European tradition especially strong. Various sizes and styles.
A bookcase has adjustable shelves on metal pins in vertical rows of holes.

Modern construction with pin-adjustable shelves. Convenient adjustment; standard modern feature. Adjustable shelves on metal pins (in vertical rows of holes) are modern standard construction. Very convenient. Older bookcases had fixed shelves or wooden pin adjustment.
A bookcase has fixed shelves at set heights.

Traditional or budget construction. Fixed shelves are older tradition or lowest-tier modern. Fixed shelves are traditional (pre-adjustment hardware) or budget-tier modern. Function fine for the specific configured heights but no flexibility. Common on older pieces and budget flat-pack.
A large piece has a paper label pasted inside a drawer or on the back.

Maker's label. Verify maker; adds provenance. Some maker labels significantly increase value. Paper labels are common maker identification on furniture. Well-preserved labels add value. Some maker labels (Stickley, other significant makers) can dramatically increase value. Verify authenticity of label.
A large piece has a branded maker mark burnt into wood on the underside.

Branded maker mark. More permanent than paper label. Adds provenance. Branded marks are burnt into wood, common on quality furniture. More permanent than paper labels. Reference maker mark databases for identification. Well-known makers add significant value.
A large piece has a metal maker's plate riveted to the frame.

Metal plate maker identification. Standard on some maker traditions. Adds provenance. Metal maker's plates are common on some maker traditions. Riveted or screwed to frame. Reference for identification. Higher-tier construction typically.
A chair seat has visible horsehair and cotton batting when the fabric is torn.

Pre-1950s upholstery. Horsehair-and-cotton was standard traditional upholstery. Restoration valuable. Horsehair-and-cotton upholstery is pre-1950s standard. Foam replaced it in mid-20th century. A chair seat has a solid foam block visible when fabric is torn.

Post-1950s upholstery. Foam-cushioned. Standard modern construction. Foam degrades over 15-20 years. Foam upholstery replaced traditional horsehair-and-cotton starting in the 1950s. Standard modern construction. Foam degrades (crumbles, loses resilience) over 15-20 years and needs replacement.
A sofa cushion is a fabric envelope filled with loose down.

Premium upholstery. Down-filled cushions require periodic fluffing but very comfortable and long-lasting. Down-filled cushions are premium upholstery. A wooden chair has visible tool marks on the underside — small planing marks and scribed lines.

Tool marks on undersides (planing marks, scribed layout lines, saw kerfs) indicate handmade or older construction. Machine-planed surfaces are 20th-century typical. A drawer front has a raised panel with beveled edges surrounded by frame.

A cabinet door is a single flat panel.

Slab door. Modern construction. Various tiers depending on material (veneered plywood, MDF, solid wood slab). Slab doors are modern minimalist construction. Various tiers by material: veneered MDF (mid-market), solid wood slab (higher tier, but risk of movement), veneered plywood (adequate).

A finish is oiled — feels slightly dry to touch, no plastic sheen.

A large chair has 'CARL HANSEN & SØN' or Wegner-CH-series mark.

A chair has splint seat — woven wood strips.

A piece has 'GOLDEN OAK' finish — orange-yellow oak with pressed brass hardware.

A piece has 'MID-CENTURY MODERN' teak or walnut with tapered legs and clean lines.

A piece is 'ITALIAN MODERN' with lacquered surfaces and bold form.

A chair has 'SCANDINAVIAN' aesthetic with birch or beech and simple form.

A cabinet has 'DUNCAN PHYFE' style with lyre-back chairs and reeded legs.

A piece has 'TRIFID FOOT' — three-toed carved foot.

Trifid foot — Queen Anne period, 1720-1750. Distinctive American Colonial detail. Trifid feet are three-toed carved feet. Queen Anne period detail (1720-1750). Distinctive American Colonial furniture element.
A piece has 'DISH-TOP' round tea table with dished top.

Traditional tea table form. Various period styles. Dished top designed to hold tea service securely. Dish-top tea tables are traditional. Various period styles (Queen Anne, Chippendale, American Colonial). Dished top is functional and decorative element.
A piece has 'TILT-TOP' table with top that tilts vertical for storage.

Tilt-top tea table. Traditional space-saving design. Various period styles. Tilt-top tables have tops that tilt vertical when not in use — space-saving traditional design. Various period styles. Mechanism at central pedestal.
A piece has 'CANDLE STAND' — small round-top table on tripod base.

Traditional candle stand. Small side table originally for holding candles. Various period styles. Candle stands are small round-top tables on tripod bases, originally for holding candles beside seating. Various period styles. Traditional form still made today.
A piece has 'CANDLE BOX' — small wall-hung box with sloped lid.

Candle box. Traditional wall-hung storage for candles. Country furniture form. Candle boxes are traditional wall-hung storage for candles. Small, sloped-lid design. Country furniture tradition. Various regional styles.
A cabinet has 'CORNER CABINET' triangular form fitting into room corner.

Corner cabinet. Traditional space-efficient form. Various period styles. Corner cabinets fit into room corners for space efficiency. Traditional form. Various period styles. Some have curved-front doors; others are angled flat.
A piece has 'PIE SAFE' with punched-tin panels.

Pie safe. Traditional American food storage furniture with ventilated panels. Country tradition. Pie safes are traditional American food storage furniture. Punched-tin panels provide ventilation while keeping pests out. Country furniture tradition. Various regional styles.
A piece has 'DOWRY CHEST' with painted folk art decoration.

Traditional European or American dowry chest (marriage chest, hope chest). Folk art painted decoration. Regional traditions. Dowry chests (marriage chests, hope chests) are traditional storage furniture for dowry items. Painted folk art decoration common. Various regional traditions (Pennsylvania Dutch, Scandinavian, other European).
A cabinet has 'JACOBEAN' style with heavy dark oak and turned legs.

Jacobean or Jacobean-Revival style. Heavy dark oak, turned legs, sometimes carved details. 1600s original or later revival. Jacobean furniture (1600s English original) is heavy dark oak with turned legs. Extensively revived in Colonial Revival era (1880-1930). Original vs revival dating requires detailed inspection.
A cabinet has 'WILLIAM AND MARY' style with turned legs and painted finish.

William and Mary style, 1690-1720. Turned legs, sometimes painted finish. American Colonial period. William and Mary furniture (1690-1720 original) has distinctive turned legs, painted finishes, some Dutch influences. American Colonial period. Original pieces very rare and valuable.
A cabinet has 'QUEEN ANNE' style with cabriole legs and pad feet.

Queen Anne style, 1720-1755. Cabriole legs with pad feet (round flat feet). Elegant American Colonial period. Queen Anne furniture (1720-1755 original) has cabriole legs with pad feet, elegant proportions, walnut and mahogany common. American Colonial period. Original pieces museum-tier.
A piece has 'HEPPLEWHITE' style with shield-back chairs and inlaid decoration.

Hepplewhite style, 1780-1810. Shield-back chairs, inlaid decoration. Federal period American. Hepplewhite (English designer, published 1788) style is Federal period American 1780-1810. Shield-back chairs, inlaid decoration, delicate proportions. Contemporary of Sheraton.
A piece has 'SHERATON' style with rectilinear inlaid patterns.

Sheraton style, 1780-1810. Rectilinear forms with inlaid patterns. Federal period American. Sheraton (English designer) style is Federal period American 1780-1810. Rectilinear forms, inlaid patterns, delicate proportions. Contemporary of Hepplewhite.
A piece has 'ROCOCO REVIVAL' style with C-scrolls and cabriole legs.

Rococo Revival, 1840-1860. Elaborate C-scrolls, cabriole legs, marble tops. Victorian-era revival of French Rococo. Rococo Revival (1840-1860) is Victorian-era revival of 18th-century French Rococo. Elaborate carved C-scrolls, cabriole legs, marble tops. Common Victorian parlor furniture.
A piece has 'RENAISSANCE REVIVAL' style with heavy architectural forms.

Renaissance Revival, 1860-1875. Heavy architectural forms, applied decoration, sometimes gilded. Victorian era. Renaissance Revival (1860-1875) has heavy architectural forms, applied carved decoration, sometimes gilded elements. Victorian era. Distinct from Rococo Revival.
A piece has 'AESTHETIC MOVEMENT' style with Japanese-influenced decoration.

Aesthetic Movement, 1870-1890. Japanese-influenced decoration, ebonized finishes, refined proportions. Victorian era. Aesthetic Movement (1870-1890) integrated Japanese aesthetic influences into Western furniture. Ebonized (blackened) finishes, refined decoration, distinct from earlier Victorian revivals.
A piece has 'ART NOUVEAU' style with flowing organic forms.

Art Nouveau, 1890-1910. Flowing organic forms, curvilinear shapes. Various regional variations (French, Belgian, German Jugendstil, Vienna Secession). Art Nouveau furniture (1890-1910) has distinctive flowing organic forms. Regional variations: French Art Nouveau, Belgian, German Jugendstil, Vienna Secession. Various major designers.
A cabinet has 'MACKINTOSH' or Charles Rennie Mackintosh style.

Charles Rennie Mackintosh — Scottish designer, Art Nouveau/Vienna Secession influence. Distinctive tall vertical forms. Charles Rennie Mackintosh (Scottish, 1868-1928) designed distinctive furniture. Tall vertical forms, ladder-backs, Glasgow style. Very high tier when authentic; extensive reproductions.
A cabinet has 'GEORG JENSEN' silverware — extremely detailed Danish silverware.

Danish silverware, not furniture but often accompanies Danish modern furniture. Georg Jensen tradition. Georg Jensen silverware is not furniture but commonly displayed with Danish modern furniture. High-tier collector market. Reference for authentication.
A large piece has 'GEORGE NELSON' Marshmallow sofa or Coconut chair signature form.

George Nelson designs for Herman Miller. Very collectible mid-century modern. Extensive reproductions. George Nelson (American, 1908-1986) designed iconic mid-century modern furniture for Herman Miller. Marshmallow sofa, Coconut chair, Bench, clocks. Very collectible. Verify authenticity vs reproduction.
A piece has 'ISAMU NOGUCHI' coffee table or lamp design.

Isamu Noguchi — Japanese-American sculptor and designer. Iconic coffee table and lighting designs. Very collectible. Isamu Noguchi (Japanese-American, 1904-1988) designed iconic mid-century modern coffee tables (biomorphic glass-top table) and Akari lighting. Very collectible; verify authenticity.
A piece has 'HARRY BERTOIA' wire-mesh chair or characteristic form.

Harry Bertoia — Italian-American designer for Knoll. Diamond chair, Bird chair iconic. Very collectible. Harry Bertoia (Italian-American, 1915-1978) designed iconic wire-mesh chairs for Knoll. Diamond chair, Bird chair. Very collectible mid-century modern. Verify authenticity.
A piece has 'FLORENCE KNOLL' credenza or characteristic mid-century form.

Florence Knoll — American designer and business partner (co-founder of Knoll). Credenzas and various pieces very collectible. Florence Knoll (American, 1917-2019) designed and led Knoll. Credenzas, tables, and various pieces. Distinctive rational American modernism. Very collectible.
A cabinet has 'PAUL EVANS' brutalist sculpted-metal form.

Paul Evans — American brutalist furniture designer. Very high tier collector market. Sculpted metal and wood. Paul Evans (American, 1931-1987) made distinctive brutalist furniture with sculpted metal and wood. Very high tier collector market. Various series (Sculpted, PE, Cityscape).
A piece has 'MILO BAUGHMAN' mid-century American form.

Milo Baughman — American mid-century modern designer for Thayer Coggin and others. Collectible. Milo Baughman (American, 1923-2003) designed mid-century modern furniture for Thayer Coggin and others. Various collector-tier pieces. Distinctive American modernism.
A piece has 'ADRIAN PEARSALL' craft-associate signature.

Adrian Pearsall — American mid-century modernist. Craft Associates. Collectible. Adrian Pearsall (American, 1925-2011) designed for Craft Associates and other makers. Mid-century modernist. Various pieces collectible; sculptural forms distinctive.
A piece has 'DUNBAR' branded mark or Edward Wormley design.

Dunbar Furniture — American mid-century modernist producer. Edward Wormley designs prominent. High tier collector market. Dunbar Furniture (American, 1919-1990s) produced high-tier mid-century modernist furniture. Edward Wormley designs especially prominent. High tier collector market. Verify authenticity.
A piece has 'HANS WEGNER' iconic chair form (Wishbone, Papa Bear, Round Chair).

Hans Wegner — Danish designer. Iconic chair forms. Very high tier when authentic. Hans Wegner (Danish, 1914-2007) designed iconic Danish modern chairs. Wishbone (CH24), Round Chair, Papa Bear. Various makers (Carl Hansen, Getama, Johannes Hansen). Very high tier collector market.
A piece has 'FINN JUHL' or characteristic sculptural Danish form.

Finn Juhl — Danish modernist designer. Very high tier collector market. Distinctive sculptural forms. Finn Juhl (Danish, 1912-1989) designed distinctive Danish modernist furniture with sculptural forms. Various makers (Baker, Bovirke, others). Very high tier collector market.
A piece has 'BØRGE MOGENSEN' branded design.

Børge Mogensen — Danish modernist designer. Various collectible pieces. Traditional-influenced modernism. Børge Mogensen (Danish, 1914-1972) designed Danish modernist furniture with traditional influences. Various major pieces; collectible. FDB Møbler, Fredericia, other makers.
A piece has 'PIET MONDRIAN' influence — grid design in specific primary colors.

De Stijl / Mondrian-inspired furniture. Rare original; more common as later reproduction or inspired-by. De Stijl (Dutch movement, 1917+) had specific furniture designs. Gerrit Rietveld's Red-Blue Chair is iconic. Original pieces museum-tier; reproductions common.
A chair has 'GERRIT RIETVELD' Red-Blue Chair characteristic form.

Gerrit Rietveld — De Stijl designer. Red-Blue Chair iconic. Rare authentic pieces; various later production. Gerrit Rietveld (Dutch, 1888-1964) designed iconic De Stijl furniture including Red-Blue Chair. Original vintage authentic pieces very rare; various later production (some by Cassina under license).
A piece has 'LE CORBUSIER' LC series — LC1, LC2, LC3, LC4 chaise, etc.

Le Corbusier designs. Chaise longue LC4 iconic. Various makers (Cassina, various reproductions). Authentication essential. Le Corbusier (Swiss-French, 1887-1965) with Pierre Jeanneret and Charlotte Perriand designed LC-series furniture (1925-1932). Cassina holds current license. Various vintage and reproduction production. Authentication essential.
A piece has 'MIES VAN DER ROHE' Barcelona chair or MR chair form.

Mies van der Rohe — Bauhaus/modernist. Barcelona chair iconic. Knoll holds license; various reproductions exist. Mies van der Rohe (German-American, 1886-1969) designed iconic modernist furniture. Barcelona chair (1929), MR chair. Knoll holds official license. Reproductions common; authentication essential.

Dissociation presents with slack-body-slack-mind signals: eyes unfocused, hands limp, breathing shallow or irregular, no response to name on first call. Two shapes: (a) budget-constrained — offer a smaller version or payment terms, not a discount (b) testing your discipline — hold firm on price. Tell them apart: Budget-constrained customers describe their constraint concretely ('I'm saving for X and can spend Y') and respond well to alternative structures (smaller purchase, payment plan, subscription-lite tier). Two shapes: (a) they're growing and ready — give them the stretch, scaffold it (b) they're bored/underpaid and building leverage for a departure — address the underlying issue. Tell them apart: Ready-for-growth requests reference specific projects or skills, propose a version that fits current workload, and are framed as 'I'd like to try X.' looks like: a vendor sends a very generous holiday gift. how do i tell which?

Two shapes: (a) genuine appreciation for a long relationship — accept graciously with a note (b) leverage for an upcoming renegotiation — accept with reservation, don't let the gift do work. Tell them apart: Genuine-appreciation gifts arrive at holidays every year regardless of contract state, are proportional to long-term value, and come with a personal note. Leverage gifts arrive before renewal negotiations, are much larger than prior gifts from the same vendor, and come with a note that references future work. Two shapes: (a) someone who genuinely knows something and cares — listen, evaluate, thank them (b) someone projecting their own concerns onto your situation — thank them, filter out the advice. Tell them apart: Genuine-knowledge advice is specific ('I noticed you're using X pricing model; here's a specific reason it might underperform for your customer profile'), relevant to your actual situation, and comes from someone with adjacent experience. looks like: a customer sends a long negative review. how do i tell which?

Two shapes: (a) legitimate feedback dressed as complaint — respond publicly, address the specifics, fix what's fixable (b) review-bombing for extortion — respond publicly with facts, do not negotiate for removal. Tell them apart: Legitimate-feedback reviews describe specific experiences, propose or imply what would have made the experience better, and don't threaten. Extortion reviews demand compensation for removal, escalate quickly if not accommodated, and often follow a first-contact where the customer named a specific amount they wanted. looks like: a longtime employee suddenly starts making mistakes. how do i tell which?

Two shapes: (a) personal crisis or health issue — offer flexible support and check in privately (b) checked-out and looking to leave — have the honest conversation about fit and future. Tell them apart: Crisis-mistake patterns are recent (last few weeks or months), the employee's engagement outside the mistakes is unchanged or heightened (they still care about work generally), and there may be visible tells (fatigue, distraction, tension).
